text:
A commercial contract between Nakamura Consulting Inc. and Petrov Industries Ltd. contains a clause stating: 'Any claim arising from this agreement must be commenced within six months of the event giving rise to the claim, failing which all claims are forever barred.' Two years after a breach occurs, Nakamura wishes to sue Petrov but is confronted with this contractual limitation clause.

Under the Limitations Act, 2002, is a contractual clause shortening the limitation period to six months enforceable between commercial parties?
opts: Yes — parties to a commercial contract may freely agree to any limitation period shorter or longer than the statutory period; such agreements are always enforceable.

Yes — under s.22 of the Limitations Act, 2002, parties to a business agreement may agree to vary or exclude the limitation periods in the Act; a six-month contractual limitation period between commercial parties is therefore enforceable.
No — the two-year limitation period in s.4 of the Limitations Act, 2002 is mandatory and cannot be shortened by contract; any clause purporting to shorten it is void.
No — contractual limitation periods require court approval to be enforceable; the parties cannot unilaterally agree to alter the statutory limitation period.
expl: Under s.22 of the Limitations Act, 2002 (S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B), parties to a business agreement — defined as an agreement made in the course of a business between persons each of whom is acting in a business capacity — may agree to vary or exclude the limitation periods provided for by the Act. Commercial parties dealing at arm's length in a business context may therefore validly agree to a shorter limitation period (such as six months) for claims arising from their agreement. The result is that Nakamura's claim may be barred by the contractual limitation clause, even though the statutory period has not expired. Consumer agreements are treated differently — the Act restricts the ability to limit consumer rights.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s.22
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Limitation Periods | Agreement to Vary Limitation Periods

text:
Yolanda is sued by a plaintiff in July 2024 for negligence. Yolanda believes that a third party, Conrad, contributed to the plaintiff's loss and wishes to claim contribution and indemnity from Conrad by issuing a third-party claim. The plaintiff's accident occurred in June 2022.

Under s. 18 of the Limitations Act, 2002, when does the limitation period for Yolanda's contribution and indemnity claim against Conrad begin to run?
opts: June 2022, when the accident giving rise to the plaintiff's loss occurred
The date Yolanda first knew or ought to have known that she had a claim against Conrad for contribution or indemnity — which typically arises when a claim is made against Yolanda in the main action
June 2024, two years after the accident, which is the earliest that a contribution claim can be made
There is no limitation period for contribution and indemnity claims because they are derivative of the main action
expl: Under s. 18 of the Limitations Act, 2002, a claim for contribution and indemnity is discovered on the day on which the person making the claim first knew or ought to have known that they had the right to make the claim. For contribution claims, this typically means the day on which a claim is made against the party seeking contribution — because it is only at that point that the party knows it may have suffered a loss giving rise to a right of contribution. Yolanda's two-year limitation period for her contribution claim against Conrad therefore began to run when she was served with the plaintiff's claim in July 2024, not on the date of the accident in June 2022. She has until approximately July 2026 to issue the third-party claim.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 18
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Limitation Periods | Limitation Period for Contribution and Indemnity Claims

text:
Yusuf lent $40,000 to his friend Andrei under a written loan agreement in 2015. The agreement stated that the loan was repayable 'on demand.' Yusuf never made a demand for repayment. In March 2025, Yusuf makes a demand and then commences an action one month later when Andrei refuses to pay.

Under the Limitations Act, 2002, when does the two-year limitation period begin to run for a demand obligation, and is Yusuf's claim statute-barred?
opts: The two-year period began in 2015 when the loan was made; Yusuf's 2025 action is statute-barred.
The two-year period begins when Yusuf makes a demand; since Yusuf made his demand in March 2025 and commenced within two years of that demand, his claim is not statute-barred.
There is no limitation period for demand obligations; Yusuf may sue at any time as long as the debt has not been paid.
The two-year period begins when Yusuf first learned Andrei was unable to pay; since Yusuf did not know Andrei was financially troubled before 2025, his claim is not statute-barred.
expl: Under s. 5(1) of the Limitations Act, 2002 (as interpreted by the courts), for a demand obligation — a debt payable on demand — the limitation period runs from the date on which the demand is made, because the cause of action does not arise until demand is made and refused (or until a reasonable time for compliance has passed). However, s. 5(1) and the surrounding case law also impose an objective standard: the obligation is deemed to have been discovered no later than it would have been discovered with reasonable diligence. For a demand obligation, discovery occurs when demand is made. Since Yusuf made his demand in March 2025 and commenced his action within two months, his claim is not statute-barred — provided the ultimate 15-year limitation period under s. 15 does not also apply (which it would not on these facts, as the loan was in 2015 and 15 years have not yet passed).
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 5
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Limitation Periods | Limitation Period for Demand Obligations

text:
Rosamund experienced severe psychological trauma in 2019 as a result of an assault. Her psychological condition rendered her unable to appreciate the nature of her legal rights or manage her legal affairs from the date of the assault through June 2023, when she was treated and regained capacity. She commences an action against her assailant in May 2025.

Under s.7 of the Limitations Act, 2002, how does Rosamund's psychological incapacity affect the running of the two-year limitation period, and is her claim timely?
opts: Psychological incapacity has no effect on the running of a limitation period; the two-year period ran from 2019 and Rosamund's claim is statute-barred.

Under s.7, the limitation period is suspended during any period in which the claimant is a person with incapacity who is not represented by a litigation guardian; Rosamund's period was suspended from 2019 to June 2023, began running in June 2023, and expires in June 2025 — making her May 2025 claim likely statute-barred.

Under s.7, the limitation period is suspended during a period of incapacity, so Rosamund's two-year period would not begin until June 2023; commencing in May 2025 is within two years of regaining capacity.
Rosamund's assault claim has no limitation period because it arises from a criminal act; all claims for damages from criminal assault are exempt from the Limitations Act, 2002.
expl: Under s.7(1) of the Limitations Act, 2002, the limitation period established by s.4 does not run during any period in which the person with the claim is incapable of commencing a proceeding because of psychological or physical incapacity and is not represented by a litigation guardian. 'Incapacity' for this purpose means the person is not reasonably able to understand the nature of the claim or to determine whether it is appropriate to commence a proceeding. During the period from 2019 to June 2023, Rosamund was incapable of appreciating her legal rights due to her psychological condition. The two-year period was suspended for that entire period and began running in June 2023 when she regained capacity. Her May 2025 action is within two years of June 2023 and is timely.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s.7
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Limitation Periods | Suspension of Limitation Period — Persons with Psychological Incapacity

text:
Mirela is injured in a slip-and-fall accident on April 15, 2020, when she is 14 years old. Her claim is immediately obvious: the occupier left ice on a public walkway. On September 1, 2021 — while Mirela is still 15 — her aunt is appointed as her litigation guardian by court order. The litigation guardian does not commence an action. Mirela turns 18 on April 15, 2024. Her lawyer must now determine when the two-year limitation period began to run and when it expires.

Under ss. 6(1) and 6(2) of the Limitations Act, 2002, when did the two-year limitation period begin to run for Mirela's claim, and when does it expire?
opts: The limitation period began running on April 15, 2020, the date of the accident; Mirela's minority does not affect the limitation period because the claim was immediately discoverable.

The limitation period began running on April 15, 2024 — Mirela's 18th birthday — and expires on April 15, 2026, because the limitation period is suspended during minority and runs from the later of the litigation guardian appointment and the 18th birthday.

The limitation period began running on September 1, 2021 — the date the litigation guardian was appointed — and expires on September 1, 2023, because s. 6(2) provides that a minor's claim is discovered on the earlier of the litigation guardian appointment date or the 18th birthday.
The limitation period is suspended indefinitely during minority and does not begin to run until Mirela affirmatively elects to proceed; no fixed expiry date exists.
expl: Under s. 6(1) of the Limitations Act, 2002, the basic two-year limitation period does not run while the claimant is a minor. Under s. 6(2), for purposes of s. 4 (the basic two-year period), a minor's claim is discovered on the earlier of: (a) the day the person ceases to be a minor (turns 18); or (b) the day a litigation guardian is appointed for the minor. Here, the litigation guardian was appointed on September 1, 2021 — before Mirela's 18th birthday. Under s. 6(2), discovery is triggered on the earlier date, which is the litigation guardian's appointment on September 1, 2021. The two-year period therefore ran from September 1, 2021, and expired on September 1, 2023 — more than six months before Mirela turned 18. The action is now out of time.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, ss. 6(1), 6(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Limitation Periods | Suspension of Limitation Period — Minors and Persons Under Disability

text:
Szymanski was sexually assaulted by her former employer when she was 15 years old. The assault occurred in 2000. Szymanski is now 38 and wishes to bring a civil claim for damages from the sexual assault. The defendant argues that the 15-year ultimate limitation period under s.15 of the Limitations Act, 2002 expired in 2015, barring her claim absolutely.

Under s.15(4) of the Limitations Act, 2002, does the 15-year ultimate limitation period apply to a civil claim for damages arising from sexual assault?
opts: Yes — the 15-year ultimate limitation period is absolute and applies to all civil claims in Ontario without exception.
No — under s.15(4) of the Limitations Act, 2002, the 15-year ultimate limitation period does not apply to proceedings based on claims for sexual assault; there is no ultimate period for such claims.
Yes — but the 15-year period is suspended while the claimant was a minor, so it began running on Szymanski's 18th birthday in 2003 and expires in 2018; her claim is therefore barred.
No — the 15-year ultimate period does not apply to any personal injury claim, including Szymanski's.
expl: Under s.15(4) of the Limitations Act, 2002 (S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B), the 15-year ultimate limitation period in s.15(1) does not apply to a claim for sexual assault. The Legislature recognizes that survivors of sexual assault often require many years before they are psychologically able to bring a civil claim, and that an absolute ultimate limitation period would be unjust in such circumstances. Accordingly, s.15(4) creates an express exception to the ultimate limitation period for sexual assault claims. Szymanski's claim is not barred by the 15-year ultimate period. Under s.16(1)(h) of the Limitations Act, 2002, no limitation period applies to a claim arising from sexual assault — neither the basic two-year period under s.4 nor the ultimate 15-year period under s.15 applies to Szymanski's claim. Her civil claim for damages from the sexual assault is not subject to any limitation period.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s.15(4)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Limitation Periods | Ultimate Limitation Period — Exceptions Under s.15(4)

text:
A client believes her employer has wrongfully denied her contractual right to a share of a business sale. The dispute depends entirely on the proper legal interpretation of a written shareholders' agreement — there are no material facts in dispute. Counsel is deciding whether to proceed by way of action or application.

Under r.14.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, which originating process is most appropriate where the relief sought requires the determination of a question of law or interpretation with no genuine factual dispute?
opts: An action commenced by statement of claim is always the appropriate originating process; applications are only available for certain statutory proceedings.

An application under r.14.02 is the most appropriate process where the issues involve questions of law or interpretation on an agreed or undisputed factual record, without the need for trial or oral evidence.
A motion for summary judgment immediately on an action is the preferred approach where there are no material facts in dispute.
An application can only be used where all parties consent to the process; unilateral election of an application over an action is not permitted.
expl: Under r.14.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a proceeding may be commenced by notice of application where it is authorized by statute or the Rules or where the relief claimed is a determination of rights under a document or statute, particularly where there are no material facts in dispute and the proceeding can be resolved on affidavit evidence without a trial. An application is heard by a judge on affidavit evidence and proceeds more quickly than an action through pleadings, discovery, and trial. Where the sole issue is the interpretation of a written contract (shareholders' agreement) on undisputed facts, the application is the more efficient and appropriate process.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.14.02
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Pleadings | Application vs. Action — When to Use Each

text:
Three defendants are named in a personal injury action. Defendant A — a property owner — believes that Defendant B — a snow removal contractor — is entirely responsible for the icy walkway that caused the plaintiff's injury, and wishes to claim contribution and indemnity from Defendant B within the existing action.

Under r.28.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, how may Defendant A assert its claim for contribution and indemnity against Defendant B within the main action?
opts: Defendant A must commence a separate action against Defendant B; contribution and indemnity claims between co-defendants cannot be raised within the main action.
Defendant A may file a crossclaim against Defendant B within the main action under r.28.01, claiming contribution and indemnity or asserting that Defendant B alone is liable for the plaintiff's loss.
Defendant A may only claim contribution and indemnity from Defendant B by making a written demand to Defendant B's insurer; the claim cannot be made through any pleading in the main action.
Defendant A must wait until the plaintiff's main action is resolved before commencing a claim against Defendant B; cross-defendant claims cannot be brought while the main action is pending.
expl: Under r.28.01(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a defendant may crossclaim against any co-defendant — that is, another defendant who is already a party to the main action — by asserting a claim for contribution and indemnity or by claiming that the co-defendant alone is liable for the plaintiff's loss or a part of it. A crossclaim is a pleading filed within the main action; it enables all related claims among co-defendants to be resolved in a single proceeding without the need for a separate action. Defendant A should therefore file a crossclaim against Defendant B under r.28.01.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.28.01
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Pleadings | Crossclaims Between Co-Defendants — Rule 28

text:
A statement of claim for breach of contract is served on the defendant, Beaumont Logistics Inc., in Ontario on March 1. Beaumont's counsel asks how much time the company has to deliver its statement of defence.

Under r.18.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, how many days does a defendant ordinarily have to deliver a statement of defence after being served with the statement of claim in Ontario?
opts: 30 days after service of the statement of claim.
20 days after service of the statement of claim.
14 days after service of the statement of claim.
60 days after service of the statement of claim.
expl: Under r.18.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a defendant who is served with a statement of claim in Ontario must deliver a statement of defence within 20 days after service. If the defendant is served outside Ontario but within Canada or the United States, the time is 40 days; if served elsewhere, the time is 60 days. A defendant who does not deliver a statement of defence within the prescribed time is noted in default, and the plaintiff may proceed to obtain default judgment under Part 19 of the Rules.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.18.01
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Pleadings | Time to Deliver Statement of Defence and Default

text:
A defendant corporation was personally served with a statement of claim in Toronto on Monday, May 4, 2026. The defendant's counsel asks how many days the corporation has to deliver a statement of defence.

Under r.18.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, how many days does a defendant served in Ontario have to deliver a statement of defence?
opts: 10 days after service of the statement of claim.
20 days after service of the statement of claim.
30 days after service of the statement of claim.
60 days after service of the statement of claim.
expl: Under r.18.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a defendant who has been served with a statement of claim in Ontario must deliver a statement of defence within 20 days after service. Service occurred on May 4, 2026, so the defendant has until May 24, 2026 to deliver the statement of defence. If the defendant fails to deliver a statement of defence within the prescribed time, the plaintiff may note the defendant in default under r.19.01.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.18.01
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Pleadings | Statement of Defence — Time to Deliver

text:
The defendant in a commercial lease dispute delivers a statement of defence that, in addition to denying the plaintiff's breach-of-contract allegations, contains three paragraphs accusing the plaintiff's principal of tax fraud and criminal conduct — allegations that have no connection to any issue in the lease dispute. The defendant has not commenced any separate proceeding arising from those allegations. Pleadings are not yet closed. The plaintiff brings a motion targeting only those three paragraphs.

Which rule and remedy most precisely addresses the plaintiff's motion to remove the three objectionable paragraphs from the defendant's statement of defence?
opts: Rule 21.01(1)(b) — the court strikes the entire statement of defence because it discloses no reasonable defence to the plaintiff's claim.

Rule 25.11 — the court may strike out all or part of a pleading that is scandalous, frivolous, or vexatious, or that may prejudice or delay the fair trial of the action, targeting only the offending paragraphs without disturbing the rest of the defence.
Rule 20.04 — the plaintiff should bring a motion for summary judgment immediately, since the scandalous allegations show the defendant has no genuine defence.
Rule 26.01 — the court should order the defendant to amend the statement of defence within 30 days; striking is not available until the amendment deadline passes.
expl: Under r.25.11 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court may strike out or expunge all or part of a pleading — with or without leave to amend — on the grounds that it is scandalous, frivolous, or vexatious, or that it may prejudice or delay the fair trial of the action, or that it is otherwise an abuse of process. Rule 25.11 is the precise tool for removing objectionable content from within an otherwise valid pleading: it targets specific portions without requiring the entire document to be struck. Here, the three paragraphs alleging unrelated criminal conduct are irrelevant to the lease dispute, prejudicial to the plaintiff, and fall squarely within r.25.11. The plaintiff's motion correctly targets r.25.11 and seeks to excise only the offending paragraphs, leaving the balance of the defence intact.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.25.11
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Pleadings | Striking Out Pleadings — Scandalous, Frivolous, or Vexatious

text:
Bellwood Construction Ltd. is a defendant in a negligence action brought by a homeowner for defective roofing work. Bellwood believes its subcontractor, Peak Roofing Inc., performed the defective work and is responsible for any damages. Bellwood wishes to claim contribution and indemnity from Peak Roofing as part of the main action.

Under r.29.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, when may a defendant commence a third-party claim, and must Bellwood obtain leave to do so?
opts: A third-party claim may only be commenced with leave of the court in all circumstances; Bellwood must bring a motion for leave before issuing any third-party claim.

Under r.29.01, a defendant may issue a third-party claim without leave of the court if it is issued within the time for delivery of the statement of defence or before the close of pleadings; leave is required only if those deadlines have passed.
A third-party claim can only be issued if the main plaintiff consents to the addition of a third party to the action.
A defendant may never issue a third-party claim in a negligence action; contribution and indemnity must be claimed in a separate proceeding commenced after the main action is resolved.
expl: Under r.29.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a defendant who claims contribution and indemnity from a person not already a party to the main action may issue a third-party claim. If the third-party claim is issued before the defendant's statement of defence or within the time for delivery of the statement of defence, no leave is required. If the defendant wishes to issue the third-party claim after the close of pleadings or at a later stage, leave of the court is required under r.29.01(2). The third-party claim is a separate pleading issued as part of the main action, enabling all related contribution and indemnity issues to be resolved in a single proceeding.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.29.01
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Pleadings | Third-Party Claims — Contribution and Indemnity (Rule 29)

text:
A plaintiff's process server has been unable to personally serve Northgate Dynamics Inc., an Ontario corporation, at its registered office. The office was closed each time the process server attended. The plaintiff's lawyer considers using an alternative to personal service on the corporation without obtaining a court order.

Under r.16.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what alternative to personal service is available for service on a corporation without a court order?
opts: Sending the originating process by email to the corporation's general inquiry email address.
Serving the originating process on any employee of the corporation found anywhere in Ontario.
Sending a copy of the originating process by registered mail to the registered office of the corporation, which constitutes an alternative to personal service under r.16.03.
Filing the originating process with the registrar and requesting the registrar to notify the corporation by ordinary mail.
expl: Under r.16.03(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), as an alternative to personal service, a document may be served on a corporation incorporated or registered under a statute of Ontario by sending a copy of the document by registered mail to the registered office of the corporation as shown in the records of the Ministry of Government and Consumer Services. Service is effective on the day the registered mail is delivered, or if not delivered, on the fifth day after mailing. This alternative does not require a court order and is specifically designed to address the difficulty of personally serving corporations at their registered office.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.16.03(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Service of Process | Alternative Service on Corporations — Registered Mail Under r.16.03

text:
A plaintiff incorporated in Ontario sues a British Columbia corporation for breach of a contract that was negotiated and to be performed entirely in Ontario. The plaintiff wishes to serve the statement of claim on the defendant at its registered office in Vancouver without first obtaining leave of the court.

Under r.17.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, may a plaintiff serve an originating process outside Ontario without first obtaining leave of the court, and if so, in what circumstances?
opts: No — service outside Ontario always requires prior leave of the court; a motion under r.17.03 must be brought before service may be attempted.

Yes — under r.17.02, a plaintiff may serve an originating process outside Ontario without leave where one of the prescribed circumstances applies, including where the contract was made in Ontario or is to be performed in Ontario.
Yes — a plaintiff may serve outside Ontario without leave in any commercial dispute without restrictions.
No — service outside Ontario is not recognized in Ontario proceedings; the plaintiff must domesticate the claim by serving an Ontario agent of the defendant.
expl: Under r.17.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), an originating process or notice of a reference may be served outside Ontario without leave of the court in a variety of prescribed circumstances. These include where the contract in dispute was made in Ontario, is to be performed in Ontario, or was breached in Ontario; where the tort was committed in Ontario; where there is real and substantial property situated in Ontario; and several other grounds. Rule 17.03 provides for service outside Ontario with leave in circumstances not covered by r.17.02. In this scenario, the contract was made and to be performed in Ontario — a ground listed in r.17.02 — so no leave is required.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.17.02; r.17.03
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Service of Process | Service Outside Ontario — Leave and Without Leave Under r.17

text:
A plaintiff in Ontario wishes to sue a German corporation for an alleged misrepresentation made entirely in Germany. The misrepresentation induced the plaintiff to enter into a contract in Germany. No other connection to Ontario is apparent. The plaintiff applies for leave to serve the defendant in Germany under r.17.03.

Under r.17.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what must a plaintiff demonstrate to obtain leave to serve an originating process outside Ontario?
opts: The plaintiff need only show that the defendant is a foreign corporation against whom a judgment would be recognizable in Ontario.

The plaintiff must demonstrate that: (1) there is a good arguable case on the merits; (2) there is a real and substantial connection between Ontario and the subject matter of the action; and (3) Ontario is the appropriate forum for the dispute.
The plaintiff must show that service within Ontario is not reasonably possible and that the defendant can be located in the foreign jurisdiction.
The plaintiff must show the defendant has assets in Ontario against which any eventual judgment could be enforced.
expl: Under r.17.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where a plaintiff seeks leave to serve outside Ontario in circumstances not covered by r.17.02, the court will grant leave where it is satisfied that: (1) the claim discloses a good arguable case; (2) there is a real and substantial connection between Ontario and the facts on which the proceeding is based; and (3) Ontario is the appropriate forum for the proceeding. The court must be satisfied as to jurisdiction and forum. In the scenario, the misrepresentation occurred in Germany and the contract was formed in Germany — the absence of any Ontario connection will make it very difficult to satisfy the real and substantial connection requirement.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.17.03
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Service of Process | Service Outside Ontario — Leave Required Under r.17.03

text:
A plaintiff served a statement of claim on January 5. The defendant has not yet delivered a statement of defence. On January 20, the plaintiff realizes a key paragraph in the claim is inaccurate and wants to amend it. Counsel asks whether leave of the court is required to amend the statement of claim at this stage.

Under r.26.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, when may a party amend a pleading without leave of the court?
opts: A pleading may only be amended with leave of the court at all times after it has been served.

Under r.26.02, a party may amend a pleading without leave at any time before the pleadings are closed, provided no statement of defence (or similar responding pleading) has been delivered by the opposing party.
A party may amend a pleading without leave at any time, including during or after trial.
A pleading may only be amended without leave within 10 days of service; after 10 days, leave of the court is required.
expl: Under r.26.02(a) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a party may amend a pleading without leave of the court at any time before the close of pleadings — specifically, before the responding party has delivered its defence or other responding pleading. In this scenario, the plaintiff served the claim on January 5 and the defendant has not yet delivered a statement of defence. The plaintiff may therefore amend the statement of claim as of right, without obtaining leave of the court, under r.26.02(a). Once the defendant delivers a statement of defence, the pleadings are no longer open to amendment as of right and leave under r.26.01 would be required.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.26.02
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Default Proceedings and Dismissal for Delay | Amendment of Pleadings as of Right Before Close of Pleadings

text:
An action was commenced in 2022 and has been proceeding very slowly. No steps have been taken in the action for more than two years. The registrar issues a status notice to the parties. The plaintiff's counsel is unsure what will happen if no further steps are taken.

Under r.48.14 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what occurs if no step is taken in the action for two years and the parties fail to respond adequately to a status notice?
opts: The action is automatically stayed until the plaintiff brings a motion to restore it to the active list.

The registrar will dismiss the action for delay without further notice if no step is taken in the action for two years and the parties fail to file a timetable or otherwise respond adequately to the status notice.
The court will schedule a case management conference to review the status of the action before any dismissal order is made.
The action will be struck from the trial list but not dismissed; the plaintiff may restart the action by paying an additional court fee.
expl: Under r.48.14 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where no step has been taken in an action for two years, the registrar may issue a status notice. If the parties do not respond to the status notice by filing a timetable or otherwise demonstrating that the action is being actively pursued, the registrar may dismiss the action for delay. This automatic dismissal mechanism is designed to clear dormant cases from the court's docket. A plaintiff who receives a status notice must act promptly to restore the action and file the required documents.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.48.14; r.24.01
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Default Proceedings and Dismissal for Delay | Dismissal for Delay — Status Notice and r.48.14

text:
Priya sues her former employer, Caldwell Logistics Ltd., for wrongful dismissal. Her draft statement of claim contains the following four items: (1) 'Caldwell terminated Priya's employment without notice on March 3, 2025'; (2) 'Caldwell's vice-president told Priya the reason was her performance, but Priya's annual reviews for the three preceding years all rated her performance as 'exceeds expectations''; (3) 'a psychologist's report documenting Priya's workplace stress symptoms'; and (4) 'Caldwell failed to act in good faith in the manner of dismissal.' Caldwell brings a motion to strike, arguing that items (2), (3), and (4) are improperly pleaded under r.25.06(1).

Under r.25.06(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, which of the following best classifies items (2), (3), and (4)?
opts: Item (2) is a properly pleaded material fact; item (3) is evidence that must be excluded from the pleading; item (4) is a bare legal conclusion that must be supported by specific material facts.
Items (2), (3), and (4) are all properly pleaded because they all relate to the wrongful dismissal claim and provide the defendant with notice of the case to meet.
Items (2) and (4) are both properly pleaded legal effects of facts, permissible under r.25.07; item (3) is a material fact that must remain in the pleading.
Item (2) is evidence and must be struck; item (3) is a material fact relating to damages; item (4) is properly pleaded as a legal conclusion identifying the cause of action.
expl: Rule 25.06(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure requires a pleading to contain material facts — not the evidence by which those facts will be proved, and not bare legal conclusions without underlying facts. Item (1) ('terminated without notice on March 3, 2025') is a material fact: it identifies the specific event with date and circumstances. Item (2) ('vice-president's stated reason' + 'three years of exceeding expectations reviews') is also a material fact: it pleads the specific conduct and circumstances that give rise to the bad faith claim. Item (3) (the psychologist's report) is evidence — a document that will prove a fact at trial — not a fact itself; it must not be pleaded in the statement of claim. Item (4) ('failed to act in good faith') is a bare legal conclusion; without identifying what specific acts or omissions constituted the bad faith, it adds nothing beyond a label and must be supported by or replaced with specific factual allegations.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.25.06(1), r.25.07
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Default Proceedings and Dismissal for Delay | Pleading Material Facts — Distinguishing Facts from Evidence

text:
The defendant receives the plaintiff's statement of claim alleging misrepresentation by the defendant. The claim contains general allegations of 'numerous false and misleading representations' without identifying the specific statements alleged to be false, when they were made, or to whom they were made. The defendant's counsel is unable to plead properly without more detail.

Under r.25.10 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what step may the defendant take to require the plaintiff to provide more specific detail before the defendant delivers its statement of defence?
opts: The defendant must file a motion to strike the statement of claim and cannot compel the plaintiff to provide more detail through any other mechanism.

Under r.25.10, the defendant may serve a demand for particulars on the plaintiff, requiring the plaintiff to supply particulars of any allegations in the pleading that are vague or insufficiently specific; if the plaintiff fails to provide particulars, the defendant may move for an order compelling them.
The defendant may file an application for summary judgment because the claim is too vague to establish a cause of action.
The defendant must simply deliver a general denial in its statement of defence and raise lack of particularity as a defence; no mechanism exists to compel particulars before pleading.
expl: Under r.25.10 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a party who requires more particulars of an allegation in a pleading may serve a demand for particulars on the pleading party. If the party served with the demand refuses to provide particulars or fails to respond, the requesting party may bring a motion under r.25.10 for an order requiring the particulars to be delivered. Particulars are additional specifics needed to enable the opposing party to know the case it must meet and to plead in response — here, the specific representations alleged to be false, when and to whom they were made. The demand for particulars is the proper and proportionate mechanism before resorting to a motion to strike.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.25.10
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Default Proceedings and Dismissal for Delay | Demand for Particulars — r.25.10

text:
Beatrix serves a notice of motion on the opposing party on a Monday. The motion is scheduled to be heard the following Wednesday, which is nine days later. The opposing party objects that insufficient notice has been given under the Rules of Civil Procedure.

Is the opposing party's objection well-founded?
opts: No — a notice of motion requires only 5 clear days' notice, so 9 days is more than sufficient.
Yes — r.37.07(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure requires at least 10 days' notice of a motion; serving 9 days before the hearing date is deficient by one day.
No — the 10-day notice requirement applies only to motions for summary judgment, not ordinary motions.
Yes — the notice period for any motion is 21 days, so 9 days' notice is far too short.
expl: Rule 37.07(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194) provides that, unless the court orders otherwise, a notice of motion shall be served at least 10 days before the hearing date. Nine days' notice falls one day short of the mandatory 10-day minimum. The opposing party's objection is well-founded. The 10-day default applies to motions generally; it is not limited to summary judgment motions or any other specific category.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.37.07(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Motions | Notice of Motion — Required Contents and Notice Period

text:
The defendant brings a motion under r.21.01(1)(b) to strike the plaintiff's statement of claim on the ground that it discloses no reasonable cause of action. The plaintiff's claim alleges that the defendant, a large retailer, deliberately created a shopping atmosphere that was unconscionably pressuring. The defendant argues the claim is novel and has no legal basis recognized in Ontario.

Under r.21.01(1)(b) of the Rules of Civil Procedure and Hunt v Carey Canada Inc, [1990] 2 SCR 959, what is the test for striking a pleading on the ground that it discloses no reasonable cause of action?
opts: The court strikes the claim if the defendant can demonstrate that the plaintiff is unlikely to succeed at trial on a balance of probabilities.

The court strikes the claim only where it is plain and obvious, assuming the facts as pleaded are true, that the claim discloses no reasonable cause of action; a novel or uncertain claim should not be struck on this ground.
The court strikes the claim if any element of the pleaded claim is disputed by the defendant on the facts.
The court strikes the claim where the allegations in the statement of claim are improbable or speculative.
expl: Under r.21.01(1)(b) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court may strike a pleading that discloses no reasonable cause of action. The test, established by the Supreme Court of Canada in Hunt v Carey Canada Inc, [1990] 2 SCR 959, is whether it is plain and obvious — assuming the facts as pleaded to be true — that the claim discloses no reasonable cause of action. The threshold is high: the claim should not be struck merely because it is novel, because the law is uncertain, or because the claim may be difficult to establish. Only if there is no chance of success should the claim be struck at this stage. On a motion under r.21.01(1)(b), no evidence is admissible — only the pleadings are considered.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.21.01(1)(b); Hunt v Carey Canada Inc, [1990] 2 SCR 959
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Motions | Striking Pleadings — No Reasonable Cause of Action Under r.21.01

text:
Patel Distribution Ltd. obtains an interlocutory injunction restraining Kowalski Logistics Inc. from operating a competing business pending trial. The trial judge ultimately finds in favour of Kowalski and dismisses Patel's action. Kowalski suffered significant revenue losses during the period the injunction was in force.

What is the standard condition imposed on a moving party who obtains an interlocutory injunction, and what is its significance to Kowalski?
opts: There is no standard condition on injunctions; Kowalski has no remedy for losses suffered during the injunction period.

The moving party is routinely required to give an undertaking as to damages — a promise to pay any losses suffered by the restrained party if the injunction is ultimately found to have been wrongly granted; Kowalski may claim on this undertaking.
An undertaking as to damages is optional and imposed only at the court's discretion; it is rare in commercial disputes.
Kowalski may recover losses only by commencing a new action for wrongful injunction, not by enforcing any undertaking given on the original motion.
expl: As a standard condition of granting an interlocutory injunction in Ontario, the moving party is required to give an undertaking as to damages. This undertaking is a promise to compensate the restrained party for any losses it suffers as a result of the injunction if the injunction is ultimately found to have been wrongly granted — that is, if the moving party does not succeed at trial. The undertaking protects the restrained party against the risk of being wrongly enjoined. Once Patel loses at trial, Kowalski may pursue the undertaking as to damages to recover its losses caused by the wrongful injunction, without commencing a separate action.
caseRef: RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.40
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Motions | Undertaking as to Damages — Condition for Interlocutory Injunction

text:
Osei-Appiah Construction Ltd. brings an ambitious summary judgment motion seeking dismissal of a $900,000 breach of contract claim. The motion record is comprehensive but the motion fails because the motion judge finds there are genuine issues of credibility requiring a trial. The plaintiff seeks substantial indemnity costs of the motion on the basis that the summary judgment motion was a tactical delay and was brought without a reasonable basis.

Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, when may a court award substantial indemnity costs against a party that brings an unsuccessful summary judgment motion?
opts: Substantial indemnity costs are automatically awarded against the losing party on any unsuccessful summary judgment motion.

Substantial indemnity costs may be awarded where the court finds the unsuccessful party's conduct in bringing the motion was reprehensible, vexatious, or devoid of any reasonable legal basis — not merely because the motion failed.
Substantial indemnity costs can only be awarded against a moving party if the opposing party proves the motion was brought in bad faith by sworn evidence.
The court may not award any costs on a failed summary judgment motion; the parties bear their own costs in that situation.
expl: Under r.57.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), costs follow the event on a failed summary judgment motion — the successful respondent is presumptively entitled to costs on a partial indemnity scale. Substantial indemnity costs are reserved for circumstances where a party's conduct has been reprehensible, scandalous, or outrageous. The mere fact that a summary judgment motion fails does not automatically warrant substantial indemnity costs. However, where the court finds the motion was brought for tactical purposes, without legal merit, or in a manner designed to harass or delay rather than to advance genuine summary disposition, a higher scale may be appropriate. An allegation alone that the motion was tactical is insufficient; the conduct must cross the threshold of reprehensibility.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Summary Judgment | Costs on Summary Judgment — Costs Consequences of Unsuccessful Motion

text:
Tremblay Media Inc. moves for summary judgment on a claim involving four discrete issues. The motion judge grants summary judgment on two of the four issues in favour of Tremblay and dismisses the motion on the remaining two issues, finding there are genuine issues requiring a trial on those. Neither party is entirely successful. Both parties seek full costs of the motion.

How will a court likely approach costs when a summary judgment motion succeeds on some issues but fails on others?
opts: The court will award full costs of the motion to Tremblay because it obtained judgment on at least some issues and is therefore the 'successful party.'
The court will award full costs of the motion to the defendant because Tremblay failed to obtain judgment on all issues and therefore did not fully succeed.

The court exercises broad discretion under r.57.01 and may apportion costs, order each party to bear its own costs, or award costs on specific issues — reflecting the mixed outcome and the relative success of each party.
On a partially successful summary judgment motion, costs are always in the cause and cannot be fixed until after the trial of the remaining issues.
expl: Under r.57.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court has broad discretion in fixing costs and may consider the result achieved, the relative success of each party, and all the circumstances. Where a summary judgment motion is partially successful, the court is not bound to follow a simple costs-follow-the-event rule. The court may: award costs on specific issues to the party who succeeded on those issues; apportion costs globally reflecting overall success; order no costs (each party bears its own); or award costs in the cause on the issues that must proceed to trial while awarding motion costs on the issues resolved. This approach reflects the nuanced discretion codified in r.57.01 and prevents either party from obtaining a windfall on a mixed-result motion.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01; r. 20.04(3)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Summary Judgment | Costs on Summary Judgment — Costs Where Motion Granted in Part Only

text:
Bhattacharya Engineering Ltd. is a defendant in a contract dispute. During documentary production, its counsel instructs the company's paralegal to prepare the affidavit of documents. The paralegal asks which documents should appear in each of the three schedules.

Under r.30.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, which of the following correctly describes the contents of each schedule in a properly prepared affidavit of documents?
opts: Schedule A lists all documents that the party produced voluntarily before the litigation began; Schedule B lists documents in the party's possession that have not yet been reviewed; Schedule C lists documents the party created after the statement of claim was served.

Schedule A lists documents in the party's possession, control, or power that are not privileged and are relevant to any matter in issue; Schedule B lists relevant documents for which privilege is claimed; Schedule C lists relevant documents that were formerly in the party's possession, control, or power but are no longer.
Schedule A lists all documents ever created by the party; Schedule B lists documents that the party does not wish to produce; Schedule C lists documents that the opposing party has already produced.

Schedule A lists documents the party will rely on at trial; Schedule B lists documents the party objects to producing on grounds of cost; Schedule C lists all third-party documents obtained by the party.
expl: Under r.30.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), an affidavit of documents is divided into three schedules. Schedule A contains documents in the party's possession, control, or power that are relevant to any matter in issue in the action and are not privileged — these must be produced. Schedule B contains documents in the party's possession, control, or power that are relevant to any matter in issue but are claimed to be privileged — these must be listed but need not be produced. Schedule C contains documents that were formerly in the party's possession, control, or power but are no longer — the party must identify when and how it ceased to have possession and the present location of the documents, if known.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.30.03
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Documentary Discovery | Affidavit of Documents — Three Schedules Under r.30.03

text:
Adekunle Energy Corp. is a defendant in a complex commercial dispute involving five separate contracts, each managed by a different vice-president. The plaintiff, Whitmore Systems Inc., argues that it needs to examine multiple corporate representatives — one for each contract — and that the corporation's designation of a single CFO who has general financial knowledge but no direct involvement in any of the five contracts is insufficient.

Under r.31.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, how many corporate representatives may a party be required to examine, and what remedy is available if the designated representative lacks sufficient knowledge?
opts: A corporation may always limit examination to a single designated representative, regardless of the complexity of the issues; the examining party has no right to examine additional individuals.
A corporation must make available as many representatives as the examining party requests, with no limit on the number.

A corporation generally designates one or more representatives on its behalf; if the designated representative lacks knowledge of the relevant matters, the examining party may bring a motion and the court may order examination of one or more additional persons with direct knowledge of the relevant matters.
The plaintiff may examine any officer or director of the corporation directly without the corporation's designation, as long as the individual has personal knowledge.
expl: Under r.31.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a corporation to be examined for discovery shall designate one or more officers, directors, or employees to be examined on its behalf. The corporation has the right to designate its representatives, but if the designated person or persons lack adequate knowledge of the relevant matters — for example, a CFO with no direct knowledge of the five specific contracts at issue — the examining party may bring a motion. The court has jurisdiction under r.31.03 to order that additional persons with direct knowledge be examined to ensure the examining party is not left without adequate discovery. There is no absolute right to examine every officer involved; the remedy lies in a court motion.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.31.03
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Examination for Discovery | Corporate Discovery — Multiple Representatives Under r.31.03

text:
At trial, the defendant's counsel seeks to read in portions of the defendant's own examination for discovery transcript as part of the defendant's case in chief. The defendant was examined for discovery by the plaintiff. The defendant is now available and testifying at trial. The plaintiff objects, arguing that a party may not use its own discovery evidence to bolster its own case.

Under r.31.11 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, may a party read in its own examination for discovery as part of its own case in chief?
opts: Yes — a party may always read in its own examination for discovery as part of its own case, because it constitutes part of the trial record.

No — under r.31.11, only the adverse party (the examining party) may read in the discovery evidence; the party who was examined cannot read in their own examination for discovery as part of their own case.
Yes — a party may read in its own examination for discovery but only to rebut evidence introduced by the opposing party.
No — once a party testifies at trial, their examination for discovery is automatically excluded from the record.
expl: Under r.31.11(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the right to use examination for discovery evidence at trial is conferred on the adverse party — the party who conducted the examination. The examination for discovery is a mechanism for the opposing party to obtain admissions and test the case of the party examined. A party cannot use its own examination for discovery as part of its own case in chief; to permit this would allow a party to create self-serving testimony outside the trial process. The rule operates as an asymmetric right: only the adverse party (here, the plaintiff) may read in the defendant's discovery evidence; the defendant cannot read in its own discovery evidence to bolster its own case.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.31.11(1)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Examination for Discovery | Use of Discovery Evidence at Trial — Adverse Party vs Own Client

text:
In a product liability action, the defendant served a written offer to settle for $200,000 two months before trial. The plaintiff rejected the offer and proceeded to trial. The plaintiff was awarded $250,000 — more than the defendant's offer. The defendant argues that because the judgment exceeded its own offer, there should be no cost consequences against the plaintiff, as the plaintiff 'beat' the defendant's offer.

Under r.49.10 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, does the cost consequences provision apply where the plaintiff obtains a judgment more favourable than the defendant's offer?
opts: Yes — r.49.10(1) still applies, and the plaintiff must pay the defendant's partial indemnity costs from the date of the offer, regardless of the outcome.

No — r.49.10(1) applies only where the plaintiff's recovery is no more favourable than the defendant's offer; where the plaintiff's recovery exceeds the defendant's offer, the defendant's offer is not triggered and the ordinary costs follow the event principle applies.
Yes — r.49.10(1) applies to any rejected offer, and the plaintiff must always pay substantial indemnity costs from the date the defendant made any offer, regardless of the trial outcome.
No — where the plaintiff's recovery exceeds any offer made by either party, costs are fixed at zero because all offers failed.
expl: Under r.49.10(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the defendant's cost-shifting consequence is triggered only where the plaintiff obtains a judgment that is no more favourable than the terms of the defendant's offer. In other words, the plaintiff must fail to beat the defendant's offer for r.49.10(1) to apply. Where, as here, the plaintiff's recovery of $250,000 exceeds the defendant's offer of $200,000, the plaintiff has obtained a result more favourable than the defendant's offer. Rule 49.10(1) is not triggered. The ordinary costs-follow-the-event rule applies, and the plaintiff is entitled to its costs as the successful party. The defendant's offer created no adverse cost consequence for the plaintiff.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.10(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Offers to Settle | Cost Consequences — Offer Not Accepted; Plaintiff's Recovery Exceeds Defendant's Offer

text:
Osei Pharmaceuticals Inc. brings an action against Bergman Distribution Ltd. for breach of a supply contract. The matter proceeds to a three-day judge-alone trial. Both parties have retained experienced counsel. On the morning of trial, Bergman's counsel rises first and announces that, given Bergman's extensive documentary evidence, the defence should present its case first to give the trial a proper commercial context. Osei's counsel objects, arguing the established order of proceedings governs. The trial judge must decide.

Under the established Ontario civil trial procedure, who presents their case first, and in what order do the parties address the court in closing — and may the trial judge accede to Bergman's request to reverse the order?
opts: Bergman's request is proper: in commercial cases, the party with the greater volume of documentary evidence presents first to orient the court; the judge has absolute discretion to set any order convenient to case management.

The plaintiff (Osei) presents its case first, followed by the defendant (Bergman); closing addresses are made with the plaintiff addressing last as the party bearing the burden of proof. The trial judge may vary this order in the interests of justice but the established default clearly favours the plaintiff presenting first.
The defendant presents its evidence first in all civil trials; the plaintiff then responds and has the right to the first and last addresses in closing, since the plaintiff bears the burden of proof.
Both parties present their cases simultaneously through exchanged written submissions; the trial judge renders judgment without live oral argument or witness examination.
expl: Under r.52 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194) and established Ontario civil trial practice, the default order of proceedings is: (1) plaintiff's opening address; (2) plaintiff's evidence; (3) defendant's opening address (if not given at the outset) and defendant's evidence; (4) plaintiff's closing address; (5) defendant's closing address (or plaintiff's reply). As the party bearing the burden of proof, the plaintiff goes first in both presenting evidence and addressing last in closing — the 'last word' reflects that the plaintiff must ultimately discharge the burden. The trial judge has some discretion to adjust the order in the interests of justice, but Bergman's rationale — that its documentary evidence is extensive — is not a recognised basis for reversing the default order. Granting Bergman's request would improperly shift the burden of proof dynamics: a defendant cannot put itself in the plaintiff's procedural shoes simply by having more documents.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.52
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Trial Procedure | Order of Proceedings at Trial — Who Opens and Closes

text:
At trial in a negligence action, the plaintiff calls a witness who testifies in chief that the defendant's vehicle was travelling at a reasonable speed. On cross-examination, defense counsel asks the witness whether she had told a bystander at the scene that the car was 'going very fast.' The plaintiff's counsel objects to the question on the basis that it is a leading question.

Which statement correctly describes the permissible scope of cross-examination and the use of leading questions in Ontario civil trials?
opts: Leading questions are never permitted on cross-examination; all questions on cross-examination must be open-ended.
Cross-examination is limited to the topics covered in the witness's examination in chief; questions about prior inconsistent statements are not permitted on cross-examination.

On cross-examination, counsel may use leading questions and may explore any relevant matter, including matters not covered in examination in chief, such as prior inconsistent statements; leading questions are generally permissible on cross-examination.
Cross-examination may only be conducted if the examining party has first obtained leave of the trial judge to depart from the scope of examination in chief.
expl: Under the Evidence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.23 and r.53 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, cross-examination in Ontario civil trials is broader in scope than examination in chief. On cross-examination, counsel may: (1) use leading questions (which suggest the answer in the question itself); (2) explore any relevant matter, including matters not covered in examination in chief; and (3) put prior inconsistent statements to the witness to impeach their credibility. The objection to a leading question on cross-examination is unfounded — leading questions are a standard and permitted feature of cross-examination. The question about what the witness told the bystander at the scene is a proper cross-examination question going to credibility through a prior inconsistent statement.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.53; Evidence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.23, s.23
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Trial Procedure | Cross-Examination at Trial — Scope and Leading Questions

text:
An action for breach of contract was commenced in May 2019. By May 2024 — five years later — the action has not been set down for trial. The registrar notes that the action has not been placed on the trial list and no status notice has been issued. The defendant brings a motion to dismiss the action for delay under r.48.14 of the Rules of Civil Procedure.

Under r.48.14 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what power does the court have to dismiss an action for delay, and what must the plaintiff show to prevent dismissal?
opts: The court has no power to dismiss an action for delay unless the defendant has formally served a demand for a trial date.

Under r.48.14, where an action has not been set down for trial within the time period prescribed by the rules, the registrar may serve a status notice on the parties; if the action is not set down or dismissed by consent within 90 days after the status notice, the registrar shall dismiss the action for delay. On a motion to dismiss, the plaintiff must show a satisfactory explanation for the delay and that no prejudice will result from the action continuing.
An action is automatically dismissed after three years of inactivity without any status notice or motion requirement.
The court may dismiss an action for delay only where the limitation period for the underlying claim has also expired.
expl: Under r.48.14 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the registrar may issue a status notice to the parties where an action has not been set down for trial within the prescribed period. If the action is not set down or dismissed by consent within 90 days of the status notice, the registrar shall dismiss the action for delay. On a motion to dismiss for delay (or to resist one), the plaintiff must demonstrate: (1) a satisfactory explanation for the delay; and (2) that the defendant will not suffer prejudice from the action continuing. Courts assess both the reason for the delay and any prejudice caused to the defendant. A five-year dormant action is vulnerable to a r.48.14 dismissal motion.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.48.14
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Trial Procedure | Dismissal of Action for Delay — r.48.14

text:
Larochelle Hotels Inc. brings a claim against a commercial tenant for unpaid rent. The tenant serves a jury notice requiring the action to be tried by judge and jury. Larochelle Hotels argues that the claim involves complex accounting issues and that a jury trial is inappropriate. Larochelle brings a motion to strike the jury notice.

Under s.108 of the Courts of Justice Act and the applicable Rules of Civil Procedure, when may the court strike a jury notice in a civil case?
opts: A party may not strike a jury notice once it has been validly served; the right to a jury trial in civil proceedings is absolute and cannot be overridden by the court.

The court may strike a jury notice where the case involves matters that require prolonged examination of accounts or technical scientific questions that cannot be conveniently dealt with by a jury, or where a jury trial would otherwise be inappropriate.
A jury notice may be struck only where the defendant has failed to pay the required jury fee within the prescribed time.
The court may strike a jury notice only if all parties consent to the action being tried without a jury.
expl: Under s.108 of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43 (as applicable), a party may require a jury trial in a civil proceeding by serving a jury notice. However, the court has discretion to strike a jury notice where the case involves matters that are not suitable for jury determination, including: prolonged examination of accounts, technical scientific or commercial questions that cannot be conveniently dealt with by a jury, or where a jury trial would be inappropriate in the circumstances. Complex accounting issues in a commercial rent dispute may provide a basis for the court to strike the jury notice, as such matters are among the recognized categories for which a jury trial can be struck. The right to a civil jury trial is not absolute.
caseRef: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.108; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.47
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Trial Procedure | Right to Jury Trial in Civil Proceedings

text:
At trial, the plaintiff completes its evidence and rests its case. The defendant's counsel rises and moves for a non-suit, arguing that the plaintiff has failed to adduce any evidence on the element of causation. The plaintiff's counsel argues that the motion should be refused because the defendant will be required to elect not to call evidence before the non-suit motion can be heard.

Under Ontario civil trial procedure, what is the rule regarding a defendant's election on a non-suit motion?
opts: A non-suit motion may be heard at any time during trial and the defendant need not make any election before the motion is heard.

A defendant who moves for a non-suit in a civil trial must elect not to call evidence before the non-suit motion will be heard; if the defendant elects not to call evidence and the non-suit is refused, the case goes to the trier of fact on the plaintiff's evidence alone.
The defendant may bring a non-suit motion but retains the right to call evidence regardless of the outcome of the motion.
Non-suit motions are not available in civil proceedings; they are a procedure unique to criminal trials.
expl: Under r.52 of the Rules of Civil Procedure and established Ontario civil trial practice, a defendant who moves for a non-suit (arguing that the plaintiff has failed to make out a prima facie case) must first elect not to call any evidence. This election is required before the motion will be entertained by the court. The rationale is that a defendant should not be able to test the strength of the plaintiff's case through a non-suit motion and then, if the motion fails, have the opportunity to call evidence. If the defendant makes the election and the non-suit is refused, the case proceeds on the basis of the plaintiff's evidence alone, and the trier of fact determines whether the plaintiff has proved its case. The plaintiff's counsel's position on the election requirement is correct.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.52; Ontario civil trial practice
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Trial Procedure | Non-Suit Motion — When Available and the Test

text:
In a breach of contract action, the plaintiff claims $500,000 but is awarded only $25,000 at trial. The defendant seeks an order that, because the plaintiff's recovery was so disproportionately small relative to the amount claimed, the defendant should receive its costs of the action rather than the plaintiff.

Under the general costs principle in r.57.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, who is ordinarily entitled to costs after a contested trial, and does the disproportionately small recovery change this?
opts: Costs are always split equally between the parties where one party wins but recovers significantly less than claimed.

The successful party at trial is ordinarily entitled to costs (costs follow the event); however, the court has discretion under r.57.01 to make any order as to costs, and a grossly disproportionate recovery may be a factor the court considers in exercising that discretion — but the defendant is not automatically entitled to costs simply because the plaintiff recovered far less than claimed.
The defendant is automatically entitled to costs whenever the plaintiff's recovery is less than one-half of the amount claimed.
There are no costs consequences where the plaintiff succeeds at trial; costs are only awarded to defendants who succeed.
expl: Under r.57.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court has a broad and general discretion to award costs, and the successful party ordinarily receives costs (costs follow the event). The plaintiff here succeeded — obtaining judgment for $25,000 — and is prima facie entitled to costs. However, the court has discretion to consider the amount claimed versus the amount recovered as a factor under r.57.01(1), and may reduce the costs award or make no costs order or even award costs to the defendant where a plaintiff has advanced a wholly disproportionate claim. This is a discretionary exercise, not an automatic rule; the defendant is not entitled to costs simply because the plaintiff recovered less than claimed, but the court may adjust the costs award in light of the disproportionality.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 — Costs | Costs Follow the Event — General Principle and Exceptions

text:
Mensah v. Johansson Ltd. is a commercial fraud action. The plaintiff succeeds at trial. In exercising its discretion on costs, the judge must determine whether to award partial indemnity costs or substantial indemnity costs. The defendant engaged in sharp litigation practices — making numerous last-minute adjournment requests, filing unnecessary cross-motions, and generally prolonging the proceedings — but did not engage in any dishonest or fraudulent conduct during the litigation itself.

Which costs scale should the court apply, and why?
opts: Solicitor-client costs, because the plaintiff was successful in a fraud action and the defendant's litigation conduct caused unnecessary delay.

Partial indemnity costs, because substantial indemnity costs require reprehensible, scandalous, or outrageous conduct that goes beyond sharp litigation practices, and the defendant's conduct here — though prolific and dilatory — does not cross that threshold.
Substantial indemnity costs, because the defendant made unnecessary procedural motions and caused the plaintiff to incur extra costs.
Costs are not available to the plaintiff because the defendant's conduct, while problematic, did not include fraud within the litigation.
expl: Under r.57.01(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), partial indemnity costs are the ordinary scale awarded to a successful party. Substantial indemnity costs — approximately 1.5 times partial indemnity — are reserved for cases where a party's litigation conduct has been reprehensible, scandalous, or outrageous. Sharp litigation practices — making last-minute adjournments, filing unnecessary motions, and causing delay — are dilatory and may attract adverse comment, but courts have generally held that such conduct, while deserving of censure, does not automatically cross the threshold into the reprehensible or outrageous conduct required for substantial indemnity. Partial indemnity is the appropriate scale on these facts; the r.57.01(1) factors for fixing the quantum of partial indemnity costs can account for the impact of the defendant's conduct on the proceedings.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 — Costs | Partial Indemnity vs Substantial Indemnity — Distinguishing the Scales

text:
Reyes Construction Ltd. succeeds in a complex contract action where the defendant knowingly maintained a fraudulent defence for three years despite possessing internal emails proving the contract was performed as alleged. The trial judge awards costs. Reyes seeks solicitor-client costs; the defendant argues only partial indemnity is appropriate.

Which statement correctly identifies the three costs scales in Ontario and the circumstances appropriate to each?
opts: There are only two costs scales in Ontario: partial indemnity (the default) and solicitor-client costs (full indemnity, reserved for the most egregious misconduct).

There are three costs scales: partial indemnity (the default, awarded to successful parties); substantial indemnity (approximately 1.5 times partial indemnity, awarded for reprehensible litigation conduct); and solicitor-client costs (full indemnity, reserved for the most serious misconduct, bad faith, or abuse of process). Knowingly maintaining a fraudulent defence warrants substantial indemnity or solicitor-client costs at the court's discretion.

There are three costs scales: partial indemnity (50% of actual fees); substantial indemnity (75% of actual fees); and solicitor-client costs (100% of actual fees). The court must choose the scale that corresponds exactly to the percentage of fees the court believes was reasonable.
There is only one costs scale in Ontario — partial indemnity — and the court has no power to award costs beyond the Tariff A amounts except in cases of contempt.
expl: Under r.57.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194) and established Ontario practice, there are three costs scales in Ontario civil litigation. Partial indemnity is the default scale awarded to successful parties; it reflects a partial recovery of actual legal costs, typically assessed against Tariff A guidelines. Substantial indemnity — approximately 1.5 times partial indemnity — is awarded where a party's litigation conduct has been reprehensible, scandalous, or outrageous, or where triggered by the offer-to-settle regime under r.49.10(2). Solicitor-client costs represent full indemnity for actual legal fees reasonably incurred and are reserved for the most egregious misconduct, bad faith litigation, or abuse of process. Knowingly maintaining a fraudulent defence for three years in the face of contradictory evidence is classic reprehensible conduct that supports substantial indemnity costs at minimum, and may warrant solicitor-client costs in the court's discretion.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 — Costs | Solicitor-Client Costs vs Substantial Indemnity — The Three Scales

text:
A party loses a final judgment in the Superior Court of Justice in a commercial action where the amount in dispute is $350,000. The party wishes to appeal. Counsel must determine whether the appeal goes to the Divisional Court or the Court of Appeal.

Which court has jurisdiction to hear an appeal from a final order of the Superior Court of Justice where the amount in dispute exceeds $50,000?
opts: The Divisional Court hears all appeals from the Superior Court of Justice regardless of the amount.
The Court of Appeal for Ontario has jurisdiction under s.6 of the Courts of Justice Act to hear appeals from final orders of the Superior Court where the amount in dispute exceeds $50,000.
The Supreme Court of Canada has direct jurisdiction to hear appeals from the Superior Court of Justice in commercial matters over $50,000.
The Court of Appeal and the Divisional Court share concurrent jurisdiction over all appeals from the Superior Court of Justice.
expl: Under s.6(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, an appeal lies to the Court of Appeal from a final order of the Superior Court of Justice in a proceeding where the amount in dispute is more than $50,000. The Divisional Court's appellate jurisdiction under s.19 of the Courts of Justice Act is limited to appeals from final orders of the Superior Court where the amount in dispute does not exceed $50,000, and to interlocutory orders with leave. With $350,000 in dispute, the Court of Appeal is the correct appellate forum.
caseRef: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.6(1)(b); s.19; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.61
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Appeals | Appeal Routes — Court of Appeal vs Divisional Court

text:
A claimant obtains a final judgment against a contractor for $38,000 in the Superior Court of Justice. The contractor wishes to appeal the judgment. Counsel must identify the correct court to which the appeal lies.

Under s.19 of the Courts of Justice Act, which court hears appeals from final orders of the Superior Court of Justice where the amount in dispute does not exceed $50,000?
opts: The Court of Appeal for Ontario, as it has jurisdiction over all appeals from the Superior Court of Justice.
The Divisional Court, which under s.19(1)(a) of the Courts of Justice Act hears appeals from final orders of the Superior Court where the amount in dispute does not exceed $50,000.
The Small Claims Court, as a final judgment of $38,000 falls within its monetary jurisdiction.
