Under s. 737 of the Criminal Code as currently in force, may the sentencing judge waive the victim surcharge?
opts: No — the victim surcharge is mandatory and cannot be waived under any circumstances.
Yes — the judge may waive the surcharge if the offender establishes undue hardship.
Yes — the judge always has an unlimited discretion to waive the surcharge.
No — but the judge may defer payment of the surcharge for up to 10 years.
expl: The victim surcharge under s. 737 of the Criminal Code has had a complex legislative history. Under the 2019 amendments (Bill C-75), the court was given discretion to waive the surcharge where payment would cause the offender undue hardship. Prior to the 2019 amendments, the surcharge was mandatory with no waiver power (as enacted by the Increasing Offenders' Accountability for Victims Act, 2013). The Supreme Court in R. v. Boudreault, 2018 SCC 58 struck down the mandatory surcharge as unconstitutional under ss. 7 and 12 of the Charter for offenders in Alejandro's circumstances. Following Boudreault, Parliament reintroduced a waiver discretion in s. 737(3): the court may order that no surcharge be paid if it is satisfied that the offender is unable to pay it or that paying it would cause undue hardship to the offender or their dependants.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 737(3); R. v. Boudreault, 2018 SCC 58
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9: Sentencing | Victim Surcharge — s. 737

text:
A 14-year-old, Jayla, is arrested at 2:00 a.m. on suspicion of robbery. She has a diagnosed intellectual disability that places her cognitive functioning at approximately a 10-year-old level. Police read Jayla her rights and the YCJA waiver rights in plain language, and she nodded. They then advised her she could speak to a lawyer or parent; she said 'I don't need them.' Police questioned Jayla for 90 minutes, during which she made incriminating admissions. At the voir dire, a psychologist testifies that Jayla likely did not understand the consequences of speaking to police without counsel. Police testify they believed she understood and waived voluntarily.

Under s. 146 of the YCJA, which element of admissibility is most directly in contest on these facts, and what is the governing standard?
opts: The voluntariness of the statement — assessed under the common law confessions rule — is the only relevant test; the young person's intellectual disability goes only to weight.

The operating mind requirement — whether Jayla had the cognitive capacity to understand what she was saying and its consequences — is the contested element; it is assessed subjectively based on Jayla's actual state of mind, not on what the police reasonably believed.
The parental/counsel notification requirement is the sole dispositive issue; because Jayla waived notification, the statement is admissible and no further inquiry is necessary.
The statement is automatically excluded because the police failed to seek a judicial order authorizing questioning of a young person with an intellectual disability before proceeding.
expl: Section 146 of the YCJA imposes a layered admissibility framework for statements by young persons that is more protective than the general common law confessions rule. In addition to common law voluntariness, the YCJA requires: (i) the young person was informed of their right to retain counsel, have a parent/adult present, and was advised of the right to silence; (ii) the young person had a reasonable opportunity to consult counsel or an adult; and (iii) the young person waived those rights. The hardest element on these facts is the 'operating mind' requirement — a component of voluntariness that asks whether the young person had the cognitive capacity to understand what they were saying and appreciate the consequences of making a statement. The standard is subjective: the court must assess Jayla's actual state of mind and cognitive capacity, not merely what a reasonable person in her position would understand, and not what the police believed she understood. The psychologist's evidence that Jayla likely did not grasp the consequences directly challenges operating mind. A police belief that she understood is legally insufficient if the subjective operating mind threshold is not met.
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 146; R. v. L.T.H., 2008 SCC 49
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Statements by Young Persons — YCJA s. 146

text:
A 16-year-old is charged with aggravated assault. A local newspaper reporter attends the youth court proceedings and wishes to publish a story identifying the young person by name, including a photo. The young person has not been sentenced to an adult sentence.

Under s. 110 of the YCJA, which of the following correctly states the default rule regarding publication of identifying information about a young person who is the subject of youth court proceedings?
opts: Publication of identifying information about a young person is prohibited unless the young person consents and the court orders publication.

Publication of identifying information about a young person who has not received an adult sentence is prohibited; however, the young person may themselves apply to the court for an order permitting publication.
Publication of identifying information is prohibited as a default rule, but becomes automatically permitted once the young person turns 18.
There is no prohibition on publication because youth court proceedings are open to the public.
expl: Section 110(1) of the YCJA prohibits any person from publishing the name of a young person, or any other information related to a young person, if it would identify the young person as a person dealt with under the Act. This prohibition applies to young persons who have not received an adult sentence. However, s. 110(2) and (3) create limited exceptions. Notably, s. 110(2)(b) provides that a young person who is 18 years of age or older may apply to the youth justice court for an order permitting publication. Also, publication may be permitted if a young person is at large and dangerous (s. 110(4)), or in certain other exceptional circumstances. The publication ban is a default rule reflecting the YCJA's principle of protecting young persons' privacy to facilitate rehabilitation.
caseRef: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 110(1), s. 110(2)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10: Youth Criminal Justice | Publication Prohibition — YCJA s. 110

text:
The accused is acquitted by a jury of first degree murder after the trial judge incorrectly directed the jury on the law of mens rea. The Crown wishes to appeal the acquittal.

Under s. 676 of the Criminal Code, on what grounds may the Crown appeal an acquittal by a jury?
opts: The Crown may appeal on any ground — questions of law, fact, or mixed fact and law — as of right.
The Crown may appeal only on a question of law, as of right.
The Crown may not appeal a jury acquittal in Canada because of the constitutional prohibition against double jeopardy.
The Crown may appeal on any ground with leave of the Court of Appeal, including questions of fact.
expl: Section 676(1) of the Criminal Code provides that the Attorney General or counsel acting on behalf of the Attorney General may appeal against a verdict of acquittal or a judgment in favour of the accused on any ground of appeal that involves a question of law alone. The Crown's right to appeal an acquittal is significantly more restricted than the accused's appeal rights. The Crown may appeal a jury acquittal only on a question of law (including a legal error in the trial judge's charge to the jury), and this is an as-of-right appeal. The Crown cannot appeal on questions of fact, mixed fact and law, or on the ground that the verdict was unreasonable. If the Crown's appeal is successful, the Court of Appeal may set aside the acquittal and enter a conviction, order a new trial, or make any other order that justice requires (s. 686(4)).
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 676(1), s. 686(4); Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 11(h)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11: Appeals | Crown Appeal of Acquittal — s. 676

text:
The accused pleads guilty to sexual assault and is sentenced to a conditional sentence order of 18 months in the community. The Crown, believing the sentence is demonstrably unfit, seeks to appeal the sentence to the Court of Appeal.

Under s. 676(1)(d) of the Criminal Code, on what basis may the Crown appeal a sentence imposed at trial?
opts: The Crown may appeal a sentence only if the trial judge committed an error of law in the sentencing principles applied.
The Crown may appeal a sentence with leave of the Court of Appeal, on the ground that the sentence is inadequate.
The Crown may appeal a sentence as of right, without leave, whenever the Crown considers the sentence manifestly inadequate.
The Crown has no right to appeal a sentence imposed following a guilty plea.
expl: Section 676(1)(d) of the Criminal Code provides that the Attorney General may appeal to the Court of Appeal against the sentence passed by a trial court, with leave of the Court of Appeal, unless the sentence is one fixed by law. On a Crown sentence appeal, the Court of Appeal applies the standard of review from R v Lacasse, 2015 SCC 64: the Court of Appeal may intervene only if the sentence is demonstrably unfit or the trial judge committed an error in principle that had an impact on the sentence. The ground for a Crown sentence appeal is that the sentence is 'inadequate' from the Crown's perspective — essentially, demonstrably unfit on the low end. Leave is required for Crown sentence appeals, unlike the Crown's as-of-right appeal on questions of law from acquittals (s.676(1)(a)).
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 676(1)(d); R v Lacasse, 2015 SCC 64
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11: Appeals | Crown Sentence Appeal — s. 676(1)(d)

text:
The accused was acquitted of impaired driving. The trial judge, after finding the accused's blood alcohol level to be 0.092 at the time of testing, concluded that this was insufficient to establish impairment at the time of driving. The Crown appeals, arguing the trial judge erred in assessing the evidence. The accused argues this is a finding of fact unreviewable by the Crown on appeal.

The Crown's appeal is properly grounded as a question of law if which of the following is true?
opts: The trial judge reached a conclusion on the evidence that another judge might reasonably have reached differently.
The trial judge applied a legal standard requiring the Crown to prove impairment at a threshold beyond what the law requires, constituting a legal error in the standard of proof.
The trial judge made credibility findings that the Court of Appeal considers unreasonable.
The trial judge failed to give reasons for rejecting the Crown's expert evidence on back-calculation.
expl: The distinction between a question of law and a question of fact (or mixed fact and law) is critical to Crown appeals under s.676(1)(a), which restricts the Crown to questions of law alone. A 'question of law' on appeal includes: errors in the legal standard applied, errors in the legal test for an element of an offence, errors in the burden of proof, and unreasonable verdicts (in some formulations). A 'question of fact' is a finding about what happened — it is not reviewable by the Crown. On these facts, if the trial judge incorrectly applied the legal threshold for impairment — requiring proof to a standard higher than required by law — that is a question of law. For example, if the trial judge incorrectly concluded that 0.092 BAC could not, as a matter of law, constitute impairment, that is a legal error. By contrast, if the judge simply weighed the evidence and was not satisfied beyond a reasonable doubt, that is a question of fact not open to Crown appeal.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 676(1)(a); R v Biniaris, 2000 SCC 15
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11: Appeals | Error of Law vs. Mixed Fact and Law — Crown Appeal

text:
Renata is charged with theft over $5,000, a straight indictable offence. She elects trial by judge alone in the Ontario Superior Court of Justice. After a five-day trial, the Superior Court judge convicts her. Renata believes the judge misapplied the law on the definition of 'fraudulently' and also made a factual finding she views as unreasonable. Her counsel advises her that the appeal route and available grounds depend on the mode of trial.

To which court should Renata appeal, and on what grounds is her appeal available as of right versus with leave?
opts: Renata must appeal to the Ontario Court of Justice, because that court has jurisdiction over all appeals from indictable offence convictions tried in the Superior Court.
Renata appeals to the Court of Appeal for Ontario; on a question of law she appeals as of right, and on a question of fact or mixed fact and law she requires leave of the Court of Appeal.
Renata appeals to the Court of Appeal for Ontario on all grounds as of right, because a judge-alone trial — unlike a jury trial — allows full appellate review of fact without leave.
Renata appeals to the Divisional Court because she was tried in the Superior Court; the Court of Appeal only hears appeals from jury trials.
expl: Under s. 675(1) of the Criminal Code, a person convicted of an indictable offence appeals to the Court of Appeal of the province (here, the Court of Appeal for Ontario). The appeal court is the same whether the accused was tried before a judge alone or a jury — both routes go to the Court of Appeal, not to a lower court. The grounds available, however, are the same under s. 675(1) regardless of mode of trial: (a) on a question of law alone — as of right; (b) on a question of fact alone, or mixed fact and law — with leave of the Court of Appeal or on certification by the trial judge. Renata's argument that the judge misapplied the law on 'fraudulently' is a question of law, available as of right. Her challenge to the factual finding is a question of fact, which requires leave. The mode of trial (judge alone vs. jury) does not expand the grounds available as of right on an indictable conviction appeal — leave is still required for fact questions under either mode.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 675(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11: Appeals | Indictable Conviction Appeal — s. 675

text:
The accused is convicted of robbery after a jury trial. On appeal, the Court of Appeal finds that the trial judge made a legal error in instructing the jury on the defence of honest but mistaken belief. However, the evidence of the accused's guilt was overwhelming — numerous eyewitnesses and DNA evidence directly implicated the accused. The Court of Appeal considers whether to apply the curative proviso.

Under s. 686(1)(b)(iii) of the Criminal Code, in which circumstances may the Court of Appeal dismiss an appeal despite finding that the trial judge made an error of law?
opts: Where the accused consents to dismissal of the appeal and waives the right to a new trial.
Where no substantial wrong or miscarriage of justice occurred as a result of the error — the curative proviso applies to harmless legal errors.
Where the Crown establishes that the accused would have been convicted on the same charges even without the error.
Where the error was procedural rather than substantive.
expl: Section 686(1)(b)(iii) of the Criminal Code — the 'curative proviso' — provides that the Court of Appeal may dismiss an appeal from conviction where, despite finding that there was an error of law at trial, the Court is of the opinion that no substantial wrong or miscarriage of justice occurred. The test for applying the proviso was clarified in R v Khan, 2001 SCC 86, and R v Bevan, [1993] 2 SCR 599: the proviso applies in two situations: (i) where the error was harmless in the sense that it could not have affected the verdict (minor procedural irregularity); or (ii) where the evidence of guilt was so overwhelming that any reasonable and properly instructed jury would inevitably have convicted. On these facts, with overwhelming eyewitness and DNA evidence, the second branch (overwhelming evidence) of the proviso would likely apply, allowing the Court of Appeal to dismiss the appeal despite the jury instruction error.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 686(1)(b)(iii); R v Khan, 2001 SCC 86; R v Bevan, [1993] 2 SCR 599
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11: Appeals | Curative Proviso — s. 686(1)(b)(iii)

text:
Roland was convicted of second degree murder in 1998 and has exhausted all conventional appeals, including an unsuccessful application for leave to the Supreme Court of Canada. New DNA evidence has emerged that Roland's supporters argue conclusively exonerates him. Roland's counsel wishes to pursue any available remedy.

Under s. 696.1 of the Criminal Code, what process is available to Roland, and what must be established to obtain a remedy?
opts: Roland may apply directly to the Supreme Court of Canada for a re-hearing of his appeal on the basis of the new evidence.

Roland may apply to the Minister of Justice under s. 696.1 for a review of his conviction; the Minister may direct a new trial or refer the matter to the Court of Appeal if satisfied there is a reasonable basis to conclude a miscarriage of justice likely occurred.
Roland may apply to the Ontario Court of Appeal for a fresh evidence hearing under s. 683 of the Criminal Code without any preconditions.
Roland's only remedy is a pardon or record suspension through the Parole Board of Canada.
expl: Section 696.1 of the Criminal Code establishes the ministerial review mechanism — sometimes called the 'conviction review' process — for persons who have been convicted of an offence and who have exhausted all available appeals. The applicant must satisfy the Minister of Justice that there are reasonable grounds to conclude that a miscarriage of justice likely occurred. If satisfied, under s. 696.3, the Minister may: (a) direct a new trial before any court the Minister thinks proper; or (b) refer the matter to the Court of Appeal for hearing and determination as if it were an appeal by the convicted person (or on a specific question as if it were a question of law). The ministerial review process is an extraordinary remedy of last resort designed to address wrongful convictions — it is available only after conventional appeals are exhausted. The DNA evidence in Roland's case would be the basis for the application, and the Minister would assess whether it provides reasonable grounds to conclude a miscarriage of justice likely occurred.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 696.1, s. 696.3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies | Ministerial Review — s. 696.1

text:
The accused was convicted of manslaughter. On appeal, the Court of Appeal finds that defence counsel at trial failed to call a critical alibi witness whose evidence was known to counsel before trial. The defence of the case at trial was inadequate. The accused argues his conviction resulted in a miscarriage of justice.

Under s. 686(1)(a)(iii) of the Criminal Code, what must the accused demonstrate to establish that the conviction resulted in a miscarriage of justice?
opts: The accused must prove that defence counsel was incompetent and that this incompetence caused the conviction.

The accused must show that a miscarriage of justice occurred — including that the trial was unfair or the verdict is unreliable as a result of counsel's failure — such that the verdict cannot be allowed to stand.
The accused need only show that the outcome might have been different if the alibi witness had testified.
The accused must demonstrate that the trial judge was aware of the ineffective assistance and failed to intervene.
expl: Section 686(1)(a)(iii) allows the Court of Appeal to allow an appeal where the verdict resulted in a miscarriage of justice. For claims of ineffective assistance of counsel, the test is derived from R v GDB, 2000 SCC 22: the accused must establish (1) that counsel's acts or omissions constituted incompetence (falling below the standard of a reasonably competent criminal lawyer), and (2) that the incompetence resulted in a miscarriage of justice — meaning the trial was not fair or the verdict is unreliable. The miscarriage of justice component requires more than showing the outcome 'might have been' different — it requires a reasonable probability that the verdict would have been different but for the incompetence, or that the trial was rendered fundamentally unfair. Merely proving incompetence without the resulting prejudice is insufficient.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 686(1)(a)(iii); R v GDB, 2000 SCC 22
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies | Miscarriage of Justice — Ineffective Assistance of Counsel — s. 686(1)(a)(iii)

text:
The accused is charged with drug trafficking. During the investigation, police engaged in outrageous misconduct: they planted evidence, fabricated documents, and suborned perjury from informants. The accused moves under s. 24(1) of the Charter for a stay of proceedings, arguing that proceeding to trial would bring the administration of justice into disrepute.

Under the framework established in R v O'Connor (1995) and affirmed in subsequent jurisprudence, what is the legal test for granting a stay of proceedings as a remedy under s. 24(1) of the Charter?
opts: A stay is available whenever the accused's Charter rights have been violated, regardless of the severity of the breach.
A stay is available only for breaches of ss. 7, 11(d), or 11(h) of the Charter — not for all Charter rights.

A stay is an exceptional remedy granted only where: (1) the prejudice to the accused's right to a fair trial or to the integrity of the justice system will be manifested, perpetuated, or aggravated through the conduct of the trial or its outcome; and (2) no other remedy is reasonably capable of removing that prejudice.
A stay is an available remedy wherever a court finds that police misconduct occurred, as a general deterrent.
expl: In R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411, the Supreme Court established the two-part test for granting a stay of proceedings under s. 24(1) of the Charter as an abuse of process or Charter remedy. The test is: (1) the prejudice to the accused's right to a fair trial or to the integrity of the justice system will be manifested, perpetuated, or aggravated through the conduct of the trial or by its outcome; and (2) no other remedy is reasonably capable of removing that prejudice. The stay is described as a remedy of last resort — granted only in the 'clearest of cases' where irreparable harm to the fairness of the trial or to the integrity of the justice system cannot otherwise be addressed. On these facts (planted evidence, fabricated documents, suborned perjury), the case is a strong candidate for a stay as the only remedy that would address the systemic abuse — proceeding to trial would perpetuate the prejudice to the justice system's integrity.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 24(1); R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies | Stay of Proceedings — Charter s. 24(1) — O'Connor Test

text:
Nina discovered her husband Leo had an affair two years ago. After the discovery, she decided to try to save the marriage and they resumed cohabitation for eight months, though Nina remained deeply troubled by the affair. Ultimately, the reconciliation failed and Nina now seeks a divorce on the ground of adultery. Leo argues that Nina's return to cohabitation after learning of the affair constitutes condonation, which bars her from using adultery as the divorce ground.

Under the Divorce Act, what is condonation and does it bar Nina's divorce on the ground of adultery?
opts: Condonation is not a recognized bar to divorce; Leo's argument has no legal basis.

Condonation under DA s.11(1)(c) occurs when a spouse forgives the other's matrimonial offence, typically evidenced by resuming cohabitation; however, the court has discretion under DA s.11(2) to still grant the divorce where the court is satisfied that it would be in the interests of public morality to do so.

Condonation under DA s.11(1)(c) operates as a bar to using the condoned offence as a ground for divorce; however, s.11(2) gives the court discretion to grant the divorce despite condonation if the court is satisfied it would not be contrary to the public interest to do so; a genuine failed reconciliation attempt may support the court exercising its discretion.
Condonation automatically bars the divorce and Nina must wait one year from the separation that followed the failed reconciliation before she can obtain a divorce.
expl: Under Divorce Act s.11(1)(c), condonation of the other spouse's adultery or cruelty is a circumstance the court must take into account; if the court finds condonation, it must satisfy itself whether it would be in the public interest to grant the divorce (s.11(2)). The court has discretion — condonation is not an absolute bar to divorce (unlike collusion, which is absolute). Nina's genuine attempt at reconciliation that ultimately failed may weigh in favour of granting the divorce on adultery, as the policy behind the bar is to discourage opportunistic use of a condoned offence, not to penalize genuine reconciliation efforts. Alternatively, Nina may simply wait and use the one-year separation ground.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 11(1)(c), s. 11(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Bars to Divorce — Condonation DA s.11(1)(c)

text:
During divorce proceedings, Yvonne asks her lawyer whether the court can include an order dividing the marital home and investment accounts as part of the divorce judgment in the Ontario Superior Court of Justice. Her lawyer must advise her about the correct legal framework.

Can an Ontario court divide property between spouses as part of a divorce proceeding under the Divorce Act?
opts: Yes — the Divorce Act gives Ontario courts complete authority to divide all property between spouses as part of the divorce judgment.

No — property division is exclusively a provincial matter governed by the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3; the Divorce Act does not give courts jurisdiction over property division; Yvonne must pursue equalization of net family property under the FLA in the same or a separate proceeding.
Yes — courts in divorce proceedings may divide property if both parties consent to the court making a property order.
No — property can only be divided by a separate Ontario Court of Justice proceeding; the Superior Court of Justice has no jurisdiction over property in divorce cases.
expl: The Divorce Act is federal legislation and deals with corollary relief: spousal support (s.15.2), child support (s.15.1), and parenting orders (s.16). Property division is not included in the Divorce Act's corollary relief regime. Property division between spouses in Ontario is governed exclusively by the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part I (equalization of net family property). While the same Superior Court of Justice judge may hear both the divorce and the FLA property claim simultaneously, the property claim is brought under the FLA, not the Divorce Act. The court's jurisdiction over property rests on provincial law, not the federal divorce statute.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), ss. 15.1, 15.2, 16; Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part I
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Divorce Act Does Not Divide Property — Provincial FLA Governs

text:
Patrick and Vivienne are spouses who separated after Patrick admitted to an extramarital affair. After the affair came to light, the parties reconciled and resumed cohabitation for four months. The reconciliation broke down and they separated again. Patrick now applies for divorce on the basis of one year's separation. Vivienne opposes the divorce, arguing two bars apply: first, that the parties colluded by agreeing to resume cohabitation 'on paper only' to satisfy Patrick's family while privately planning to separate again; second, that she had condoned Patrick's conduct by resuming cohabitation. The judge must determine whether either bar applies.

Which of the following correctly identifies whether collusion or condonation operates as a bar to divorce on these facts?
opts: Both collusion and condonation bar the divorce: the resumed cohabitation was agreed-upon and therefore constitutes collusion; and Vivienne condoned the affair by resuming cohabitation, which prevents Patrick from relying on it as grounds.
Collusion bars the divorce because the parties agreed to separate; agreements to cooperate in the divorce process constitute collusion under DA s.11(1)(a).

Condonation applies because Vivienne resumed cohabitation after learning of the affair, but condonation under DA s.11(1)(c) is not a bar to a separation-based divorce; since Patrick applies on the ground of one year's separation — not on the ground of adultery — neither condonation of the affair nor the alleged collusion bars the divorce.

Collusion requires fabrication of evidence; since no evidence was fabricated here, collusion cannot apply; however, condonation of the affair by resuming cohabitation is a complete bar to the divorce even where the applicant relies on one year's separation.
expl: Divorce Act s.11(1) requires the court to satisfy itself that collusion has not occurred (s.11(1)(a)), that reasonable arrangements have been made for children (s.11(1)(b)), and where the ground is adultery or physical/mental cruelty, that there has been no condonation or connivance (s.11(1)(c)). The critical point on these facts is that Patrick is applying for divorce on the ground of one year's separation under DA s.8(2)(a), not on the ground of adultery. Condonation under s.11(1)(c) bars divorce only where the ground relied upon is adultery or cruelty — it does not bar a separation-based divorce. As for collusion: DA s.11(4) defines collusion as an agreement or conspiracy to subvert the administration of justice — such as fabricating or suppressing evidence of a ground for divorce or conniving at a matrimonial offence. Agreeing to separate, or even cooperating in the divorce process, is expressly not collusion under s.11(4). The parties' agreement to cooperate in the separation does not constitute collusion. Patrick's application on the separation ground is not barred by either collusion or condonation.
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Bars to Divorce — DA s.11

text:
Thomas and Sandra separated on January 1, 2025. After two months apart, they attempted to reconcile and resumed cohabitation for 60 days in March–April 2025 before separating again. They have lived continuously separate and apart since May 1, 2025. Thomas files for divorce on January 15, 2026, relying on marriage breakdown by reason of separation. Sandra argues that the reconciliation attempt restarted the one-year clock and the divorce cannot be granted until May 2026.

Under the Divorce Act, has Thomas established the one-year separation period sufficient for divorce by January 15, 2026?
opts: No — any resumption of cohabitation, however brief, breaks the separation period and restarts the one-year clock from the date of re-separation.

Yes — under DA s.8(2)(a), marriage breakdown by one-year separation is established; and under DA s.8(3), a cohabitation for reconciliation purposes of 90 days or less does not interrupt the one-year period, which runs from the original January 1, 2025 separation date.
No — Thomas must prove not only the separation period but also that the marriage is irretrievably broken by independent evidence beyond the period of separation.
Yes — but Thomas must first obtain the court's finding that the reconciliation attempt was genuine before the separation period can be counted continuously.
expl: Under Divorce Act s.8(2)(a), marriage breakdown is established by the spouses having lived separate and apart for at least one year immediately preceding the determination of the divorce application. Under DA s.8(3), a period or periods of resumed cohabitation totalling not more than 90 days and undertaken for the purpose of reconciliation do not count as a break in the separation period. Thomas and Sandra's 60-day reconciliation attempt falls within the 90-day exception. The one-year separation period therefore runs unbroken from January 1, 2025; by January 15, 2026, more than one year has elapsed. Sandra's argument is wrong because she fails to account for the s.8(3) reconciliation exception. No independent proof of irretrievable breakdown is required beyond the statutory separation period.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 8(1), s. 8(2)(a)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Grounds for Divorce — DA s.8

text:
Abbas and Farida separated in October 2023. Abbas has been ordinarily resident in Ontario continuously since October 2023. Farida moved to British Columbia in October 2023 and has been ordinarily resident there since that date. In October 2024 — exactly one year after each spouse moved to their respective province — Abbas commences divorce proceedings in Ontario, and Farida independently commences divorce proceedings in British Columbia on the same day. Each spouse's proceeding is validly filed and served. Abbas's Ontario lawyer and Farida's BC lawyer each argue that their province's court has the only proper jurisdiction.

Under the Divorce Act, which court has jurisdiction to hear the divorce, and what mechanism governs this conflict?
opts: Only Ontario has jurisdiction because Abbas filed his application first; the race-to-file rule determines which province governs when both spouses meet the one-year requirement simultaneously.
Neither court has jurisdiction because proceedings were filed simultaneously in two provinces; the parties must agree on a single province before either court may proceed.

Under DA s.3(1), both Ontario and British Columbia have concurrent jurisdiction because each spouse has been ordinarily resident in their respective province for at least one year immediately before proceedings were commenced; under DA s.3(2), where proceedings are commenced in two provinces on the same day, the two courts shall meet or communicate to determine which court shall have jurisdiction, and the other court's proceedings are stayed.
Only British Columbia has jurisdiction because the Divorce Act gives priority to the province where the respondent spouse resides when both spouses have satisfied the one-year requirement.
expl: Under Divorce Act s.3(1), a court in a province has jurisdiction where either spouse has been ordinarily resident in that province for at least one year immediately preceding the commencement of proceedings. Both Abbas (Ontario, since October 2023) and Farida (British Columbia, since October 2023) have each met the one-year requirement as of October 2024. This creates concurrent jurisdiction. DA s.3(2) addresses the concurrent jurisdiction conflict: where proceedings on the same petition are commenced in two provinces on the same day, both courts shall consult with one another, and the court in the province that is more appropriate to determine the corollary relief issues shall exercise jurisdiction and the other court's proceeding is stayed. The transfer and stay mechanism — not a race-to-file rule, not a respondent-residence priority, and not an impasse — is the statutory resolution. Both courts have jurisdiction; the DA provides a defined mechanism to resolve the conflict rather than leaving it to the parties.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 3(1), s. 3(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Jurisdiction — Ordinarily Resident One Year DA s.3

text:
Catherine and Raymond have been separated for 18 months. They agree on all issues — property, support, and parenting — and have signed a comprehensive separation agreement. Catherine asks her lawyer whether the divorce can be obtained without attending court, and whether the separation agreement itself is sufficient to dissolve the marriage without any further step. Raymond's lawyer mentions that there are barriers to remarriage concerns that must be addressed before the court will grant the divorce.

Which of the following accurately describes the procedure by which Catherine and Raymond can obtain an uncontested divorce in Ontario, and what additional requirement must be satisfied?
opts: The separation agreement constitutes a valid divorce once signed by both parties and filed with the court registrar; no judicial order is required for an agreed divorce.

They may use the joint application for divorce under Family Law Rules r. 36; both are co-applicants; the divorce proceeds on affidavit evidence reviewed by a judge in chambers without a court appearance; the applicant must also file an affidavit confirming there are no barriers to remarriage as required by DA s.21.1.
They must each file separate divorce applications because each spouse must independently satisfy the court of their own entitlement to the divorce; joint applications are not permitted.
An uncontested divorce still requires both parties to attend a brief court hearing before a judge; affidavit-only procedure is available only for marriages of less than five years.
expl: Ontario's Family Law Rules r. 36 provides for a joint application for divorce where both spouses consent and agree on all issues. Both are named as co-applicants and the matter proceeds on affidavit evidence reviewed by a judge in chambers — without any court appearance. This is commonly called a 'desk order divorce.' Critically, DA s.21.1 imposes an obligation on the applicant to file an affidavit confirming that they are not aware of any barriers to the other party's remarriage that they have not taken reasonable steps to remove. This requirement reflects the legislature's sensitivity to religious barriers (e.g., a get under Jewish law). The separation agreement itself does not dissolve the marriage — only a court order does. The judge reviews the materials to ensure compliance with the Divorce Act, including bar requirements under DA s.11.
caseRef: Family Law Rules, O. Reg. 114/99, r. 36; Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 8
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Uncontested Divorce — Joint Application Procedure

text:
Following a 6-year marriage with no children, a spousal support order is made in favour of Wendy, a 35-year-old with a nursing diploma. She stopped working during the marriage to accompany her husband on overseas assignments. It is now 3 years post-separation. She has not returned to work and has not sought employment, claiming the job market is difficult.

What obligation does the Divorce Act place on Wendy as the support recipient in relation to self-sufficiency?
opts: Wendy has no obligation to seek employment or become self-sufficient; spousal support is payable as long as the original order is in place and is not varied.

Under DA s.15.2(6)(d), a support order must promote economic self-sufficiency of each spouse within a reasonable period, having regard to the circumstances; Wendy has an obligation to take reasonable steps to achieve self-sufficiency, and her failure to seek employment as a nurse may be grounds for reducing or terminating support.
Wendy must achieve complete financial independence within one year of the support order or the order automatically terminates.
Wendy's obligation to seek work is irrelevant because the support order is already in place; the court cannot consider self-sufficiency in a variation application.
expl: Divorce Act s.15.2(6)(d) requires that a spousal support order 'insofar as practicable, promote the economic self-sufficiency of each spouse within a reasonable period of time.' This provision reflects the policy that spousal support is intended to address economic disadvantage, not to create permanent dependency. A recipient spouse with marketable skills (here, a nursing diploma) and who is of working age has an obligation to make reasonable efforts toward self-sufficiency. Courts may consider a recipient's failure to take reasonable steps toward employment when addressing duration and amount on a variation application under DA s.17.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(6)(d)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Self-Sufficiency Obligation — DA s.15.2(6)(d)

text:
A divorce judgment was granted five years ago. The corollary relief order required Hamid to pay spousal support of $2,500/month indefinitely. Since then, Hamid has remarried and had two new children, and his expenses have increased significantly. He earns the same income as at the time of the order. He seeks a variation of the spousal support order, arguing that his new family obligations constitute a material change.

Does Hamid's remarriage and new family obligations constitute a material change warranting variation of the corollary relief order under DA s.17?
opts: Yes — remarriage and new children automatically constitute a material change requiring immediate reduction of all pre-existing support obligations.
No — a payor's voluntary decision to remarry and have more children cannot constitute a material change because the financial consequences were entirely foreseeable.

Possibly — under DA s.17, a material change is one that, had it existed at the time of the original order, would have led to a different result; while a payor's new family obligations are a relevant change in circumstances, courts typically give less weight to voluntarily assumed new obligations; Hamid's unchanged income limits the persuasiveness of his variation application.
Yes — under DA s.17, any change in personal circumstances automatically constitutes a material change and the court must vary the order.
expl: Under Divorce Act s.17(4.1), a court may vary a spousal support order where there has been a change in the condition, means, needs, or other circumstances of either former spouse since the making of the order. The test for material change requires that the change, had it been known at the time, would likely have resulted in a different order (Leskun v. Leskun, [2006] 1 SCR 920). Courts do consider new family obligations as a relevant change but apply considerable scrutiny to voluntarily assumed new financial burdens. Because Hamid's income has not changed, and his new obligations were voluntarily incurred, courts are likely to give limited weight to this as a basis for reduction, particularly where the first family's interests may be prejudiced.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17(4.1); Leskun v. Leskun, [2006] 1 SCR 920
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Divorce and Corollary Relief | Variation of Corollary Relief — DA s.17

text:
Omar and Leila divorced after a 9-year marriage. They have no children. Omar earned $160,000 per year as a lawyer throughout the marriage; Leila earned $65,000 as a graphic designer and continued working throughout the marriage. Leila did not take any career steps back for the marriage. Their separation agreement is silent on support. Leila now seeks spousal support.

Based on these facts, which ground of spousal support entitlement, if any, is most likely available to Leila?
opts: Compensatory — because Omar earns more than Leila, creating an economic imbalance attributable to the marriage.
Non-compensatory — because Leila has a lower income and therefore a financial need relative to Omar.

Leila faces difficulty establishing any of the three entitlement grounds: there is no compensatory basis (she suffered no career disadvantage), likely insufficient non-compensatory basis (she has employment and income), and no contractual basis (the agreement is silent); a court would likely find no entitlement.
Contractual — the separation agreement creates an implied obligation to pay support.
expl: The three recognized grounds for spousal support entitlement are: (1) compensatory — the claimant suffered an economic disadvantage from the marriage and its breakdown (Moge v. Moge, [1992] 3 SCR 813); (2) non-compensatory — the claimant has a need arising from the relationship of mutual interdependence (Bracklow v. Bracklow, [1999] 1 SCR 420); and (3) contractual. Leila has no compensatory claim (she maintained her career without sacrifice). Her non-compensatory claim is weak: she has steady employment and income of $65,000, so there is no significant need arising from marriage breakdown. The separation agreement creates no contractual entitlement. A court would likely find no entitlement on these facts.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(6); Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 33(8); Moge v. Moge, [1992] 3 SCR 813; Bracklow v. Bracklow, [1999] 1 SCR 420
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Spousal Support | Entitlement — Three Grounds Compensatory Non-Compensatory Contractual

text:
Violet and Conrad separated on January 1, 2022, without applying for divorce. As of February 1, 2026 — more than four years post-separation — Violet has not commenced any spousal support proceedings. She now wishes to claim spousal support under the Family Law Act. Conrad argues her claim is statute-barred.

What limitation period governs Violet's spousal support claim under the Family Law Act, and is her claim likely out of time?
opts: The two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 bars Violet's claim; she had two years from the date of separation to commence proceedings.
There is no limitation period for spousal support claims; Violet may claim at any time.

Under the Family Law Act, a claim for spousal support (Part III) is subject to the general two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002, running from the date the claim was discovered; however, the date of discovery may be later than separation if Violet did not know or could not reasonably have known of her entitlement earlier.
The limitation period for FLA spousal support is six years from the date of separation; Violet's claim is not yet out of time.
expl: Spousal support claims under the Family Law Act Part III are subject to the Limitations Act, 2002, which imposes a basic two-year limitation period running from the date the claim was discovered (s.4 of the Limitations Act, 2002). The discovery date may differ from the separation date — a claim is discovered when the person knew or ought to have known that the harm, loss, or damage occurred, and that a proceeding against the appropriate person would be an appropriate means to seek remedy. In practice, for spousal support, courts typically hold that the claim is discoverable from separation or shortly thereafter. If Violet's claim was discoverable in early 2022, it may be statute-barred by early 2024, four years later. Note: claims under the Divorce Act as corollary relief in divorce proceedings are not subject to this limitation period while divorce proceedings are pending.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part III; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Spousal Support | Limitation Period — Spousal Support FLA vs Divorce Act

text:
Heather and Marcus separated after 14 years of marriage. During the marriage, Heather reduced her hours from full-time to part-time to care for their two children, earning $30,000/year less than her former full-time income. At the time of separation, she has also been diagnosed with a debilitating autoimmune disease unrelated to the marriage, which further limits her ability to work. She currently earns $20,000/year; Marcus earns $150,000. She claims both compensatory and non-compensatory spousal support. Marcus argues she can only receive support on one ground.

Under FLA s.33(8) and Divorce Act s.15.2(6), may Heather claim spousal support on both compensatory and non-compensatory grounds simultaneously?
opts: No — courts must identify a single ground of entitlement and award support on that basis only; mixing grounds is not permitted.
Yes — but only the non-compensatory (need-based) ground applies because Heather's illness is the dominant cause of her current inability to earn income.

Yes — both compensatory and non-compensatory grounds may simultaneously support entitlement; the compensatory basis addresses the economic disadvantage caused by career reduction for child care (Moge), while the non-compensatory basis addresses the need arising from illness independent of the marriage (Bracklow); amount and duration may reflect both grounds.
No — Heather must elect between compensatory or non-compensatory support; once she elects, she cannot claim on the other basis.
expl: Spousal support entitlement under the Divorce Act s.15.2(6) and the Family Law Act s.33(8) is not mutually exclusive between compensatory and non-compensatory grounds. In Bracklow v Bracklow, [1999] 1 SCR 420, the Supreme Court confirmed that a claimant may have entitlement on both grounds simultaneously. The compensatory basis (Moge v Moge, [1992] 3 SCR 813; FLA s.33(8)(a)/(b)) addresses the economic disadvantage caused by the marriage — here, Heather's career reduction to care for children. The non-compensatory basis (Bracklow; FLA s.33(8)(f)/(g)) addresses need arising from the breakdown of a relationship of mutual interdependency, including illness not caused by the marriage. Courts may award support that reflects both grounds — for example, a higher quantum or longer duration than either ground alone would justify. The amount and duration of support may be shaped by the interaction of both grounds.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 33(8); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(6); Moge v Moge, [1992] 3 SCR 813; Bracklow v Bracklow, [1999] 1 SCR 420
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Spousal Support | Entitlement — Compensatory and Non-Compensatory Grounds

text:
A spousal support order was made two years ago. The order includes the following clause: 'This order shall be subject to review on January 1, 2027 without the need to demonstrate a material change in circumstances.' The husband now seeks to terminate support, arguing he need not wait for the review date and can bring a variation application at any time.

What is the legal distinction between a review order and a variation application for spousal support, and how does the review clause affect the husband's ability to bring a variation application?
opts: A review order and a variation application are legally identical; both require a material change in circumstances.

A review order allows the court to revisit support at the specified date without requiring the applicant to prove a material change in circumstances; a variation application under DA s.17 always requires proof of a material change; however, a review clause does not necessarily preclude a variation application where a material change has occurred before the review date.
A review clause permanently replaces the variation regime; the husband has no right to bring any application before the review date.
Review orders are not permitted under the Divorce Act; only variation applications are legally recognized.
expl: A review order is a special kind of order that expressly allows the court to revisit the support obligation at a specified future date without the moving party needing to prove a material change in circumstances. This contrasts with a variation application under DA s.17, which requires the applicant to demonstrate a material change in the condition, means, needs, or circumstances of either spouse since the last order. A review clause does not necessarily bar a s.17 variation application before the review date — if a material change has occurred (e.g., significant income change, loss of employment), the husband may still bring a variation application. The review clause merely establishes an additional mechanism for revisiting support at the specified date without a material change threshold.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17; Fisher v. Fisher, 2008 ONCA 11
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Spousal Support | Review vs Variation — Distinct Mechanisms

text:
A spousal support order in favour of Priya was made 3 years ago following a 7-year marriage with no children. Priya has a bachelor's degree in commerce. She has made no effort to find employment, claiming personal preference for not working. Her former husband Anil, who pays $2,000/month in support, brings a variation application arguing that Priya could be self-sufficient if she made reasonable efforts to find work.

What is the legal significance of Priya's failure to pursue self-sufficiency in the context of Anil's variation application?
opts: Priya has no obligation to seek employment; once an order is made, the recipient's choices about employment are irrelevant.

A recipient spouse's failure to take reasonable steps toward self-sufficiency when capable of employment is a relevant factor on a variation application; courts may impute income to Priya based on her earning capacity and reduce or terminate support on that basis.
The court can only impute income to a payor, not to a recipient spouse.
Priya's support order cannot be varied because it was made less than five years ago.
expl: Divorce Act s.15.2(6)(d) requires support orders to promote the economic self-sufficiency of each spouse within a reasonable period. FLA s.33(8)(h) similarly requires consideration of a spouse's ability to become self-sufficient. Courts have consistently held that a recipient who is capable of employment but makes no effort to become self-sufficient may have income imputed to them on a variation application, and support may be reduced or terminated. Three years after separation from a 7-year marriage with no children, a degree-holder who makes no employment effort is a strong candidate for income imputation and variation of the support order.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(6)(d); Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 33(8)(h)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Spousal Support | Self-Sufficiency and Variation — DA s.15.2(6)(d)

text:
Noah turned 18 last September and began a full-time four-year engineering degree program in September. He lives on campus during the school year and returns to his mother's home during breaks. He has no employment income. His parents divorced when he was 12; the existing order requires his father to pay table-amount child support of $1,200/month based on the father's income at the time. The father brings a motion to terminate the child support order on the basis that Noah is now an adult. The mother cross-moves to vary the order to address Noah's current university costs of $21,500/year.

How should the court approach child support for Noah as a full-time university student over the age of majority?
opts: Child support terminates automatically at age 18; the father's motion to terminate succeeds and no variation is required.
The existing $1,200/month table-amount order continues unchanged; the table amount is the mandatory amount for all children regardless of age.

Under FCSG s.3(2)(b) and DA s.2(1), Noah remains a 'child of the marriage' unable to withdraw from parental charge due to full-time studies; the table amount is not automatically applied; the court must assess the appropriateness of the table amount or an alternative amount based on Noah's condition, means, needs, and circumstances and each parent's financial ability to contribute — typically the actual post-secondary costs apportioned proportionately between parents.
Support for adult children is capped at a maximum of $1,000/month regardless of actual expenses or the table amount.
expl: Under Divorce Act s.2(1), a 'child of the marriage' includes an adult child unable to withdraw from parental charge by reason of, among other things, pursuit of reasonably required education — which courts consistently apply to full-time post-secondary studies. Termination at age 18 is therefore incorrect. Under FCSG s.3(2)(b), once a child reaches the age of majority, the mandatory table-amount rule under s.3(1) no longer applies automatically; the court must consider whether the table amount is appropriate having regard to the child's condition, means, needs, and circumstances and each parent's ability to contribute. For a university student living away from home, courts regularly use the student's actual post-secondary costs as the starting point, apportion them between the parents proportionate to their incomes, and may also maintain a contribution to the residential parent's household costs. The table amount is one option — not the only one.
caseRef: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 3(2)(b); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 2(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Adult Child Support — FCSG s.3(2)(b)

text:
Diane earns $120,000 per year and her former husband Carl earns $80,000 per year. Their daughter participates in competitive gymnastics with annual costs of $6,000 and receives a $600 athletic bursary from her school board applied directly toward gymnastics. The court has determined the gymnastics cost qualifies as an extraordinary extracurricular expense under FCSG s.7(1)(f). Carl acknowledges s.7 applies but argues he should pay the same dollar amount as Diane since both are parents.

What amount does Carl owe under FCSG s.7(2) after accounting for the bursary, given the parties' respective incomes?
opts: Carl pays $2,400 — half of the net $4,800 expense after deducting the bursary, because parents split s.7 expenses equally.
Carl pays $3,000 — half of the gross $6,000 expense; the bursary belongs to the child and cannot reduce either parent's obligation.

Carl pays $2,160 — under FCSG s.7(2), the bursary of $600 is first deducted from the $6,000 gross cost leaving $5,400 net, which is then apportioned 60/40 in proportion to the parties' incomes; Carl's 40% share of $5,400 is $2,160.
Diane pays the full $5,400 net expense because she earns more than Carl; Carl owes nothing.
expl: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.7(2) provides that s.7 expenses are apportioned in proportion to each parent's income after deducting any contribution from the child. Step 1: Deduct the child's bursary — $6,000 minus $600 = $5,400 net expense. Step 2: Determine income proportions — Diane's $120,000 is 60% of the combined $200,000; Carl's $80,000 is 40%. Step 3: Apply proportions — Diane pays $3,240 (60%); Carl pays $2,160 (40%). The s.7(2) deduction of the child's contribution before apportionment is mandatory. The 50/50 split (option A) misapplies the proportionality rule. Ignoring the bursary deduction (option B) skips the mandatory first step. The higher earner bearing all costs (option D) misreads 'proportionate' as 'full obligation.'
caseRef: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 7(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Section 7 Expenses — Apportionment and Child Contribution

text:
Aaron and Bianca separated six years ago. A child support order was made at that time based on Aaron's income of $60,000. Aaron's income has since increased to $100,000, but he never voluntarily disclosed this to Bianca. Bianca learned of Aaron's income increase only recently and now brings a variation application seeking retroactive child support for the past four years.

Under the framework in DBS v SRG, [2006] 2 SCR 231, what factors govern whether and how far back a retroactive child support award should be made?
opts: Retroactive child support is not available; child support can only be increased from the date of the variation application.

Under DBS v SRG, a court may award retroactive child support, taking into account: the reason for the recipient's delay in seeking support; the conduct of the payor (including non-disclosure of income); the circumstances of the child; and hardship to the payor from retroactive payment.
Retroactive child support is automatically awarded for the full limitation period of six years before the application.
Retroactive child support may only be awarded for one year prior to the application, with no exceptions.
expl: In DBS v SRG, [2006] 2 SCR 231, the Supreme Court of Canada established the analytical framework for retroactive child support. The four factors are: (1) whether the recipient had a reasonable excuse for the delay in bringing the application; (2) whether the payor engaged in blameworthy conduct (such as non-disclosure of income increases); (3) the circumstances of the child and whether they will benefit from a retroactive award; and (4) whether the payor would suffer hardship from the retroactive award. The Court also established a general 'rule of thumb' that retroactive awards should not generally extend more than three years before the date of the formal claim, but this may be exceeded where there is payor misconduct (such as non-disclosure). Aaron's consistent non-disclosure of income is blameworthy conduct supporting a more generous retroactive award.
caseRef: DBS v SRG; LJW v TAR; Henry v Henry; Hiemstra v Hiemstra, [2006] 2 SCR 231
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Retroactive Child Support — DBS v SRG

text:
Dr. Sharma earns $500,000 per year as a surgeon. He and his former spouse have two children aged 8 and 11 who live primarily with the mother. Under the Ontario Schedule I table, the table amount for a $150,000 income with two children is $1,980/month. Dr. Sharma argues that the court must award the table amount at the $150,000 benchmark since the table does not extend beyond $150,000. The mother argues the court should award more.

How does FCSG s.4 address child support where the payor's annual income exceeds $150,000?
opts: The table amount at $150,000 is the maximum; a payor earning more than $150,000 pays no more than the table amount at that income level.

Under FCSG s.4, where the payor's income exceeds $150,000, the court has two options: (a) award the table amount for $150,000 plus an additional amount the court considers appropriate; or (b) if the table amount for $150,000 does not appropriately reflect the children's needs and the payor's ability to pay, award any amount it considers appropriate.
For income above $150,000, child support is automatically capped at 2% of income regardless of the table amount.
The court must apply the full proportional table formula by extrapolating beyond $150,000 as if the table extended indefinitely.
expl: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.4 provides that where the payor's income exceeds $150,000, the court shall award: (a) the amount shown in the table for the $150,000 income level, plus an additional amount the court considers appropriate; or (b) if the table amount for $150,000 does not appropriately reflect the condition, means, needs, and circumstances of the children and the payor's ability to pay, any amount that the court considers appropriate. The SSAG for high-income payors recognizes that the table amounts for lower incomes may not adequately reflect what is appropriate when the payor has very high means. In Dr. Sharma's case, a court would likely award substantially more than the $1,980 table amount for $150,000.
caseRef: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 4
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | High-Income Payor — FCSG s.4

text:
A parenting order grants the mother 210 overnights per year and the father 155 overnights per year with their one child. The father argues that because he has the child for approximately 42% of overnights, FCSG s.9 applies and he should pay a reduced amount of child support rather than the full table amount. The mother argues the full table amount applies because s.9 should be calculated differently.

For the purpose of FCSG s.9, how is the 40% threshold calculated, and does the father meet it?
opts: The 40% threshold is calculated based only on daytime hours, not overnights; 155 overnights is insufficient without counting daytime hours.

The 40% threshold under FCSG s.9 is measured over the course of a year; a parent who exercises parenting time for at least 40% of the time (approximately 146 days or more out of 365) meets the threshold; 155 overnights represents approximately 42% and satisfies the threshold.
The 40% threshold requires exactly 50/50 parenting time; anything less requires payment of the full table amount.
FCSG s.9 applies only if the child sleeps at each parent's home an equal number of nights; at 155 vs 210 overnights, s.9 is inapplicable.
expl: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.9 applies where 'a spouse exercises parenting time with a child for not less than 40 percent of the time over the course of a year.' The threshold is 40% — not 50%. Courts typically calculate the percentage based on the total number of days (or nights) in a year attributable to each parent. At 155 overnights out of 365 (approximately 42.5%), the father meets the s.9 threshold, and the court must apply the three-factor analysis from Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, [2005] 3 SCR 217 rather than simply imposing the full table amount. The calculation is not limited to overnights if there is significant daytime-only time, but overnights are the most common metric used.
caseRef: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 9; Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, [2005] 3 SCR 217
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Shared Parenting 40% Threshold — FCSG s.9

text:
In 1995, a court order required Kwame to pay $800/month in child support to his former spouse Amara. Under that order, Kwame deducted the payments from his income and Amara included them as taxable income — consistent with the rules at the time. In 2022, the parties returned to court and the order was varied to increase child support to $1,400/month due to Kwame's increased income. Kwame's accountant now advises him that the 1995 order predates the 1997 amendments so he may continue to deduct all child support payments under the old regime. Amara's accountant advises her to continue including all payments as income.

Which tax regime applies to Kwame's child support payments following the 2022 variation order?
opts: The pre-1997 regime (deductible by payor, taxable to recipient) continues to govern all payments because the original order was made in 1995, before the May 1997 amendments; the variation does not affect the tax treatment.

Following the 2022 variation order, a 'commencement day' is triggered under the Income Tax Act; from that date, all child support payments — including the base amount — are no longer deductible by Kwame and are no longer taxable income to Amara.

Only the incremental $600/month increase is tax-neutral under the post-1997 regime; the original $800/month base amount remains deductible by Kwame and taxable to Amara under the grandfathered 1995 order.
The 2022 variation is a new order and creates a new tax regime only for future orders; Kwame may elect which regime applies by filing an election with CRA within 60 days of the variation.
expl: The Income Tax Act establishes the concept of a 'commencement day' to determine which tax regime applies to child support payments. Under ITA s.56.1(4) and s.60.1(4), a commencement day is triggered when a pre-May 1997 order or agreement is varied after April 1997 — specifically, the commencement day is the effective date of the variation. Once a commencement day arises, all child support amounts payable under the agreement or order on and after that day fall under the post-1997 tax-neutral regime: the payments are not deductible by the payor (ITA s.60(b)) and not includible as income by the recipient (ITA s.56(1)(b)). The variation does not split the treatment between old and new amounts — the commencement day converts the entire ongoing child support obligation to the tax-neutral regime. Both accountants' advice is incorrect after the 2022 variation.
caseRef: Income Tax Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 1 (5th Supp.), ss. 56(1)(b), 56.1(4), 60(b), 60.1(4) (Commencement Day rules)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Child Support | Tax Treatment of Child Support Payments

text:
In contested parenting proceedings under the Divorce Act, the father's counsel argues that because the children (ages 6 and 10) have expressed a clear preference to live primarily with the father, the court must order that arrangement. The children's preference has been communicated through the Office of the Children's Lawyer. The mother argues that the children's views are only one of 16 factors and cannot be determinative.

Under DA s.16(3), what is the weight to be given to the children's expressed preferences in a parenting determination?
opts: The children's expressed preference is determinative; if the children clearly prefer one parent, the court must order accordingly.

The children's expressed preference is one of 16 enumerated factors in DA s.16(3) and must be considered by the court; however, it is not determinative — the court must weigh it against all other best interests factors, and greater weight is given to the preferences of older children who can articulate reasons for their preference.
Children under 12 have no legal standing to express a preference in parenting proceedings; their views are inadmissible.
The children's preference must be followed by the court unless there is evidence of parental alienation, in which case it is ignored entirely.
expl: Divorce Act s.16(3)(e) lists 'the child's views and preferences, giving due weight to the child's age and maturity' as one of the 16 enumerated best interests factors. Children's views must be considered but are not determinative. Courts give increasing weight to a child's preference as the child matures — the views of a well-reasoned 10-year-old carry more weight than those of a 4-year-old. At age 6 and 10, the preferences are relevant and to be considered, but must be weighed against all other factors including the children's safety, stability, each parent's ability to care for the children, and family violence. The court cannot simply adopt the children's stated preference without examining the full best interests picture.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 16(3)(e) (as amended S.C. 2019, c. 16)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Best Interests — Children's Views and Preferences DA s.16(3)(e)

text:
A parenting order was made by an Australian court two years ago, granting primary parenting time to the father. The mother has since relocated to Ontario with the children without the father's consent or court approval. The father applies to an Ontario court to recognize and enforce the Australian parenting order. The mother argues that the Ontario court should make its own fresh determination of parenting based solely on current circumstances in Ontario.

How does an Ontario court approach an application to recognize and enforce a foreign parenting order where the children are now resident in Ontario?
opts: Ontario courts automatically refuse to enforce foreign parenting orders; the father must obtain a new Ontario order from scratch.

Ontario courts recognize and enforce foreign parenting orders as a matter of international comity, regardless of any change in circumstances, and must restore the children to the jurisdiction of the original order without any best interests analysis.

Ontario courts may recognize a foreign parenting order and return the children to the jurisdiction of the original order; the court's primary consideration remains the best interests of the child; however, the wrongful removal of children to Ontario without the other parent's consent is a relevant factor and the court may rely on the Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction (Hague Convention) principles where applicable.
Foreign parenting orders have no legal force in Ontario; the mother's relocation with the children to Ontario automatically creates Ontario jurisdiction and the foreign order is void.
expl: Ontario courts may recognize foreign parenting orders under principles of comity and, where applicable, the Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction (Hague Convention), implemented in Ontario through the Children's Law Reform Act. Where a child has been wrongfully removed from the country of habitual residence, the Ontario court may order the return of the child to that jurisdiction, unless doing so would expose the child to a grave risk of harm. The best interests of the child remain the overarching consideration, but wrongful removal without consent is treated as highly relevant. Australia and Canada are both signatories to the Hague Convention on international child abduction.
caseRef: Children's Law Reform Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.12; Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction (Hague Convention), implemented in Ontario
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Recognition of Foreign Parenting Orders — Hague Convention

text:
Following a bitter divorce, the mother restricts the maternal grandparents from seeing their two grandchildren, aged 6 and 9. The grandparents have had a close and continuous relationship with the children throughout their lives and provided substantial childcare during the marriage. The grandparents now apply for contact in Ontario court proceedings that arise under the CLRA — not as part of a pending Divorce Act proceeding. The mother argues: (1) grandparents have no standing to apply for contact; and (2) even if they do, they should seek the order under the Divorce Act, not the CLRA.

Under Ontario law for non-divorce proceedings, do the grandparents have standing to apply for a contact order, which statute governs, and what test applies?
opts: Grandparents have standing under the Divorce Act s.16.1(2) only; since this is not a divorce proceeding, they have no standing and cannot bring an application at all.

Grandparents have an automatic right to contact with grandchildren under Ontario law; the court must grant contact without a best interests assessment unless the mother proves the children would be harmed by contact.

Under CLRA s.21(1), any person — including grandparents — may apply to court for a contact order in non-divorce proceedings; the governing test is the best interests of the child with no presumptive right to grandparent contact; the CLRA (not the DA) applies when proceedings are not brought under the Divorce Act.
Grandparents may only apply for contact under the CLRA if the children's parents are both deceased or incapacitated.
expl: The Children's Law Reform Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.12, s.21(1) provides that 'any person' may apply to the court for a contact order with a child. This expressly includes grandparents and other third parties such as step-parents or close family friends. Standing is not limited to parents. The governing test is the best interests of the child under CLRA s.24; there is no presumptive right to contact — the court must affirmatively conclude that contact serves the child's interests. The CLRA applies to non-divorce proceedings; in a Divorce Act proceeding, DA s.16.1(2) provides the equivalent mechanism for third-party contact applications. The mother's argument that the Divorce Act should apply conflates two distinct statutory regimes — the CLRA governs non-divorce parenting proceedings in Ontario courts. The grandparents correctly filed under the CLRA.
caseRef: Children's Law Reform Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.12, s. 21(1), s. 24; Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 16.1(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Grandparent Contact — CLRA s.21 vs DA s.16.1(2)

text:
Philip and Nadia separated four months ago. Since separation, the children (aged 5 and 8) have lived primarily with Nadia during the week, with Philip having parenting time every weekend and one overnight mid-week. Philip now brings an interim motion seeking equal week-on/week-off parenting time, arguing the children have a strong bond with him. Nadia opposes any change, noting the children are settled in their school routines and have shown no distress. Philip argues that, because the existing arrangement pre-dates any court order, the court is not bound by any 'status quo' and should order the parenting arrangement that is best on a full analysis.

How should the court approach Philip's interim motion for equal parenting time, and what is the legal significance of the post-separation de facto arrangement?
opts: Philip is correct — because there is no prior court order establishing a status quo, the court must conduct a full best interests analysis at the interim stage as if no arrangement exists, and must award equal parenting time as the presumptive starting point.
Courts must always award equal parenting time at the interim stage to avoid prejudicing either parent before trial.

Under DA s.16(1) and the recognized status quo principle, courts on interim applications apply the best interests standard but generally maintain the existing de facto post-separation arrangement to ensure stability and minimize disruption for the children pending trial; the absence of a prior court order does not negate the relevance of the de facto arrangement — it is the de facto arrangement, not a court order, that constitutes the relevant status quo.

Interim parenting orders under the Divorce Act can only maintain the arrangement that existed at the time of marriage breakdown; any post-separation arrangement agreed by the parties has no legal significance.
expl: Interim parenting orders are governed by the Divorce Act s.16(1), which authorizes the court to make parenting orders at any stage of the proceedings. At the interim stage, courts apply the best interests of the child standard (DA s.16(3)) but also recognize a distinct evidentiary limitation: the full factual record will not be available until trial. Courts therefore generally apply the status quo principle — maintaining the de facto post-separation arrangement that the children have adjusted to — unless there is a compelling reason to depart from it. The status quo for this purpose is the existing living arrangement since separation, not a previously court-ordered arrangement. Philip's argument conflates the absence of a prior court order with the absence of a relevant status quo. The de facto arrangement (Nadia primarily, Philip weekends plus one overnight) is exactly the status quo the court will generally preserve to protect the children's stability and school routines pending the full hearing.
