caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 16(1), s. 16(3) (as amended S.C. 2019, c. 16)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Interim Parenting Orders — Status Quo Principle

text:
Marie has primary parenting time (65%) of the two children. She provides proper 60-day notice of her intention to relocate from Montreal to Victoria for a significant employment opportunity that would double her salary. The father objects, having 35% parenting time. The father bears the burden of proof to show the relocation is not in the children's best interests. The court must apply the analytical framework for relocation.

Under the framework in Barendregt v Grebliunas, 2022 SCC 22, what is the correct approach to determining relocation disputes under DA s.16.9?
opts: The court should always deny relocation to preserve the child's existing relationship with the non-primary parent.

The court must apply the Barendregt framework: (1) the relocation must be made in good faith; (2) there must be reasonable and workable arrangements to preserve the child's relationship with the non-relocating parent; and (3) the decision must serve the best interests of the child — with the party bearing the burden under DA s.16.93 being required to address these considerations.
The primary consideration in relocation is always the economic benefit to the relocating parent; if the parent has a better job offer, relocation must be permitted.
Relocation must be denied unless the relocating parent can prove the move is necessary to escape family violence.
expl: In Barendregt v Grebliunas, 2022 SCC 22, the Supreme Court of Canada set out the analytical framework for relocation disputes under the 2021 Divorce Act amendments. The court must: (1) consider whether the relocation is proposed in good faith (not as a device to frustrate the other parent's parenting time); (2) assess whether there are reasonable and workable arrangements to preserve the non-relocating parent's relationship with the child; and (3) ultimately determine what outcome serves the child's best interests under DA s.16(3). The burden of proof (under DA ss.16.93-16.95) affects who must prove which elements. In Marie's case, with 65% primary parenting time, the father bears the burden of proving relocation is not in the children's best interests.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 16.9; Barendregt v Grebliunas, 2022 SCC 22
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Relocation — Barendregt v Grebliunas 2022 SCC 22

text:
Two years ago, an Ontario court made a final parenting order under the Divorce Act requiring all of the father's parenting time with his 4-year-old daughter to be supervised at a supervised access centre. The order was based on a child protection finding involving the father's alcohol dependence and two incidents of intoxicated driving with the child in the vehicle. The father has now been sober for 18 months, completed an addiction program, and holds a valid driver's licence. He applies to vary the order to unsupervised weekend parenting time. The mother opposes any change and argues: (i) the supervision order was a final order that should not be disturbed; and (ii) even if variation is possible, the father must establish that supervised access is now causing positive harm to the child, not merely that his circumstances have improved.

What is the correct legal test for the father's variation application, and which party's argument is legally accurate?
opts: The mother is correct on both points: final parenting orders cannot be varied without the other parent's consent, and the applicant must prove the existing order is actively harming the child.

The father may apply to vary under DA s.17; on a variation application, the court must make whatever order it would have made on a full best interests analysis today without any threshold requirement — variation is available on any change in circumstances.

Under DA s.17(5), the father must first establish a material change in the circumstances of the child or a parent since the final order — a change that, if it had existed at the time of the original order, would have led to a different result; sustained sobriety, addiction treatment, and licence restoration may satisfy this threshold; the test is material change, not proof that the existing order is actively harming the child.
A supervised parenting time order can only be varied if both parents consent and jointly apply to the court; a unilateral application by one parent is procedurally unavailable.
expl: Variation of a final parenting order made under the Divorce Act is governed by DA s.17(5), which requires the applicant to establish a material change in the circumstances of the child or a parent since the order was made — specifically, a change that, had it been known at the time of the original order, would have likely led to a different order. The test is not proof that the current order is actively harming the child (the mother's argument in option A misstates the legal test). Sustained sobriety (18 months), completion of addiction treatment, and restoration of a driver's licence are precisely the circumstances the court would have considered favourable at the time of the original order. Once the material change threshold is cleared, the court conducts a fresh best interests assessment (DA s.16(3)) and determines the appropriate parenting arrangement. Supervision orders are protective interim mechanisms, not permanent sanctions; they are subject to variation like any other parenting order. Consent of the other party is not required — DA s.17 is a unilateral remedy.
caseRef: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17(5), s. 16(3) (as amended S.C. 2019, c. 16)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Parenting Orders and Decision-Making | Supervised Parenting Time — Variation and Material Change

text:
A First Nations community proposes that a child who has been in CAS care be placed in 'customary care' with an extended family member within the community, pursuant to the community's traditional child-rearing practices. CAS is uncertain whether customary care arrangements can be recognized as a legal substitute for a CAS protection order.

Under the CYFSA, can a First Nations community's customary care arrangement for a child be legally recognized, and what effect does it have?
opts: Customary care has no legal recognition under the CYFSA; Indigenous communities must use the same child protection order framework as all other families.

Under CYFSA s.79, a customary care agreement between a First Nations, Inuit, or Métis community or organization and the child's family may be entered into for a child of that community; where a valid customary care arrangement is established, it operates as a recognized means of caring for the child, and may substitute for, or operate alongside, CAS involvement.
Customary care is available only for adult Indigenous persons, not for children.
Customary care is recognized but only after a formal adoption order has been made by the courts.
expl: CYFSA s.79 and related provisions recognize the right of First Nations, Inuit, and Métis communities and organizations to enter into customary care agreements for children of their communities. Customary care reflects the traditional child-rearing practices of Indigenous communities and is recognized as a distinct and culturally appropriate alternative to the standard child protection order framework. A child subject to a valid customary care arrangement may remain within the community and connected to their culture, family, and traditions. The CYFSA explicitly recognizes customary care as part of the framework responding to the over-representation of Indigenous children in the child welfare system and the duty to preserve cultural identity under CYFSA principles (s.1).
caseRef: Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017, S.O. 2017, c. 14, Sched. 1, s. 79
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Child Protection | Customary Care for Indigenous Children — CYFSA s.79

text:
A family law lawyer is acting for a client in a custody dispute. During a consultation, the client discloses information suggesting that the client's former partner is physically abusing the parties' 6-year-old child. The lawyer suspects the information constitutes reasonable grounds to believe the child is in need of protection. The lawyer is uncertain whether solicitor-client privilege overrides the mandatory reporting obligation under CYFSA s.125.

Does solicitor-client privilege override a lawyer's duty to report child protection concerns under CYFSA s.125?
opts: Yes — solicitor-client privilege is absolute and overrides all reporting obligations, including mandatory child protection reporting.

No — under CYFSA s.125(9), the duty to report applies to every person, including lawyers; solicitor-client privilege does not exempt a lawyer from the mandatory reporting duty; the duty to report child protection concerns is one of the recognized exceptions to solicitor-client privilege in Ontario.
Yes — solicitor-client privilege means a lawyer cannot disclose client communications under any circumstances, including child protection reporting.
A lawyer may choose whether to report based on their professional judgment; the duty is discretionary for lawyers but mandatory for other professionals.
expl: CYFSA s.125(9) provides that the duty to report under s.125 applies 'despite anything in the Regulated Health Professions Act, 1991 or in any other Act.' Courts and the Law Society of Ontario have confirmed that solicitor-client privilege does not exempt lawyers from the mandatory duty to report under CYFSA s.125. This is one of the recognized exceptions to solicitor-client privilege — where reporting is necessary to prevent serious harm to a child and is mandated by statute, the privilege must yield. The Rules of Professional Conduct (r. 3.3-3 and related commentary) recognize this exception. A lawyer who possesses reasonable grounds to suspect that a child is or may be in need of protection must report forthwith to CAS, regardless of the privilege.
caseRef: Child, Youth and Family Services Act, 2017, S.O. 2017, c. 14, Sched. 1, s. 125(9)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Child Protection | Duty to Report — Lawyers and Solicitor-Client Privilege

text:
Helen signed a marriage contract 12 years ago, before her marriage. She now claims that her husband failed to disclose significant assets before signing. She has known about the non-disclosure for approximately 5 years but only now seeks to set aside the contract. Her husband argues the claim to set aside the contract is statute-barred.

Is Helen's application to set aside the marriage contract under FLA s.56(4)(a) subject to a limitation period, and if so, has it expired?
opts: There is no limitation period for setting aside a domestic contract; such applications may be brought at any time.

The application to set aside a domestic contract under FLA s.56(4)(a) is subject to the general two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002, running from the date of discovery of the non-disclosure; if Helen has known of the non-disclosure for approximately 5 years, her claim may be statute-barred.
The limitation period for setting aside a domestic contract is six years from the date the contract was signed.
The limitation period is 10 years from the date of the marriage.
expl: Applications to set aside domestic contracts under FLA s.56(4) are civil law claims subject to the Limitations Act, 2002. The basic limitation period is two years from the date the claim was discovered (s.4 of the Limitations Act, 2002). The discovery rule means the period runs from the date Helen knew or ought to have known of the non-disclosure and that it would be an appropriate basis for seeking relief. If Helen has known of the non-disclosure for approximately 5 years, the two-year limitation may have expired approximately 3 years ago, potentially making her claim statute-barred. The husband's argument has merit.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 56(4); Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Domestic Contracts | Limitation Period — Setting Aside Domestic Contract FLA s.56(4)

text:
Eleanor and Frank are negotiating their post-separation arrangements. They are currently married but have been separated for six months. During the marriage, they executed a marriage contract that addressed property division. Now separated, Eleanor's lawyer drafts a document labelled 'Marriage Contract — Separation Arrangements' to settle spousal support and the matrimonial home. Frank's lawyer raises a concern about the label. Eleanor argues the label does not matter legally because both types of contracts are domestic contracts under Part IV of the FLA.

What is the legal consequence, if any, of using a marriage contract rather than a separation agreement to settle the terms of an existing separation?
opts: There is no legal consequence; both instruments are domestic contracts under FLA Part IV and are fully interchangeable once properly executed.
A marriage contract is invalid if executed after separation; only a separation agreement has legal force once the parties are living separate and apart.

Under the FLA, a separation agreement under s.54 is the legally correct instrument for parties who are already living separate and apart; a marriage contract under s.52 governs rights during or arising from the subsisting marriage, not post-separation settlement; using the wrong type of contract may create ambiguity about enforceability and may not qualify for the specific statutory protections and limitations applicable to separation agreements under FLA ss.55–56.

A marriage contract may validly address all post-separation matters including spousal support and property; the label is legally irrelevant because the FLA treats all Part IV domestic contracts identically for enforcement purposes.
expl: Family Law Act s.52 defines a marriage contract as an agreement between persons who are married or intend to marry, dealing with rights and obligations during the marriage or on separation, annulment, dissolution, or death. FLA s.54 defines a separation agreement as an agreement entered into by persons who have cohabited and are now living separate and apart, in which they agree on their respective rights and obligations. Although both are Part IV domestic contracts, they are distinct instruments designed for different phases of the relationship. A marriage contract executed post-separation may create ambiguity about whether it properly falls within s.52 — which governs marriage-phase planning — versus the s.54 separation agreement, which triggers specific statutory provisions including FLA s.56(1) (court may override parenting provisions) and s.56(4) (grounds for setting aside). Mislabelling may affect enforcement, create interpretive disputes, and may not engage the intended statutory regime. Best practice is to use a separation agreement (s.54) for post-separation settlements.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 52 (marriage contract), s. 54 (separation agreement), s. 55 (formalities), s. 56 (setting aside)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Domestic Contracts | Marriage Contract vs Separation Agreement — FLA ss.52 and 54

text:
A court order requires Samuel to pay $2,500/month in spousal support. Samuel has paid nothing for 8 months despite having the financial means to do so. His former spouse seeks to bring contempt of court proceedings against him, in addition to FRO enforcement. Samuel argues that contempt proceedings are unavailable because the FRO handles all support enforcement.

Is contempt of court available as an enforcement mechanism for breach of a support order, in addition to FRO enforcement?
opts: Samuel is correct — the FRSAEA creates a complete code for support enforcement; contempt of court is unavailable once FRO enrollment exists.

Contempt of court is available as a remedy for wilful breach of a support order, but requires a court application demonstrating the payor's intentional and contumacious refusal to comply despite having the means to do so; it operates concurrently with and does not exclude FRO enforcement.
Contempt of court applies only to parenting order violations, not support order violations.
Contempt of court for support violations carries only a monetary fine; incarceration is not available.
expl: Contempt of court is an inherent power of the Superior Court of Justice, available for wilful disobedience of any court order, including a spousal support order. It is not displaced or limited by the FRSAEA enforcement regime. Contempt proceedings require the moving party to establish beyond a reasonable doubt that the payor had knowledge of the order, had the ability to comply, and wilfully chose not to comply (contumacious disobedience). Penalties for civil contempt include fines and, ultimately, imprisonment. FRO enforcement and contempt proceedings are complementary remedies that may be pursued concurrently — the FRO handles administrative enforcement while contempt addresses wilful non-compliance as a more serious judicial sanction.
caseRef: Family Law Rules, O. Reg. 114/99, r. 31
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement | Contempt of Court — Support Order Enforcement

text:
Linda has not received child support payments from her former husband for 8 years. She wants to bring an enforcement action for all accumulated arrears. Her husband argues that the two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 bars most of the claims, and that interest does not run on support arrears.

What is the applicable limitation period for enforcement of support arrears in Ontario, and does interest run on arrears?
opts: The two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 applies to each missed support payment; therefore, Linda may only recover arrears from the past two years.

There is no limitation period for the enforcement of support arrears in Ontario; support arrears may be enforced regardless of how old they are, and interest accrues on arrears from the date each payment was due.
Support arrears are extinguished after five years under the Limitations Act, 2002; Linda can only collect arrears from the past five years.
Support arrears may only be collected for the period during which the child was under age 18; arrears relating to payments owed while the child was an adult are unenforceable.
expl: Under the Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, support arrears are not subject to the general limitation periods in the Limitations Act, 2002. Ontario courts have held that claims to enforce support orders (including recovery of arrears) are exempt from the general two-year limitation period because they are ongoing court orders, not new claims. The Limitations Act, 2002 s.16(1)(c) provides that there is no limitation period for an order of the court. Interest accrues on each missed support payment from the date it was due, as each payment becomes a judgment debt once missed. Linda may seek enforcement of all accumulated arrears, subject to the court's discretion to address any laches or delay issues.
caseRef: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 16(1)(c)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement | Arrears — Limitation Period and Interest

text:
An Ontario court made a child support order requiring the father to pay $1,500/month two years ago. The father then relocated to British Columbia, where a BC court subsequently made a separate child support order for the same child at $900/month, at the father's application, based on his lower reported BC income. Both orders are now outstanding. The Ontario FRO is enforcing the Ontario order at $1,500/month; the father argues that the BC order supersedes the Ontario order and he owes only $900/month. The mother has not consented to the BC proceeding.

Under the Interjurisdictional Support Orders Act (ISO Act) framework, how is a conflict between an Ontario support order and a subsequent BC support order for the same child resolved?
opts: The most recent order automatically prevails; the BC order at $900/month supersedes the Ontario order and the father owes only $900/month.
The original Ontario order continues to prevail; a subsequent provincial order made without consent of the recipient cannot override an existing Ontario support order.

Under the ISO Act, a support order made in another province does not automatically supersede an existing Ontario order; for a BC court to validly vary the Ontario order, the variation must be made through the ISO Act's inter-provincial coordination process; a unilateral BC proceeding initiated by the payor without proper ISO Act coordination does not extinguish Ontario's order, and the FRO continues to enforce the Ontario order until a proper variation has been obtained.
Both orders are equally valid and enforceable simultaneously; the father owes $2,400/month — the sum of both orders.
expl: The Interjurisdictional Support Orders Act, 2002 (ISO Act) establishes a framework for inter-provincial support order coordination. An existing Ontario support order does not automatically cease to be enforceable simply because the payor obtained a subsequent order in another province. For a variation of an Ontario support order to be effective, the proper statutory process must be followed: the variation application should be coordinated through the ISO Act process or, if commenced in another province, must be properly transmitted to Ontario and acknowledged. A payor cannot unilaterally reduce their support obligation by obtaining a lower order from a court in another province without complying with the coordination requirements. The Ontario FRO is correct to continue enforcing the Ontario order at $1,500/month until a proper inter-provincial variation through the ISO Act process has replaced it. The BC order obtained unilaterally by the payor without ISO Act coordination does not override the Ontario order.
caseRef: Interjurisdictional Support Orders Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 13; Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement | Inter-Provincial Enforcement — ISO Act Conflict Between Orders

text:
Darius owes $18,000 in child support arrears spanning 14 months. The FRO has suspended his personal driver's licence under FRSAEA s.34. Darius continues to drive a pickup truck registered in the name of Darius Holdings Inc., a corporation of which he is the sole director and shareholder. Darius tells his friend: 'The FRO can't touch the truck — it's the corporation's, not mine.' The FRO's enforcement officer is now considering what further steps are available.

Which statement most accurately describes the FRO's enforcement tools with respect to the vehicle and the corporate registration?
opts: Darius is correct — FRSAEA enforcement reaches only assets in the payor's personal name; assets owned by a corporation, even a solely controlled corporation, are fully exempt from FRO enforcement.
The FRO's only remedy is to suspend Darius's personal driver's licence; vehicle permits cannot be suspended regardless of whose name they are registered in.

Under FRSAEA s.35, the Director may direct suspension of a vehicle permit held by the payor; this power targets vehicle permits in the payor's personal name; for a vehicle registered in a corporation the payor solely controls, the FRSAEA itself does not automatically pierce the corporate veil, but the FRO may apply to court to hold Darius in contempt or seek other remedies; the corporate registration is a General Rule Trap — it reduces but does not eliminate FRO enforcement options.
Because Darius already had his driver's licence suspended under s.34, all further FRO enforcement action is barred until the licence is reinstated.
expl: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act s.35 empowers the Director to direct the Registrar of Motor Vehicles to refuse to validate or to suspend a vehicle permit (licence plate) held by the payor. The direct reach of s.35 is to permits held in the payor's personal name. Where the vehicle is registered in a corporation's name, s.35 does not automatically reach that permit because the corporation is a separate legal entity. However, Darius's assertion that the FRO 'can't touch the truck at all' overstates the protection: (1) the FRO has other enforcement tools beyond vehicle permit suspension, including garnishment, financial examinations, passport suspension under FRSAEA s.37, and reporting to credit bureaus; (2) in egregious cases of corporate-veil abuse, a court may pierce the corporate veil; (3) the Director retains broad enforcement discretion. The corporate registration is a real (not absolute) limitation on the direct application of s.35 — a General Rule Trap for candidates who assume all FRO enforcement tools apply equally regardless of registration name.
caseRef: Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31, s. 34, s. 35, s. 37
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 — Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement | Vehicle Permit Suspension — FRSAEA s.35 and Corporate Registration

text:
Donna and Raymond separated in January 2021. They attempted reconciliation for several months, failing permanently in October 2021. Raymond never commenced divorce proceedings. In March 2026, Donna files an application for equalization of net family property under the Family Law Act. Raymond argues her claim is statute-barred under the FLA's specific limitation period.

What limitation period governs Donna's equalization claim under the Family Law Act, and when does it begin to run?
opts: Donna's equalization claim is subject to the general two-year limitation under the Limitations Act, 2002, running from the date of separation in January 2021; her claim filed in March 2026 is statute-barred.

Under FLA s.7(3), a claim for equalization of net family property must be commenced within two years after the day the marriage is terminated by divorce or judgment of nullity, within two years after the day a separation agreement is entered into, or within six years after the day the spouses separate and there is no reasonable prospect of resumption of cohabitation; Donna's claim must be analyzed under these specific triggering events.
There is no limitation period for equalization claims in Ontario; they may be brought at any time during or after the marriage.
The limitation period for equalization is ten years from separation.
expl: Family Law Act s.7(3) provides three specific limitation periods for equalization claims: (a) two years after the day the marriage is terminated by divorce or judgment of nullity; (b) two years after the day a separation agreement is entered into; or (c) six years after the day the spouses separate in circumstances that appear to be permanent. These are specific limitation periods under the FLA that displace the general Limitations Act, 2002 two-year period. On the facts, if there is no divorce and no separation agreement, Donna must rely on the six-year period from the date of permanent separation (October 2021, when the reconciliation finally failed). If the permanent separation date is October 2021, the six-year period runs to October 2027, meaning Donna's March 2026 application is within time. Raymond's argument based on the January 2021 date may fail if the permanent separation date is found to be October 2021.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 7(3)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law | Equalization Claim Limitation — FLA s.7(3)

text:
Peter and Joan separated in 2020. Joan remained in the matrimonial home with the children. No exclusive possession order was ever sought. The home is registered in both names. Peter recently announced he plans to sell the home and wants Joan to vacate. It is now 2026. Joan consults a lawyer about whether she can now apply for exclusive possession.

Is Joan's potential application for exclusive possession of the matrimonial home subject to a limitation period, and what law governs?
opts: There is no limitation period for exclusive possession applications; Joan may apply at any time regardless of how long the parties have been separated.

The general two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 applies to exclusive possession applications, running from the date Joan knew or ought to have known her right to possession was threatened; a court would assess when the claim was discovered.
The limitation period for exclusive possession is six years from the date of separation under FLA s.7(3).
Exclusive possession orders are not available more than one year after separation.
expl: Unlike equalization claims, which have specific limitation periods under FLA s.7(3), there is no equivalent specific limitation period in the FLA for exclusive possession applications. Courts have applied the general Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic limitation period to exclusive possession claims. The two-year period runs from the date the claim was discovered — that is, when Joan knew or ought to have known that: (a) she had a right to apply for exclusive possession; and (b) a proceeding was an appropriate means to enforce that right. The discovery date may be the date of separation, or it may be later — such as when Peter first threatened to sell the home or demanded Joan vacate. Joan should seek advice urgently as the limitation period may apply.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 19; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law | Limitation Period — Exclusive Possession Applications

text:
David and Isabella began cohabiting in a conjugal relationship in January 2021. Their son Lucas was born in March 2022. In August 2023 — after 2 years and 7 months of cohabitation — David moves out and the relationship ends. Isabella seeks spousal support. David argues she does not qualify as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 because they cohabited for less than three years and therefore she has no support entitlement. Isabella argues she qualifies regardless of the cohabitation period.

Does Isabella qualify as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 entitled to seek spousal support despite the cohabitation period being under three years?
opts: No — FLA s.29 requires continuous cohabitation for at least three years; at 2 years and 7 months, Isabella falls short and does not qualify regardless of any other circumstances.
No — FLA s.29 requires five years of cohabitation for an unmarried couple; 2 years and 7 months is far below the threshold.

Yes — FLA s.29 defines 'spouse' for Part III support purposes as a person who has cohabited in a conjugal relationship continuously for at least three years OR who is in a relationship of some permanence and is the parent of a child born of the relationship; because Isabella and David are the parents of Lucas, Isabella qualifies under the parenthood arm of the disjunctive definition even though cohabitation was under three years.
Yes — any cohabitation in a conjugal relationship, regardless of duration or children, qualifies under FLA s.29.
expl: Family Law Act s.29 defines 'spouse' for the purpose of Part III (support obligations) as a person who has cohabited with another person continuously for a period of not less than three years, OR who is in a relationship of some permanence if they are the natural or adoptive parents of a child. This is a disjunctive definition: either arm independently establishes qualification. Isabella and David cohabited for only 2 years and 7 months — below the three-year threshold under the first arm. However, they are the natural parents of Lucas, born during the relationship — satisfying the second arm. Isabella therefore qualifies as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 through the parenthood arm, despite falling short of three years' cohabitation. David's argument focuses only on the first arm and ignores the parenthood disjunct — a common exam trap.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | FLA s.29 — Definition of Spouse for Support — Disjunctive Test

text:
Angela and Robert cohabited for 18 years. Throughout the relationship, they each worked full-time, pooled all income into a joint account, made joint decisions about property purchases, cared for their two children together, and treated their financial lives as fully merged. Robert's investments grew significantly. On separation, Angela claims a proportionate share of all accumulated wealth.

Under Kerr v. Baranow, which of the four joint family venture factors are most clearly present on these facts, and what remedy does a finding of joint family venture support?
opts: None of the four joint family venture factors are present because Angela and Robert both worked; the JFV doctrine only applies where one party worked exclusively in the home.

Under Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the four JFV factors are: (1) mutual effort; (2) economic integration; (3) actual intent; and (4) priority of the family; all four appear strongly present on these facts (pooled income, joint decisions, children, mutual full commitment); a finding of JFV supports a proportionate share remedy rather than a quantum meruit calculation.
The joint family venture doctrine entitles Angela to exactly 50% of all assets regardless of her actual contributions.
Joint family venture applies only where both parties have signed a formal partnership agreement.
expl: In Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the Supreme Court identified four factors indicating a joint family venture: (1) mutual effort — working together toward common goals; (2) economic integration — pooling resources and finances; (3) actual intent — the parties' own understanding of their relationship as a joint enterprise; and (4) priority of the family — decisions made in the interests of the family unit. On the facts, all four factors are strongly present: 18 years of cohabitation, pooled income, joint property decisions, raising children together, and fully merged financial lives. A finding of JFV supports a proportionate share remedy for the unjust enrichment claim — Angela would receive a share proportional to her actual contribution to the jointly acquired wealth, which may be close to 50% given the full economic integration.
caseRef: Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | Joint Family Venture — Four Kerr v. Baranow Factors

text:
During their common-law relationship, Craig transferred $80,000 from his personal savings account to an investment account he registered solely in his partner Leigh's name, without any written explanation. Three years later, they separate. Craig claims the money was always his and that Leigh holds it on a resulting trust for him. Leigh argues it was a gift to her.

What is the presumption of resulting trust and how does it apply to Craig's $80,000 transfer to Leigh?
opts: In a common-law relationship, any transfer of money between partners is presumed to be a gift; Craig bears the burden of proving otherwise.

Under the law of resulting trusts, where one person gratuitously transfers property to another person with whom they are not married, there is a presumption of resulting trust — the recipient holds the property on trust for the transferor; Craig does not need to prove the money was a loan; Leigh bears the burden of rebutting the presumption by proving Craig intended a gift.
The presumption of resulting trust applies only to transfers of real property, not to money transfers.
The presumption of resulting trust applies only between spouses, not between common-law partners.
expl: The presumption of resulting trust arises at common law when a person gratuitously transfers property to another person who is not their spouse. In such cases, the law presumes the recipient holds the property on a resulting trust for the transferor — the transferor's intent is presumed to be to retain beneficial ownership. The recipient bears the burden of rebutting this presumption by showing the transferor intended an outright gift. Note that for married spouses, the presumption of advancement applies in the opposite direction (a gratuitous transfer between spouses is presumed a gift). Craig and Leigh are not married, so the resulting trust presumption applies to the $80,000 transfer; Leigh must prove Craig intended a gift to rebut it.
caseRef: Pecore v. Pecore, [2007] 1 SCR 795; Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | Presumption of Resulting Trust — Gratuitous Transfers

text:
After a 14-year common-law relationship, Nadia and Vincent separate. During the relationship, Nadia quit her job to look after their three children and Vincent's parents while Vincent built his contracting business from scratch. The home is registered in Vincent's name. Nadia claims unjust enrichment. Vincent argues he paid Nadia a weekly allowance of $200 for her household work, constituting a juristic reason for retaining the benefit.

Under the unjust enrichment doctrine as articulated in Kerr v. Baranow, does the payment of a modest allowance constitute a juristic reason negating Nadia's claim?
opts: Any payment to the claimant, however modest, constitutes a complete juristic reason for the defendant's retention of the benefit.

Under Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the juristic reasons category includes: existing contracts, disposition of law, donative intent, and other policy-based reasons; a nominal $200/week payment may not fully reflect the value of Nadia's contributions and would not constitute a complete juristic reason if the payment was grossly disproportionate to the benefit conferred; the court assesses the reasonableness of the juristic reason and the degree to which it corresponds to the enrichment.
The juristic reason analysis is not applicable to common-law relationships; unjust enrichment is assessed purely on the value of the claimant's labour.
A modest payment always provides a complete juristic reason because parties are free to contract on any terms they choose.
expl: In Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the Supreme Court confirmed that the absence of a juristic reason is an essential element of unjust enrichment. Juristic reasons include: (1) a contract; (2) a disposition of law; (3) donative intent (the claimant intended to gift the benefit); and (4) other policy reasons. A nominal payment of $200/week as an 'allowance' for extensive child-rearing and household management over 14 years may not constitute an adequate juristic reason if it is grossly disproportionate to the value of the services rendered. The court would assess whether the allowance corresponds meaningfully to the enrichment conferred. Where a payment captures only a fraction of the economic value of the contributions, it does not negate the unjust enrichment claim in full — the claim may succeed for the excess enrichment beyond the payment.
caseRef: Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269; Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights | Unjust Enrichment — Juristic Reason Analysis

text:
Ontario enacts the Safe Communities Act, which prohibits the possession of replica firearms in any public place and imposes a $5,000 fine plus a 30-day licence suspension for the first offence. Parliament has enacted the Firearms Act, which creates a criminal offence for possessing a restricted firearm without a licence and imposes a two-year mandatory minimum sentence. A defendant is charged under both statutes after being found with a realistic-looking replica handgun on a city street. His counsel argues that the Ontario law is constitutionally invalid because replica firearm regulation is a matter of criminal law under s. 91(27) and the province cannot duplicate a federal criminal prohibition. The Crown argues both laws validly operate simultaneously.

The defendant's counsel argues that the Ontario Safe Communities Act trenches on the federal criminal law power under s. 91(27) and cannot operate alongside the federal Firearms Act. Applying the double aspect doctrine and pith and substance analysis, which of the following best describes the constitutional result?
opts: The Ontario Act is unconstitutional because any provincial law that prohibits the same conduct as a federal criminal offence is invalid under the paramountcy doctrine, regardless of the provincial purpose.
Both laws are unconstitutional because double-aspect regulation of the same conduct by both levels of government violates the exclusivity principle underlying ss. 91 and 92.

The Ontario Act is constitutionally valid if its pith and substance is directed at a distinct provincial purpose — such as public safety and community peace — rather than punishment of criminal wrongdoing, even though both laws target the same conduct; the double aspect doctrine permits coexistence of two constitutionally valid laws, with paramountcy resolving any direct operational conflict.
Provincial laws on public safety matters are automatically valid under s. 92(16) (matters of a merely local or private nature) without any need to satisfy pith and substance analysis.
expl: The double aspect doctrine, confirmed in Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22, recognizes that a single subject matter may have both a federal and a provincial dimension and may be validly regulated by both levels of government simultaneously, provided each law is valid in pith and substance under its respective head of power. The pith and substance of the Ontario Safe Communities Act — directed at maintaining public safety and civic order in public spaces through administrative penalties and licence suspensions — is properly characterized under s. 92(15) (imposition of punishment for provincial offences) and s. 92(16) (local matters). The pith and substance of the federal Firearms Act — punishing dangerous possession through criminal sanctions — is properly characterized under s. 91(27). Because each law serves a distinct constitutional purpose, both are valid and may coexist. If an individual cannot comply with both laws simultaneously (operational conflict), paramountcy renders the provincial law inoperative to the extent of the inconsistency, but it does not invalidate the provincial law entirely.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, ss. 91, 92; Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Double Aspect Doctrine — Same Matter, Dual Validity

text:
First National Bank of Canada, a federally chartered bank, operates a branch in Ontario. Ontario attempts to apply a provincial consumer protection statute requiring all lenders to disclose certain information and to comply with a maximum interest disclosure standard. The bank argues that as a federal undertaking under s. 91(15) (banking), it is immune from provincial consumer protection legislation under the doctrine of interjurisdictional immunity.

Under the doctrine of interjurisdictional immunity as restated in Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, does a provincial consumer protection statute apply to a federally chartered bank?
opts: No. All provincial laws are automatically inapplicable to federally chartered banks because banking is an enumerated federal head of power under s. 91(15).

Yes. Under Canadian Western Bank, interjurisdictional immunity requires impairment of the vital or essential core of banking; general consumer protection disclosure requirements do not impair that core and may validly apply to federally chartered banks.
No. Provincial consumer protection laws are always inapplicable to banks because they regulate the terms of banking contracts, which is the exclusive domain of federal banking regulation.
Yes, but only if the provincial law has been specifically authorized by the federal Bank Act.
expl: In Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22, the Supreme Court of Canada rejected the broad application of interjurisdictional immunity and held that the doctrine should be applied with restraint, reserved for impairment of the vital and essential core of a federal undertaking. Provincial consumer protection laws that impose general disclosure obligations do not impair the essential banking functions of a federally chartered bank; they regulate the bank's conduct in the consumer marketplace in a manner similar to how any commercial lender would be regulated. Such laws are not inapplicable under IJI. If a conflict arises with specific federal banking regulation, the paramountcy doctrine would resolve it.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91(15) (banking), s. 92(13); Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Interjurisdictional Immunity — Banks and Indian Reserves

text:
Parliament enacts the Safe Food Packaging Act. The statute requires all manufacturers and importers of consumer food packaging to meet federally prescribed chemical-safety standards, prohibits the sale of non-compliant packaging, and makes violation a federal offence carrying fines and imprisonment. The statute's preamble states the purpose is 'to protect the health and safety of Canadians from toxic substances in food-contact materials.' Ontario challenges the Act, arguing its pith and substance is regulation of 'property and civil rights in the province' under s. 92(13) because it governs commercial sale of manufactured goods — a matter of contracts and commerce within Ontario. The federal government argues the dominant purpose is criminal law under s. 91(27) — prohibiting harmful conduct with a penal sanction — or alternatively general regulation of trade and commerce under s. 91(2).

Applying the pith and substance analysis, under which head of federal power is this statute most accurately characterized, and why?
opts: Section 92(13) — property and civil rights — because the statute primarily regulates the manufacture and sale of commercial products, which is a matter of commercial contract and property law within the province.

Section 91(2) — regulation of trade and commerce — because any federal statute that affects the flow of goods between provinces automatically falls under the general trade and commerce power regardless of its stated purpose.

Section 91(27) — criminal law — because the statute's dominant purpose is to protect public health by prohibiting the use of toxic substances in food packaging, backed by penal sanctions. Prohibitions supported by penalties aimed at genuine public safety evils fall within the criminal law power, even where they incidentally affect commercial activity.

The statute cannot be characterized under a single head of power; it must be split by reading down the criminal sanctions from the regulatory standards, each assigned separately to s. 91(27) and s. 92(13) respectively.
expl: Pith and substance analysis identifies the dominant purpose and legal effect of the legislation; incidental effects on other heads of power do not change its constitutional character. Here, the statute's dominant purpose — as revealed by its preamble, structure, and operative provisions — is protecting Canadians from toxic substances in food packaging through prohibition backed by criminal penalties. This is a paradigm use of the criminal law power under s. 91(27): a prohibition directed at a genuine public safety evil (toxic contamination of food), enforced by penal sanctions. The incidental effect on commercial activity (regulating packaging manufacturers and importers) does not change the dominant character of the law. Option A incorrectly treats the commercial effect as the dominant purpose; effects on trade and commerce are incidental. Option B incorrectly treats any goods-flow effect as automatically engaging s. 91(2); the general trade and commerce branch requires either interprovincial trade regulation or a general regulatory scheme affecting all of Canada's trade that provinces cannot enact severally — that analysis is not met by a product safety prohibition. Option D (reading down or 'severance') applies only after characterization when a law contains multiple separable provisions of different constitutional character; it is not a substitute for characterization itself.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, ss. 91(2), 91(27), 92(13); Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22; R v Hydro-Quebec, [1997] 3 SCR 213
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Pith and Substance Analysis

text:
Ontario enacts the Environmental Protection and Responsibility Act, which imposes civil liability on manufacturers of toxic chemicals for environmental damage caused by their products. The dominant purpose of the legislation is to create a compensation scheme for environmental harm caused by the chemical industry in Ontario. The legislation has the incidental effect of raising the cost of manufacturing chemicals in Ontario, which may affect interprovincial trade. A chemical manufacturer argues the law is really trade regulation within exclusive federal jurisdiction.

Under the pith and substance doctrine, when do incidental effects of provincial legislation on a federal head of power render the provincial law unconstitutional?
opts: Any incidental effect of provincial legislation on a matter within federal jurisdiction automatically renders the provincial law unconstitutional.
Incidental effects on federal matters render the provincial law unconstitutional only if the federal government objects in writing.

Under the pith and substance doctrine, incidental effects of a provincial law on matters within federal jurisdiction do not render the law unconstitutional, provided the dominant purpose and legal effect of the law fall within a valid head of provincial power; a law is characterized by its pith and substance, not by its incidental effects.
A provincial law with any effect on interprovincial trade is automatically within federal jurisdiction under the trade and commerce power in s. 91(2).
expl: The pith and substance doctrine holds that legislation is characterized by its dominant purpose and legal effect (its 'matter'), not by its incidental effects on other matters. A valid provincial law may incidentally affect matters within federal jurisdiction without becoming unconstitutional on that basis alone, as long as its dominant purpose is within provincial jurisdiction. Here, a provincial compensation scheme for environmental harm is properly characterized as a matter of property and civil rights (s. 92(13)) and environmental protection, which are provincial. The incidental effect of raising production costs, while touching on interprovincial trade, does not change the pith and substance of the legislation.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91, s. 92; Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22; Reference re Firearms Act, [2000] 1 SCR 783
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Pith and Substance Analysis — Incidental Effects

text:
Parliament enacts legislation prohibiting certain pesticides that scientific evidence indicates may cause harm to human health. The legislation creates criminal offences and monetary penalties for manufacturers, distributors, and retailers who produce or sell the prohibited pesticides. Alberta argues the legislation is really environmental regulation within provincial jurisdiction and that Parliament cannot use the criminal law power as a pretext to regulate industries that fall under provincial jurisdiction.

Under s. 91(27) of the Constitution Act, 1867, what are the constitutional requirements for a valid exercise of the federal criminal law power?
opts: Federal criminal law must target conduct that was already criminal at common law; Parliament cannot criminalize new conduct using s. 91(27).

Federal criminal law under s. 91(27) requires: (1) a prohibition; (2) a penalty; and (3) a criminal law purpose — typically protection of a public interest such as public safety, public health, public morality, or security. The breadth of the criminal law power is wide, and courts do not require Parliament to prove the prohibited conduct is immoral.
Section 91(27) authorizes Parliament to regulate any industry if it attaches a criminal penalty to the regulatory scheme; the penalty alone satisfies the criminal law purpose requirement.
Parliament may only use s. 91(27) to legislate on matters exclusively federal in nature; the criminal law power cannot be used for matters that also fall within provincial jurisdiction.
expl: The three-part test for valid federal criminal law was established in Reference re Firearms Act, [2000] 1 SCR 783, and confirmed in R v Morgentaler, [1993] 3 SCR 463. The legislation must: (1) contain a prohibition; (2) backed by a penalty; and (3) be directed at a criminal law purpose such as public safety, public health, public morality, public order, or security. Courts give Parliament wide latitude under s. 91(27); protection of public health — including from harmful pesticides — is a recognized criminal law purpose. The law is not invalidated simply because it also incidentally affects provincially regulated industries, provided the dominant purpose is genuinely criminal.
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, s. 91(27); Reference re Firearms Act, [2000] 1 SCR 783; R v Morgentaler, [1993] 3 SCR 463
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Section 91(27) — Breadth of Federal Criminal Law Power

text:
Ontario enacts the Consumer Insurance Contracts Act, which requires all auto insurance policies issued in Ontario to include mandatory uninsured motorist coverage and prohibits insurers from excluding coverage for accidents involving impaired drivers. Intact Insurance, a federally incorporated company under the Insurance Companies Act, challenges the legislation. Intact argues that: (i) as a federally incorporated insurer, it is immune from provincial regulation under interjurisdictional immunity (IJI); and (ii) even if IJI does not apply, the Ontario law is in pith and substance regulation of a federally incorporated company and conflicts with the federal Insurance Companies Act, triggering paramountcy. Ontario argues the Act is a valid exercise of s. 92(13) over the terms of contracts in the province.

Which constitutional analysis most accurately describes whether the Ontario Consumer Insurance Contracts Act can validly apply to a federally incorporated insurance company doing business in Ontario?
opts: The Ontario Act is invalid because insurance regulation exclusively falls under federal s. 91(2) trade and commerce power; provinces have no jurisdiction to impose mandatory coverage terms on insurance companies, whether federally or provincially incorporated.

The Ontario Act is constitutionally valid under s. 92(13) because it regulates the terms of insurance contracts in the province — a core area of property and civil rights — and IJI does not apply unless the provincial law impairs the vital and essential core of the federal incorporation scheme, not merely the business conduct of the insurer; paramountcy applies only where compliance with both laws is impossible or where the provincial law frustrates federal purpose.

As a federally incorporated company, Intact is entirely immune from provincial legislation under IJI; Ontario legislation cannot apply to any of its business operations regardless of the provincial purpose.

The Ontario Act would be valid only if it mirrors the mandatory coverage provisions already required under the federal Insurance Companies Act; a provincial law imposing different or additional coverage terms is automatically in operational conflict with federal law.
expl: Section 92(13) grants provinces exclusive jurisdiction over 'property and civil rights in the province,' which the courts have interpreted broadly to include the regulation of contracts, including insurance contracts. Provincial legislation mandating coverage terms and prohibiting exclusions in policies issued in the province is directed at the contractual relationship between insurer and insured — a matter of civil rights in the province — not at the corporate structure or solvency of the insurer. IJI (interjurisdictional immunity) requires impairment of the vital and essential core of the federal subject matter, not merely incidental effects on a federally regulated entity's commercial operations. Federal regulation of insurance companies under the Insurance Companies Act primarily addresses solvency, capitalization, and corporate governance — not the specific terms of individual consumer contracts. Paramountcy arises only where compliance with both statutes is impossible or where the provincial law frustrates a federal purpose; Ontario requiring mandatory coverage does not conflict with federal solvency requirements. The Ontario Act therefore validly applies to Intact under s. 92(13).
caseRef: Constitution Act, 1867, ss. 91(2), 92(13); Canadian Western Bank v Alberta, 2007 SCC 22; Insurance Companies Act, RSC 1991, c. 47
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 1 — Division of Powers | Section 92(13) — Property and Civil Rights in the Province

text:
Police officers lawfully detain Marcus at a transit station under the provincial Safe Streets Act after observing him loitering in a restricted area. The detention is entirely lawful. An officer says to Marcus: 'You're being detained under the Safe Streets Act because we need to speak with you about the area you're in.' Marcus asks, 'Am I being detained because I did something wrong or because of a complaint?' The officer responds: 'I can't get into all the details right now — just come with us and we'll explain more at the station.' Marcus is taken to a nearby office and held for 45 minutes. He later argues that his s. 10(a) rights were violated because the officer's explanation was partial and ambiguous and did not allow him to understand the true reason for detention or exercise his right to counsel meaningfully.

Applying s. 10(a) of the Charter and the standard established in R v Evans, was Marcus's s. 10(a) right satisfied by the officer's partial explanation at the time of detention?
opts: Yes. Section 10(a) is satisfied as long as police identify the statutory authority under which they are acting; the officer cited the Safe Streets Act, which is sufficient compliance regardless of whether the full factual basis was disclosed.

Yes. Because the detention was lawful, s. 10(a) cannot be breached; the constitutionality of the underlying authority for detention is what determines compliance, not the completeness of the reasons given.

No. Under s. 10(a) and R v Evans, police must promptly provide reasons that are sufficiently clear and complete that the detained person can understand why they are being deprived of liberty and make a meaningful choice about whether to retain and instruct counsel; a partial or ambiguous explanation that defers full disclosure to a later time fails this standard even if the detention itself is lawful.
No. Section 10(a) requires police to provide full written reasons at the moment of detention; an oral explanation, however complete, does not satisfy the Charter obligation.
expl: Section 10(a) of the Charter guarantees that everyone detained has the right to be informed promptly of the reasons for the arrest or detention. Under R v Evans, [1991] 1 SCR 869, this obligation requires meaningful communication of the grounds — not a formal reading of charges or a written document — but sufficient to enable the person to understand why they are deprived of their liberty and to make an informed decision about exercising the right to retain counsel under s. 10(b). The lawfulness of the detention does not determine compliance with s. 10(a); the two are independent inquiries. Where police acknowledge further reasons exist but defer disclosure to 'the station,' the detained person cannot meaningfully exercise their s. 10(b) right to counsel because they do not know what they are actually accused of. This partial explanation breaches s. 10(a).
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, ss. 10(a), 10(b); R v Evans, [1991] 1 SCR 869
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 10(a) — Right to Be Informed of Reasons for Arrest or Detention

text:
Parliament enacts the Drug Possession Accountability Act, imposing a mandatory minimum sentence of three years' imprisonment for possession of a controlled substance in a Schedule I category, regardless of quantity or purpose. The Crown charges Farouk, a 22-year-old first-time offender, with possessing 0.4 grams of cocaine for personal use. The trial judge acknowledges that an appropriate sentence for Farouk's specific circumstances would be a conditional sentence of six months. Farouk argues the mandatory minimum is grossly disproportionate when applied to his facts and also when applied to a reasonable hypothetical first-time offender at the low end of the offending spectrum — for example, a 19-year-old student found with a trace amount for one-time personal use. The Crown argues the mandatory minimum must be assessed only against Farouk's actual conduct, not against a hypothetical offender.

Applying s. 12 of the Charter and the reasonable hypothetical approach established in R v Nur, which analysis most accurately describes whether the mandatory minimum in the Drug Possession Accountability Act constitutes cruel and unusual punishment?
opts: The court must assess s. 12 solely against Farouk's actual conduct; if the three-year sentence is not grossly disproportionate for Farouk specifically, the mandatory minimum is constitutional and the court cannot consider other hypothetical offenders.

The mandatory minimum is automatically unconstitutional because Parliament cannot impose any mandatory minimum sentence on a first-time offender for possession of a personal-use quantity; the Charter requires judicial discretion in all sentencing decisions.

Under s. 12 and R v Nur, 2015 SCC 15, gross disproportionality is assessed against both the specific offender and a reasonable hypothetical offender within the law's scope; where the mandatory minimum would impose a grossly disproportionate sentence on a reasonable hypothetical offender at the low end of the offending spectrum — such as a first-time possessor of a trace quantity — the provision is unconstitutional even if arguably proportionate in the case before the court.

A mandatory minimum sentence satisfies s. 12 provided Parliament considered proportionality during the legislative process; judicial review of parliamentary sentencing policy is not available under the Charter.
expl: Under s. 12 of the Charter and as confirmed in R v Nur, 2015 SCC 15, and R v Lloyd, 2016 SCC 13, a mandatory minimum sentence constitutes cruel and unusual punishment if it is grossly disproportionate — so excessive as to outrage standards of decency — when assessed against either the specific offender before the court or a reasonable hypothetical offender within the law's realistic scope of application. The reasonable hypothetical approach requires the court to consider whether the mandatory minimum would impose a grossly disproportionate sentence on a person at the low end of the offending spectrum covered by the provision. Here, a 19-year-old student with a trace quantity of cocaine for personal use is a foreseeable application of the Drug Possession Accountability Act. A three-year mandatory minimum for that hypothetical offender would be grossly disproportionate. The provision therefore violates s. 12 regardless of whether the sentence imposed on Farouk specifically might be arguable in isolation.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 12; R v Nur, 2015 SCC 15; R v Lloyd, 2016 SCC 13
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 12 — Cruel and Unusual Treatment or Punishment

text:
A province enacts the Public Safety Merit Act, which creates a separate civil service track for physically demanding emergency services roles. The Act sets out physical fitness standards and requires candidates to meet minimum lifting and endurance benchmarks. On average, 72% of male applicants meet the benchmarks versus 38% of female applicants. Sophia, a female applicant who failed the lifting benchmark by a narrow margin, argues that the Act violates s. 15 (equality) because it has a disproportionate adverse impact on women based on sex. The government responds that the Act is a facially neutral merit-based measure directed at occupational safety and does not distinguish between men and women on its face. Sophia further argues that s. 28, which guarantees Charter rights equally to male and female persons, independently requires that the s. 15 equality analysis be applied with heightened attention to how the facially neutral standard operates as a sex-based distinction in practice.

Applying s. 28 of the Charter as an interpretive provision, how does it bear on Sophia's s. 15 challenge to the facially neutral physical fitness standard?
opts: Section 28 creates a freestanding constitutional right to equal treatment for women that is independent of s. 15; Sophia succeeds on a s. 28 claim even if she cannot establish an adverse effects discrimination claim under s. 15.

Section 28 operates as an interpretive guarantee that existing Charter rights — including the s. 15 equality right — are applied equally and fully to male and female persons; in Sophia's case, s. 28 requires the court to apply s. 15 with sensitivity to how facially neutral standards that produce a disproportionate sex-based impact engage the substantive equality guarantee, without s. 28 itself overriding the s. 15 analysis or creating new remedies.

Section 28 has no application to facially neutral physical fitness standards because those standards do not distinguish between men and women on their face; s. 28 is triggered only by laws that expressly name sex as a criterion.
Section 28 applies only at the s. 1 justification stage; it cannot be used at the threshold s. 15 analysis to establish a prima facie breach.
expl: Section 28 of the Charter provides that the rights and freedoms referred to in the Charter are guaranteed equally to male and female persons. Section 28 operates as an interpretive guarantee: it directs courts to apply Charter rights — including the s. 15 equality right — in a manner that gives full and equal effect to those rights for both men and women. Section 28 does not create freestanding rights independent of the enumerated Charter provisions, and it does not generate separate remedies. In Sophia's case, s. 28 reinforces the requirement that the s. 15 adverse effects analysis be applied with sensitivity to facially neutral standards that disproportionately disadvantage women on account of their sex — a recognized form of adverse effects discrimination under s. 15. The section 15 analysis (whether the law creates a distinction, whether it is based on an enumerated or analogous ground, and whether it substantively impairs the claimant's dignity) remains the operative analytical framework; s. 28 shapes how that framework is applied.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, ss. 15, 28
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 28 — Charter Rights Guaranteed Equally to Male and Female Persons

text:
A provincial legislature enacts the Public Spaces Integrity Act. The Act's preamble declares that it operates notwithstanding s. 2 (fundamental freedoms) and ss. 7 to 15 of the Charter. The Act requires all persons to remove face coverings while using provincially operated public transit. A transit rider named Amara wears a face covering for sincere religious reasons. She argues: (1) the declaration cannot override her right to vote and participate in elections under s. 3 of the Charter; (2) the declaration cannot override her right as a Canadian citizen to move freely within Canada under s. 6; and (3) even though s. 15 (equality) is within the clause's scope, s. 28 independently guarantees that all Charter rights are guaranteed equally to male and female persons and a s. 33 override does not displace that guarantee as it applies to a sex-based religious distinction. The government argues the declaration is valid and covers all rights Amara raises.

Which of Amara's arguments correctly identifies a right that survives the province's s. 33 notwithstanding clause declaration?
opts: All three of Amara's arguments fail. A valid s. 33 declaration covering ss. 2 and 7–15 of the Charter overrides all Charter rights of the persons affected by the legislation, including ss. 3, 6, and any interpretive effects of s. 28.

Amara's arguments regarding ss. 3 and 6 both succeed. Section 33 cannot override democratic rights (s. 3) or mobility rights (s. 6) because those provisions are outside the permissible scope of a notwithstanding clause declaration; a declaration purporting to override ss. 3 or 6 has no constitutional effect on those rights.

Only Amara's argument about s. 6 succeeds. The s. 33 declaration validly overrides s. 3 because democratic rights can be suspended by the government in the public interest, but s. 6 mobility rights can never be overridden by any legislative mechanism.

Only Amara's argument about s. 28 succeeds. Because s. 28 is an interpretive guarantee for sex equality, it independently protects Amara's right to wear a face covering as a gendered religious practice, and s. 33 has no effect on interpretive provisions of the Charter.
expl: Section 33 of the Charter expressly limits the notwithstanding clause to ss. 2 and 7–15 only. Rights outside this range cannot be overridden by any s. 33 declaration, regardless of its wording. Democratic rights (ss. 3, 4, 5), mobility rights (s. 6), official language rights (ss. 16–23), minority language education rights (s. 23), and the equality guarantee in s. 28 are all beyond the reach of s. 33. A declaration that purports to override s. 3 or s. 6 is constitutionally inoperative with respect to those rights. Amara retains the full protection of ss. 3 and 6 despite the declaration. Regarding s. 28: s. 28 is an interpretive guarantee that Charter rights apply equally to men and women; it does not create a free-standing override on s. 33 and does not independently protect rights beyond what the underlying enumerated right provides. Because s. 15 can be overridden by s. 33, the interpretive lens of s. 28 applied to s. 15 is also affected. Amara's s. 28 argument therefore does not succeed as an independent basis to survive the override. Her arguments on ss. 3 and 6 succeed; her s. 28 argument does not.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, ss. 3, 6, 15, 28, 33
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 33 — Notwithstanding Clause — Scope and Use

text:
Police officers on foot patrol stop a young Black man walking in a high-crime neighbourhood late at night. The officers question him, demand his identification, and ask him to empty his pockets. The officers do not tell him he is free to leave. The man complies and contraband is found. He argues the police interaction constituted a psychological detention without reasonable cause, violating s. 9 of the Charter.

Under R v Grant, when does a police-citizen interaction constitute a psychological detention within the meaning of s. 9 of the Charter?
opts: A detention under s. 9 requires physical restraint; a verbal interaction that does not involve physical contact can never constitute a detention.
Any police questioning of a citizen automatically constitutes a detention that must be based on reasonable grounds.

Under R v Grant, a psychological detention occurs when a reasonable person in the circumstances would conclude that they were not free to walk away; relevant factors include the nature of the police conduct, the setting, and whether the person was singled out in circumstances that would lead them to feel compelled to comply.
A psychological detention is only established where the police officer explicitly says the person is not free to leave.
expl: In R v Grant, 2009 SCC 32, the Supreme Court of Canada established a contextual test for psychological detention under s. 9. A person is psychologically detained — even without physical restraint — when a reasonable person in the particular circumstances would conclude they were not free to go. The court considers the nature of the police conduct (tone, form of demands vs. requests), the setting, and whether the encounter is targeted at that individual in a way that would lead a reasonable person to feel compelled to comply. On these facts, the combination of demands, the absence of any indication the person could leave, and the targeted nature of the stop may constitute a psychological detention.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 9; R v Grant, 2009 SCC 32
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 9 — Psychological Detention and the Grant Test

text:
The Ontario Clean Energy Appeals Tribunal is a statutory tribunal created under the Clean Energy Act. Section 12 of that Act states: 'The Tribunal may, in writing, authorize a panel of one or more members to exercise the Tribunal's powers under this Act in respect of matters the Tribunal designates as streamlined proceedings.' In practice, the Tribunal designates all applications involving renewable energy projects under 500 kW as streamlined proceedings and has authorized panels of one member to conduct these hearings and issue decisions. The Tribunal Chair then goes a step further: without any further authorization in the enabling statute, the Chair issues a directive authorizing a senior legal officer (a non-member staff lawyer) to issue final written decisions in streamlined proceedings where no party requests an oral hearing, on the basis that the legal officer was present at all preliminary conferences and is familiar with the record. A developer whose application was refused by the legal officer argues the decision is ultra vires.

Applying the principle of delegatus non potest delegare, which of the following best describes the constitutional validity of the legal officer's decision in the streamlined proceeding?
opts: The decision is valid. Section 12 of the Clean Energy Act authorizes the Tribunal to designate streamlined proceedings and authorize panels; the Chair's directive expanding this to a legal officer is a reasonable administrative extension of that existing authorization.

The decision is invalid. The maxim delegatus non potest delegare prohibits any further delegation of a statutory power of decision beyond what the enabling statute expressly authorizes; the Clean Energy Act authorizes panels of members only, not non-member staff officers, and the Chair's directive cannot expand the Tribunal's subdelegation authority beyond the statutory limit.

The decision is valid because the legal officer is a qualified lawyer who participated in all preliminary conferences; where the subdelegatee has the same or greater expertise as the authorized decision-maker, administrative efficiency justifies the subdelegation.
The decision is invalid because s. 12 of the Clean Energy Act is itself unconstitutional; enabling statutes cannot grant subdelegation authority to tribunals.
expl: The maxim delegatus non potest delegare (a delegate cannot further delegate) applies with full force in Canadian administrative law. Where an enabling statute authorizes a specific form of subdelegation — here, authorization of panels of members — that authorization defines the outer limit of permissible subdelegation. The Tribunal received delegated authority from the legislature; s. 12 then expressly authorized the Tribunal to subdelegate to panels of one or more members. This partial statutory authorization does not confer a general power to subdelegate further to non-members. The Chair's directive expanding subdelegation to a staff legal officer falls outside the authorization granted by s. 12; the officer is not a member and the statute does not authorize delegation to non-members. The decision issued by the legal officer is therefore ultra vires and invalid, regardless of the officer's expertise or the administrative benefits of the arrangement.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22; delegatus non potest delegare principle; Interpretation Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. I.11
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | Delegation and Subdelegation — Delegatus Non Potest Delegare

text:
An Ontario securities regulatory body has a structure in which its staff conduct investigations and make prosecution recommendations to a panel of commissioners. The same commissioners who receive the staff's recommendation also sit as adjudicators at the hearing on the merits. A respondent argues that the institution suffers from structural bias because the adjudicators who will decide the case have been exposed to the staff's investigative conclusions before the hearing.

Under Canadian administrative law, when does overlap between investigative and adjudicative functions within the same institution give rise to a reasonable apprehension of institutional (structural) bias?
opts: Any overlap between investigative and adjudicative functions within the same institution automatically gives rise to a reasonable apprehension of bias.
Institutional bias arises only where the same individual personally conducted the investigation and also sits as adjudicator; there is no concern where different individuals handle each function.

Under IWA v Consolidated-Bathurst Packaging Ltd, [1990] 1 SCR 282, and the reasonable apprehension of bias test from Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369, a reasonable apprehension of institutional bias arises where the institutional structure is such that the adjudicators have been exposed to one-sided, partisan information about the case before the hearing — as where the adjudicating panel receives and considers the staff's investigative recommendations. The test remains whether a reasonable and informed person, knowing all the circumstances, would apprehend a risk of unfairness.
Institutional bias is not a recognized ground of challenge in Canadian administrative law; bias challenges must be directed at individual adjudicators, not the institution.
expl: Under Canadian administrative law, the reasonable apprehension of bias test from Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369, applies to both individual and institutional bias.
