Where the structure of a regulatory body is such that the adjudicators who will decide a case have been exposed to the investigating staff's one-sided conclusions before the hearing, a reasonable and informed person may apprehend that the adjudicators have been infected by the investigative perspective, impairing their ability to approach the hearing with an open mind. Courts apply a contextual analysis considering the degree of overlap and how the pre-hearing exposure occurred, rather than applying an automatic rule.
caseRef: Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369; IWA v Consolidated-Bathurst Packaging Ltd, [1990] 1 SCR 282
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | Institutional Bias — Overlapping Investigative and Adjudicative Functions

text:
A licensed nurse practitioner has for 15 years renewed her practice permit annually without any hearing or conditions. The nursing regulatory college has never required a hearing for straightforward renewals and has a published policy stating that renewal applications 'will be approved unless there are identified conduct concerns.' The college now imposes new mandatory conditions on the nurse's permit renewal without providing any hearing or opportunity to respond to concerns. The nurse argues she had a legitimate expectation of renewal without conditions.

Under the Baker framework and Canadian administrative law, what does the doctrine of legitimate expectations entitle a claimant to when a public authority departs from a past practice or representation?
opts: Legitimate expectations entitles the claimant to both the promised procedure and the substantive outcome — the authority must grant the same outcome it previously provided.

In Canadian administrative law, the doctrine of legitimate expectations is limited to procedural entitlements; it does not require the authority to grant the same substantive outcome but does require the authority to follow promised or previously observed procedures — here, providing an opportunity to be heard — before departing from past practice.
Legitimate expectations has no application where the public authority publishes a policy; the existence of a written policy extinguishes any legitimate expectation arising from past conduct.
Legitimate expectations applies only where the claimant received an individualized written guarantee from a senior official; a general published policy cannot give rise to legitimate expectations.
expl: Under Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817, the Supreme Court of Canada confirmed that in Canadian administrative law the doctrine of legitimate expectations generates procedural rights only — it does not compel a particular substantive outcome. Where a public authority has, through its conduct or representations, led a party to expect that a particular process will be followed, the party is entitled to that process before the authority departs from the expected course. The published policy stating renewals 'will be approved unless there are conduct concerns,' combined with 15 years of practice, creates a legitimate expectation of a hearing or an opportunity to respond before conditions are imposed; it does not, however, guarantee renewal without conditions.
caseRef: Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Reference re Canada Assistance Plan (BC), [1991] 2 SCR 525
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | Legitimate Expectations — Procedural vs Substantive in Canadian Law

text:
A federal Employment Insurance Commission panel denies a claimant's appeal for extended benefits after a hearing. The Commission is not governed by the Ontario SPPA (being a federal administrative body). Its enabling statute states that the panel 'shall provide a notice of decision to the parties' but is silent on whether reasons must accompany the notice. The claimant receives a one-line notice stating 'Appeal dismissed — insufficient qualifying hours established.' No reasons are given for how the panel assessed conflicting employment records submitted by the claimant. The claimant argues the common law duty of procedural fairness requires the panel to provide reasons. The Commission argues three things: (i) the SPPA does not apply to this federal body, so no statutory reasons obligation exists; (ii) Baker established only a contextual duty, and this decision does not engage the most serious Baker factors; and (iii) even if Baker requires reasons, a one-line notice satisfies whatever minimal obligation applies.

Applying the Baker v Canada framework, which of the following most accurately describes whether the Commission's one-line notice satisfies the procedural fairness obligation to provide reasons?
opts: The notice is sufficient because the SPPA does not apply to this federal body; absent a statutory obligation, a notice of decision in any form — including a one-line dismissal — satisfies all procedural fairness requirements.

Baker established an absolute duty to provide full written reasons for every administrative decision that affects individual interests; the one-line notice automatically breaches that duty regardless of which Baker factors apply.

Under Baker v Canada, the duty to give reasons is part of the content of the common law duty of procedural fairness, determined contextually by weighing the five Baker factors — including the significance of the decision to the individual, the nature of the process, and whether the decision resembles adjudication; where those factors support a duty to give reasons, a bare one-line dismissal of an appeal involving conflicting evidence does not satisfy that duty — even in the absence of a statutory requirement under the SPPA.

Baker requires reasons only where the SPPA specifically applies; for federal bodies not subject to the SPPA, the common law duty of procedural fairness includes no reasons obligation regardless of the seriousness of the decision.
expl: In Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817, the Supreme Court confirmed that the duty to provide reasons is part of the contextual content of the common law duty of procedural fairness. It is not an absolute universal duty (option B is wrong) and it does not depend on the SPPA applying (options A and D are wrong). The duty is determined by weighing the Baker factors: (1) the nature of the decision — here, an adjudicative appeal hearing involving conflicting evidence; (2) the nature of the statutory scheme — the Commission adjudicates individual claims, suggesting higher fairness content; (3) the importance of the decision to the individual — denial of employment benefits is significant; (4) the legitimate expectations of the parties — parties who present conflicting evidence expect to understand how it was resolved; (5) the choices of the decision-maker — the panel held a hearing, elevating the fairness expectation. Together, these factors strongly support a duty to give reasons. A one-line dismissal that does not address conflicting evidence does not meet the substantive requirement of reasons — even if it technically notifies the claimant of the result. Reasons must be sufficient to allow the party to understand why they lost and to assess whether grounds for further review exist.
caseRef: Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 17
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | Duty to Give Reasons — Common Law Obligation Outside SPPA

text:
A party before an Ontario professional licensing tribunal loses at first instance. The enabling statute provides a statutory right of appeal to the Divisional Court on any question of law or fact. Instead of filing an appeal, the party immediately applies for judicial review in the Divisional Court, arguing the tribunal's decision was unreasonable. The tribunal argues judicial review should be dismissed because the statutory appeal is an adequate alternative remedy.

Under Ontario law, when does the existence of a statutory right of appeal bar judicial review?
opts: A statutory right of appeal never bars judicial review; parties always have a choice between judicial review and statutory appeal.
A statutory right of appeal always bars judicial review; the applicant must pursue all statutory appeals before judicial review is available.

Under Ontario administrative law, where a statute provides an adequate alternative remedy — such as a statutory right of appeal — courts generally decline to exercise their discretion to grant judicial review, unless special circumstances exist, such as the tribunal lacking jurisdiction, the appeal mechanism being inadequate or inaccessible, or there being a constitutional question not addressed by the appeal.
Judicial review is barred only where the statutory appeal was actually filed and abandoned; failing to file an appeal at all does not engage the alternative remedy doctrine.
expl: Under the Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2, and consistent with the common law, courts retain a discretion to decline to grant judicial review where an adequate alternative remedy exists, including a statutory right of appeal. The existence of a right of appeal to the Divisional Court on any question of law or fact is a strong reason to decline judicial review in favour of the statutory appeal mechanism, which is designed to address the exact issues raised. Courts will nonetheless grant judicial review in special circumstances — such as where the appeal mechanism is inadequate (e.g., limited to questions of law only), where there is a jurisdictional issue, or where the statutory appeal cannot address a Charter or constitutional issue. Here, the statutory appeal covers all questions of law and fact, making it an adequate alternative remedy.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2; Canada (Border Services Agency) v CB Powell Limited, 2010 FCA 61
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Adequate Alternative Remedy — Bar to Judicial Review

text:
A licensed contractor receives a written decision on June 1, 2025 from the Ontario Building Materials Evaluation Commission (OBMEC) revoking his registration. The OBMEC is established under the Building Materials Evaluation Act, which provides in s. 47: 'A person aggrieved by a decision of the Commission may apply for judicial review within 60 days after the decision is communicated to the applicant.' The contractor takes no step until August 15, 2025 — 75 days after the decision — when he files a judicial review application in the Divisional Court. He relies on s. 47 of the enabling statute, arguing the 60-day period has not yet expired because he did not receive the decision letter until June 16, 2025. The Commission argues that the JRPA's 30-day limit (s. 5) began to run on June 1 and the application is out of time regardless of s. 47.

Which limitation period controls the contractor's judicial review application, and has the application been filed in time?
opts: The JRPA's 30-day limit controls exclusively; the enabling statute cannot modify the JRPA time limit. The application, filed 75 days after the decision, is out of time unless the court extends the period.

The enabling statute's 60-day limitation period controls over the JRPA's 30-day default, because a specific statutory limitation period in the tribunal's enabling legislation displaces the general JRPA time limit. Whether the application is in time depends on when the decision was 'communicated' to the contractor — if June 16 is accepted, the August 15 filing falls within 60 days and is timely.

Both time limits apply concurrently; the contractor must satisfy whichever is shorter. The application was filed 75 days after the decision date and is out of time under the 30-day JRPA limit, and the enabling statute provides no relief.

Neither time limit applies because the JRPA and enabling statutes cannot restrict the inherent jurisdiction of the Divisional Court to hear judicial review applications; no limitation period is mandatory.
expl: Where an enabling statute expressly prescribes a specific limitation period for judicial review of a particular tribunal's decisions, that specific provision governs and displaces the JRPA's general 30-day default under s. 5. The JRPA sets a default that applies in the absence of a more specific statutory provision; it is not a mandatory floor that overrides specific limitations in enabling legislation. Here, s. 47 of the Building Materials Evaluation Act expressly provides a 60-day period running from when the decision is 'communicated' to the applicant — not from the decision date. If the contractor can establish that communication occurred on June 16, 2025, the 60-day period expires August 15, 2025, and the August 15 filing is timely. The burden of establishing the communication date falls on the contractor. The Commission's reliance on the JRPA 30-day period is misplaced because the specific statutory provision displaces the JRPA general default.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 5; Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s. 6(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Delay in Bringing Judicial Review — Time Limits

text:
Yusuf, a refugee claimant, challenges his removal order through judicial review in the Federal Court, arguing the Refugee Protection Division misapplied the definition of 'particular social group' in assessing his persecution claim. While the application is pending, Yusuf voluntarily returns to his country of origin to care for a terminally ill parent. The government moves to dismiss the application as moot: the removal order cannot be enforced against Yusuf while he is abroad, and he has no immediate prospect of returning to Canada. Yusuf's counsel argues the court should nonetheless exercise its stage-two Borowski discretion to proceed because: (i) the 'particular social group' issue recurs in dozens of pending RPD decisions; (ii) the parties have fully briefed the issue and remain adversarially engaged through counsel; and (iii) the Federal Court has not definitively resolved the interpretation question, causing inconsistent RPD decisions. The government counters that Yusuf's voluntary departure destroyed the adversarial relationship and that deciding a moot case on a legal interpretation issue would put the court in an advisory role beyond its proper institutional function.

Applying the Borowski two-stage mootness framework to the stage-two discretionary analysis, which of the following best describes how the court should weigh the competing considerations?
opts: The court must dismiss the application. Once a claimant voluntarily departs Canada, the adversarial relationship is destroyed as a matter of law and the court has no discretion to proceed; Borowski stage two does not apply after voluntary departure.

The court must proceed. Where a legal issue recurs in multiple pending proceedings and the parties have fully briefed the matter, systemic importance is a sufficient basis to decide a moot case; Borowski stage two is satisfied by recurring systemic importance alone.

Under Borowski v Canada (AG), [1989] 1 SCR 342, the stage-two discretion requires the court to weigh three factors holistically: (i) whether the parties retain an adversarial relationship sufficient to sharpen the issues; (ii) whether judicial economy justifies a decision on a recurring systemic issue to prevent future litigation on the same point; and (iii) whether deciding the question would place the court in an advisory or legislative role that exceeds its proper institutional function. The court may proceed if the balance of these factors favours a decision — even if the adversarial relationship has weakened — particularly where the issue recurs, the briefing is complete, and guidance would prevent inconsistent lower-body rulings.

Borowski stage two requires the court to first obtain a bilateral agreement from both parties confirming they wish the court to decide the moot question; absent such agreement, the court cannot exercise its discretion to proceed.
expl: In Borowski v Canada (Attorney General), [1989] 1 SCR 342, the Supreme Court established a two-stage mootness test. Stage one determines whether the live controversy has disappeared. Yusuf's voluntary departure renders the immediate controversy moot — the removal order is unenforceable while he is abroad. Stage two asks whether the court should nonetheless decide the case, requiring holistic weighing of three factors: (a) adversarial relationship — the parties remain engaged through counsel and have fully briefed the legal issue, maintaining a functional adversarial context even if Yusuf's personal stake has diminished; (b) judicial economy — the 'particular social group' interpretation recurs in dozens of pending RPD proceedings; deciding the question prevents future litigation and corrects inconsistent tribunal rulings — a strong reason in favour of proceeding; (c) institutional role — the question is a genuine legal issue requiring interpretation of an existing statute, not a request for an advisory opinion on a hypothetical; the court would be clarifying law applied in pending cases, not departing from its adjudicative function. On balance, these factors may support proceeding, though the court retains full discretion.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1; Borowski v Canada (Attorney General), [1989] 1 SCR 342
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Mootness on Judicial Review — Borowski Test

text:
Leila applies for judicial review of a decision by the Ontario Workplace Safety and Insurance Appeals Tribunal (WSIAT) denying her workers' compensation claim. The certified tribunal record consists of 400 pages of medical evidence, two expert reports, and transcripts of the three-day hearing before the WSIAT panel. Leila's counsel argues three grounds: (1) the tribunal's decision was unreasonable because it ignored key medical evidence in the record; (2) there was a reasonable apprehension of bias because, through an access to information request, Leila obtained emails showing that a WSIAT vice-chair exchanged ex parte communications with the employer's insurer before the hearing — these emails were not part of the tribunal record; and (3) the court should conduct a de novo review of all the medical evidence because the statutory right to compensation is so fundamental that the reviewing court must make its own fresh findings of fact rather than defer to the tribunal. Leila asks the court to admit the emails and to conduct a full de novo review on the merits.

Which of the following best describes the reviewing court's authority to (a) admit the emails and (b) conduct a de novo review of the medical evidence?
opts: The court may admit the emails and conduct a de novo review of the medical evidence; judicial review of a statutory right to compensation is a matter of such legal importance that the court must make its own findings on all questions of fact and law.

The court may admit the emails because they go to a reasonable apprehension of bias — a ground of procedural unfairness that cannot be established from the tribunal record alone — but may not conduct a de novo review of the medical evidence; judicial review is not a de novo proceeding, and the court reviews the tribunal's factual findings on a reasonableness standard, confined to what was before the decision-maker.
The court may not admit the emails or any other post-decision evidence; the certified tribunal record is an absolute limit on the material the reviewing court may consider, with no exceptions.

The court may admit the emails and conduct a de novo review because the existence of a statutory right to compensation means the reviewing court must independently determine the correct outcome; Vavilov provides that statutory rights are reviewed on a correctness standard with de novo fact-finding.
expl: On judicial review under the Vavilov framework (Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65), the reviewing court is generally confined to the certified tribunal record — the material that was before the decision-maker. This reflects the supervisory nature of judicial review: the court examines the decision-maker's process and reasoning based on what was before them. Judicial review is not a de novo proceeding; the court does not conduct fresh fact-finding on the merits or substitute its view of the evidence. However, courts have recognized narrow exceptions permitting additional evidence to be admitted where the additional evidence is necessary to establish a ground of review that cannot be demonstrated from the record itself. A reasonable apprehension of bias arising from ex parte communications that occurred outside the hearing — and therefore could not appear in the tribunal record — falls squarely within this exception. The emails are admissible for the limited purpose of establishing the bias ground. De novo review of the medical evidence is not authorized: reasonableness review under Vavilov does not transform into de novo fact-finding simply because the right at stake is statutory or important.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Certified Tribunal Record — Scope of Review

text:
A court on judicial review finds that a federal immigration tribunal's decision to refuse a refugee claim was unreasonable — the tribunal's reasoning was internally incoherent and failed to address key evidence. The applicant asks the court to substitute a decision granting refugee protection, arguing that only one outcome is possible on the evidence. The government argues the court should remit the matter to a differently constituted tribunal.

What is the ordinary remedy when a reviewing court finds that an administrative decision is unreasonable under the Vavilov framework, and when, if ever, may a court substitute its own decision?
opts: When a decision is found unreasonable, the court must always substitute its own correct decision without remitting to the tribunal.
When a decision is found unreasonable, the court must always remit to the tribunal; it can never substitute its own decision.

Under Vavilov, when a court finds a decision unreasonable, the ordinary remedy is remittal — sending the matter back to the decision-maker for redetermination — because the decision-maker, not the court, has the statutory authority to make the decision on the merits. The court may substitute its own decision only in the rare case where the circumstances make it unnecessary or unfair to remit, such as where only one outcome is legally possible.
The court always has a choice between remittal and substitution; the choice is made based on whichever is more convenient.
expl: Under Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, when a reviewing court finds a decision unreasonable, the ordinary remedy is to remit the matter to the decision-maker for redetermination, with or without directions. This reflects the constitutional principle that it is the statutory decision-maker — not the court — that has the authority to make the decision. The court may, however, substitute its own decision in exceptional circumstances where there is only one possible outcome, remittal would serve no useful purpose, or fairness to the parties demands finality. Mere inefficiency or delay in the tribunal system is not a sufficient reason to substitute judgment.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | JR Remedies — Remittal vs Substitution of Decision

text:
CleanWaters Alliance is a national environmental advocacy organization with 40,000 members across Canada. The organization has dedicated fifteen years to researching and advocating on freshwater contamination. It seeks judicial review of a federal Cabinet Order approving a pulp mill discharge permit that the Alliance argues violates the Canadian Environmental Assessment Act. The Alliance has no direct economic interest in the permit. No individual with direct personal standing has come forward to challenge the decision; the mill operators have confirmed they will begin operations in 30 days, making this the only realistic opportunity to bring the issue before a court. The government argues the Alliance lacks standing. The Alliance argues: (1) there is a serious justiciable issue regarding federal environmental assessment obligations; (2) it has a genuine and ongoing interest in freshwater contamination; and (3) no other reasonable party is available or willing to bring the issue to court. The government counters that the Alliance fails the second part of the test because its interest is professional advocacy, not genuine stake, and that failing the second part means standing must be denied.

Applying the Downtown Eastside public interest standing test, which argument most accurately describes the Alliance's standing position?
opts: The Alliance lacks standing because a professional advocacy organization's interest in a legal issue is insufficient to satisfy the second part of the Downtown Eastside test; genuine stake requires a personal connection to the decision's legal consequences, which a professional advocacy group cannot have.

The Alliance automatically has standing because it has no conflict of interest, has relevant expertise, and the issue affects its membership; public interest organizations with these attributes always satisfy the Downtown Eastside test.

Under Canada (AG) v Downtown Eastside Sex Workers United Against Violence Society, 2012 SCC 45, the three-part standing test is applied holistically and contextually, not as a series of strict threshold gates; the Alliance satisfies all three parts — a serious justiciable issue exists, its long-term advocacy on freshwater issues gives it a genuine stake, and the absence of any willing party with direct standing makes the proceeding a reasonable and effective means of bringing the issue to court.

The Alliance fails the standing test because it did not first exhaust internal administrative review processes before applying for judicial review; public interest standing is not available where an alternative remedy has not been pursued.
expl: In Canada (Attorney General) v Downtown Eastside Sex Workers United Against Violence Society, 2012 SCC 45, the Supreme Court of Canada reformulated the three-part public interest standing test to be applied holistically, not as strict sequential threshold gates where failure on one part automatically defeats standing. The three factors are: (1) a serious justiciable issue; (2) the applicant has a genuine stake or real interest in the issue; and (3) the proposed proceeding is a reasonable and effective means of bringing the issue to court. The Alliance satisfies all three. The serious justiciable issue is the federal environmental assessment obligation — a genuine legal question. The Alliance's fifteen-year active engagement in research and advocacy on freshwater contamination, combined with its 40,000 member base personally affected by water quality, establishes a genuine stake that goes beyond professional advocacy in the abstract. The third factor is met because no willing party with direct personal standing has come forward, making the Alliance's application the only realistic means of bringing this legal question before the court.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1; Canadian Council of Churches v Canada (Minister of Employment and Immigration), [1992] 1 SCR 236; Canada (Attorney General) v Downtown Eastside Sex Workers United Against Violence Society, 2012 SCC 45
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Judicial Review | Public Interest Standing on Judicial Review

text:
A homeowner files a complaint with the Ontario Tarion Warranty Corporation alleging defective construction by a registered builder. Tarion's enabling statute, the Ontario New Home Warranties Plan Act, provides in s. 14(2) that where Tarion receives a complaint, it 'may, at its discretion, convene a hearing to review the complaint.' Tarion resolves the homeowner's complaint through an internal conciliation process without convening any formal hearing. The homeowner argues the SPPA applied to the conciliation process and that Tarion violated the Act by not providing him with notice and opportunity to make submissions as required by the SPPA. Tarion argues the SPPA did not apply because it was not required to hold a hearing.

Did the SPPA apply to Tarion's conciliation process?
opts: Yes. The SPPA applies to any proceeding in which a tribunal exercises a statutory power of decision; because Tarion's decision to resolve the complaint affected the homeowner's legal rights, the SPPA applied regardless of whether a hearing was formally held.
Yes. The SPPA applies whenever a complaint is filed with a statutory body; the homeowner's filing of a complaint automatically triggered the SPPA's procedural requirements.

No. Under s. 3(1) of the SPPA, the Act applies only where the tribunal is required by or under the enabling statute or otherwise by law to hold or afford an opportunity for a hearing. Because s. 14(2) of the enabling statute provides that Tarion 'may' convene a hearing — a discretionary not mandatory provision — Tarion was not required to hold a hearing, and the SPPA was not triggered.

No. The SPPA does not apply to private warranty corporations such as Tarion because they are not government tribunals; the SPPA only governs decisions made by ministries and provincial government departments.
expl: The pivotal requirement for SPPA application under s. 3(1) is that the tribunal must be 'required by or under' the enabling statute 'or otherwise by law' to hold or to afford the parties an opportunity for a hearing. Where the enabling statute uses 'may' rather than 'shall' for hearing convocation, the tribunal retains discretion over whether to hold a hearing. A discretionary ('may') hearing power does not satisfy the 'required to hold' threshold in s. 3(1). Tarion's enabling statute gives it discretion to convene a hearing; it does not require one. Consequently, the SPPA was not triggered and Tarion's informal conciliation process — while subject to common law fairness standards — was not bound by the SPPA's specific procedural requirements. This 'may vs shall' distinction is the central application threshold in SPPA jurisprudence.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 1 (definitions), s. 3(1) (application); Ontario New Home Warranties Plan Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. O.31
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Statutory Powers Procedure Act | SPPA Application — When Does the SPPA Apply

text:
At a hearing before an Ontario professional discipline tribunal, the regulator seeks to introduce communications obtained by its compliance officers during a covert inspection that was conducted without notice or consent. The respondent argues the communications were obtained through an improper inspection process and that the evidence should be excluded. The tribunal chair states that under s. 15 of the SPPA, all relevant evidence is admissible and the tribunal has no power to exclude it.

Does s. 15 of the SPPA require a tribunal to admit all relevant evidence regardless of how it was obtained, or does the tribunal retain discretion to exclude evidence in appropriate circumstances?
opts: Yes. Section 15(1) of the SPPA requires admission of all relevant evidence; tribunals have no discretion to exclude evidence regardless of how it was obtained.

No. Under s. 15(2) of the SPPA, a tribunal may exclude evidence where its admission would be unduly prejudicial or where its exclusion is required by law, including considerations of fairness; the SPPA does not mandate admission of all evidence in all circumstances.
Yes, but only privileged communications — such as solicitor-client communications — may be excluded by the tribunal; all other relevant evidence must be admitted.
No. The tribunal may exclude any evidence it finds unpersuasive without giving reasons; reliability is the only factor.
expl: Under the Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 15(1), a tribunal may receive as evidence any oral testimony and any document or thing relevant to the subject matter of the proceeding, whether or not it would be admissible in a court of law. However, s. 15(2) recognizes that tribunals may be required by law to exclude evidence — for example, where the exclusion is required to protect solicitor-client privilege, informer privilege, or other legally protected categories. Additionally, tribunals retain common law discretion under the duty of fairness to consider whether admitting improperly obtained evidence would make the proceeding fundamentally unfair. The chair's absolute statement that 'all relevant evidence is admissible' overstates the SPPA's reach.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 15(1), s. 15(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Statutory Powers Procedure Act | Exclusion of Evidence — SPPA Section 15(2)

text:
An Ontario professional regulatory tribunal issues a final written decision on April 1 suspending a dentist's licence for three months. On April 5, the tribunal's chair notices a clerical error in the written decision: the effective date of the suspension was incorrectly stated as 'April 30' rather than 'May 1' as was intended and orally pronounced. The tribunal reconvenes on its own initiative and issues a corrected decision. The dentist argues the tribunal was functus officio after its original decision and had no authority to issue any corrected decision.

Under the doctrine of functus officio and SPPA s. 21.2, when may an Ontario tribunal correct or reconsider its own final decision?
opts: A tribunal is absolutely functus officio once it issues a final written decision; it may not change any aspect of the decision under any circumstances.
A tribunal may reconsider any aspect of its decision at any time as long as the proceeding has not been appealed or judicially reviewed.

Under the doctrine of functus officio, a tribunal generally loses jurisdiction over a matter once it has issued a final decision; however, it retains a limited common law authority to correct clerical or accidental errors in a final decision without reconsidering the merits, and s. 21.2 of the SPPA provides a broader statutory basis for reconsideration where the tribunal's rules permit.
A tribunal may never correct a written decision; errors in written decisions can only be corrected by a reviewing court on judicial review.
expl: The doctrine of functus officio holds that once a tribunal has issued a final decision on the merits, it is generally divested of jurisdiction over the matter and cannot reconsider its decision. However, the common law has long recognized a narrow exception for the correction of clerical errors — where the written decision does not accurately record what was decided — without re-opening the merits. Additionally, the Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 21.2, provides a statutory mechanism for reconsideration where the tribunal's rules permit. Correcting a transcription error in the effective date (which does not change the substance of the decision) falls within the clerical error exception and is permissible; it does not re-open the merits of the suspension.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 21.2; Chandler v Alberta Association of Architects, [1989] 2 SCR 848
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Statutory Powers Procedure Act | Reconsideration — SPPA Section 21.2 and Functus Officio

text:
A nurse is the respondent in a professional discipline hearing before the College of Nurses of Ontario, which is governed by the SPPA. The hearing concerns allegations that she provided incompetent care to three patients on a single shift in 2023. The nurse's counsel requests that the tribunal issue a summons under s. 12 of the SPPA to a third-party data management company, requiring it to produce all electronic health records it has archived for any patient of that hospital from 2020 to 2023 — a dataset spanning over 40,000 patient files. The College's counsel objects: the request covers records of patients entirely unrelated to the three patients named in the allegations, and the connection between unrelated historical records and the specific 2023 care incidents has not been explained.

Should the tribunal issue the summons as requested?
opts: Yes. Under s. 12 of the SPPA, a tribunal may summons documents from any person; the tribunal has no authority to limit the scope of a summons request once the power to issue is established.

Yes. The nurse has a right to a full defence, and any hospital records from the relevant period may potentially be relevant; the tribunal should issue the summons and let the data management company object if it wishes.

No. While the tribunal has the power under s. 12 to issue summonses for documents it considers relevant to the subject matter of the proceeding, the request as framed seeks records of 40,000 patients with no demonstrated connection to the three named patients or the specific incidents under review. The tribunal should decline to issue a summons in that form; the nurse may apply for a narrower summons directed at records of the three named patients.
No. The tribunal has no power to issue summonses to third-party private companies; the s. 12 summons power applies only to parties to the proceeding.
expl: Section 12(1) of the SPPA gives a tribunal the power to issue a summons requiring any person to produce documents or things 'that the tribunal considers relevant to the subject matter of the proceeding.' The relevance requirement is the governing constraint: the tribunal does not have an unconstrained summons power. The subject matter of the proceeding is the alleged incompetent care of three specific patients in 2023. A request for 40,000 patient files spanning three years and extending to entirely unrelated patients is not limited to documents relevant to that subject matter. Issuing such a sweeping summons would be beyond the proper exercise of the s. 12 power. The tribunal should decline the overbroad request but may issue a targeted summons for records of the three patients at issue. The nurse's right to a full defence does not expand 'relevant to the subject matter' into a general discovery mechanism.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 12(1), s. 13
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Statutory Powers Procedure Act | Summons to Witness — SPPA Section 12

text:
A person submits a FIPPA request to the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services for records relating to a foster care placement decision that affected her family. The records contain personal information about the biological parents of the child placed in care. The Ministry refuses access under s. 21 (personal privacy exemption). The requester argues that disclosure is necessary to allow her to understand the basis for a decision that directly affected her.

Under FIPPA, is s. 21 (personal privacy) a mandatory or discretionary exemption, and does it have any exceptions that might allow disclosure?
opts: Section 21 is a discretionary exemption; the Ministry may choose to disclose personal information in any case where it considers disclosure in the public interest.

Section 21 is a mandatory exemption — the Ministry shall refuse to disclose personal information about a third party; however, FIPPA provides defined exceptions including where disclosure is desirable for the purpose of subjecting the activities of the Government to public scrutiny, or where the individual to whom the information relates has consented.
Section 21 is a mandatory exemption with no exceptions; personal information about a third party can never be disclosed under FIPPA.
Section 21 is not an exemption at all; it simply requires the Ministry to notify the third party before making a disclosure decision.
expl: Under the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 21(1), an institution shall refuse to disclose personal information to any person other than the individual to whom the information relates — making it a mandatory exemption (using 'shall'). However, s. 21(1) is subject to a number of defined exceptions, including: where the person to whom the information relates has consented (s. 21(1)(a)); where disclosure is desirable for the purpose of subjecting the activities of the Government to public scrutiny (s. 21(1)(f)(iii)); and other listed exceptions. The institution must consider whether any exception applies before invoking the mandatory exemption.
caseRef: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 21(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information | FIPPA Exemptions — Section 21 Personal Privacy

text:
A journalist submits a FIPPA request to the Ontario Ministry of Infrastructure for all emails and text messages sent on the personal cell phones of three senior Ministry officials relating to a major infrastructure contract. The Ministry responds that personal cell phones are not Ministry property and that personal device communications are outside its custody and control. The journalist argues the communications relate to Ministry business and should be disclosed.

Under FIPPA, when are records held on personal devices of government officials in the custody or control of the institution for the purpose of the right of access?
opts: Records are in the custody or control of the institution only if they are physically stored on government servers or equipment; personal device communications can never be subject to FIPPA access requests.

Under FIPPA, 'custody or control' is determined by examining the nature of the record's content and the purpose for which it was created; records on personal devices that relate to the conduct of government business may be in the control of the institution for FIPPA purposes, even if they are not physically stored on government equipment.
All communications on personal devices by government officials are automatically subject to FIPPA regardless of their content.
FIPPA applies only to records created during working hours on government premises; personal device communications outside office hours are never subject to FIPPA.
expl: Under the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 10, the right of access applies to records in the custody or control of an institution. The Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario has interpreted 'custody or control' broadly to include records in the physical possession of the institution and records over which the institution has the legal right to obtain. Records on personal devices may be in the institution's 'control' if they relate to government business and the institution has the authority and practical ability to obtain them. The test is functional and content-focused, not limited to physical storage on government equipment.
caseRef: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 10; s. 2(1) (definition of record)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information | FIPPA — What Constitutes a Record in the Custody or Control of an Institution

text:
A business association submits a FIPPA access request to the Ministry of Finance for internal economic analysis reports prepared by ministry economists projecting the fiscal impact of a proposed tax reform. The Ministry refuses access, arguing that disclosure of its economic forecasting methodology and projections would harm Ontario's ability to negotiate effectively with the federal government on equalization arrangements. The requester challenges the refusal before the IPC.

Under FIPPA, which exemption permits an institution to refuse disclosure of records where disclosure could reasonably be expected to prejudice the economic interests of Ontario or the competitive position of an Ontario institution?
opts: Section 14 (law enforcement) — it applies to any government record whose disclosure could harm the province.
Section 19 (solicitor-client privilege) — it protects all internal government analysis, including economic forecasts.

Section 18(1) — a discretionary exemption permitting the institution to refuse disclosure of a record that could reasonably be expected to prejudice the economic interests or competitive position of an Ontario institution, or reveal information that could be exploited to the economic disadvantage of Ontario.
Section 21 (personal privacy) — it applies where the record contains information about identifiable individuals, including government economists.
expl: Under the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 18(1), an institution may refuse to disclose a record that contains: (a) trade secrets or financial, commercial, scientific, or technical information belonging to the Government of Ontario; (b) information where disclosure could reasonably be expected to prejudice the economic interests of an institution or the competitive position of an Ontario institution; or (c) information whose disclosure could be exploited to the economic disadvantage of Ontario. Section 18 is a discretionary exemption (the institution 'may' refuse); the institution must exercise its discretion and demonstrate that disclosure would cause the defined economic harm.
caseRef: Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.31, s. 18(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information | FIPPA Section 18 — Economic and Other Interests of Ontario

text:
ShopNation Inc. is a large e-commerce company that processes online retail transactions for approximately 2 million registered customers across Canada. On March 15, a hacker accessed ShopNation's customer database through an unpatched server vulnerability, extracting names, home addresses, credit card numbers, and purchase histories for 50,000 customers. ShopNation's Chief Privacy Officer conducts an internal assessment and determines that the breach creates a 'real risk of significant harm' to the affected individuals, including risks of identity theft and financial fraud. The privacy officer's legal counsel now advises on the company's mandatory obligations under PIPEDA. The company's CEO asks: 'Can we just notify the Privacy Commissioner and let the Commissioner decide whether customers need to be told? That way we avoid the reputational hit of direct customer notification if the Commissioner doesn't think it's warranted.'

The CEO's proposed approach — notifying the Privacy Commissioner only and leaving the decision on individual notification to the Commissioner — is being evaluated. Under PIPEDA, which of the following best describes the company's actual notification obligations?
opts: The CEO's proposed approach is permissible. Where an organization notifies the Privacy Commissioner of Canada as soon as feasible, PIPEDA allows the Commissioner to assess the breach and determine whether individual notification is required; the organization's notification duty to affected individuals is conditional on the Commissioner's direction.

Under PIPEDA ss. 10.1 and 10.2 (as amended by the Digital Privacy Act, 2015), where a breach of security safeguards creates a real risk of significant harm, the organization must independently fulfill two simultaneous notification duties: (1) notify the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada as soon as feasible; and (2) notify affected individuals directly and as soon as feasible — regardless of whether the Commissioner has assessed the breach or directed notification. The organization must also maintain breach records.

The organization must notify only the affected individuals directly; notification to the Privacy Commissioner of Canada is optional at the organization's discretion unless the Commissioner affirmatively requests a report.

The mandatory notification obligation under PIPEDA is triggered only by breaches involving financial information; a breach of home addresses and purchase histories, without confirmed fraudulent use, does not create a 'real risk of significant harm' and does not trigger mandatory notification.
expl: Under the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, S.C. 2000, c. 5, ss. 10.1 and 10.2 (in force since November 1, 2018), organizations are subject to two independent and simultaneous mandatory notification obligations when a breach creates a real risk of significant harm: (1) report to the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada as soon as feasible; and (2) directly notify the affected individuals as soon as feasible. The CEO's proposed approach — notifying only the Commissioner and delegating the individual notification decision to the Commissioner — misunderstands the statutory scheme. PIPEDA imposes both notification duties directly on the organization; neither obligation is conditional on the Commissioner's prior assessment, direction, or approval. The Commissioner's role is oversight and enforcement, not as an intermediary through whom individual notification is channelled. The organization must also maintain records of all breaches of security safeguards, even those that do not meet the 'real risk' threshold, and make those records available to the Commissioner on request.
caseRef: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, S.C. 2000, c. 5, ss. 10.1, 10.2 (as amended by Digital Privacy Act, S.C. 2015, c. 32)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information | PIPEDA — Mandatory Breach Notification Requirements

text:
A bank detects unusual activity in a customer's account that suggests possible fraud. Without the customer's consent, the bank shares the customer's account transaction data with the Royal Canadian Mounted Police as part of a fraud investigation. The customer later learns of the disclosure and argues the bank violated PIPEDA by disclosing his personal information without consent.

Under PIPEDA, does the disclosure of personal information to law enforcement without the individual's consent constitute a violation of PIPEDA's consent principle?
opts: Yes. PIPEDA's consent requirement is absolute; no disclosure of personal information to any third party, including law enforcement, is permitted without the individual's consent.

No. Under PIPEDA s. 7(3), an organization may disclose personal information without consent where the disclosure is to a government institution that has made a request and identified its lawful authority to obtain the information, or where the organization has reasonable grounds to believe the information relates to a breach of law.
Yes. The bank violated PIPEDA because only court-ordered production orders can authorize disclosure to law enforcement without consent.
No. Banks are exempt from all PIPEDA requirements because the Bank Act governs all aspects of banking including privacy.
expl: Under the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, S.C. 2000, c. 5, Schedule 1 establishes the consent principle as foundational to PIPEDA. However, s. 7(3) of PIPEDA provides a number of exceptions to the requirement for consent for disclosure. An organization may disclose personal information without consent to a government institution that has identified its lawful authority to obtain the information and indicated that disclosure is requested for a specified purpose (s. 7(3)(c)). Additionally, s. 7(3)(d) permits disclosure without consent where the organization reasonably believes the information could be useful in investigating a contravention of federal or provincial law. The bank's disclosure to the RCMP in the context of a fraud investigation falls within these exceptions.
caseRef: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, S.C. 2000, c. 5, s. 7(3)(c), s. 7(3)(d)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Privacy and Access to Information | PIPEDA — Exceptions to the Consent Requirement

text:
Ontario Provincial Police officers investigate a suspected fraud. During the investigation, the lead detective fails to follow up on exculpatory evidence, ignores alibi witnesses, and includes demonstrably false statements in the information to obtain a search warrant. As a result, the suspect is wrongly arrested and prosecuted. After two years, all charges are stayed. The suspect sues the police for negligent investigation.

Under Canadian tort law, do police officers investigating a crime owe a duty of care to suspects that may be breached by negligent investigation?
opts: No. Police investigating crimes owe a duty of care only to victims and the public; they can never owe a duty of care to suspects, as recognizing such a duty would impede criminal investigations.

Yes. Under Hill v Hamilton-Wentworth Regional Police Services Board, 2007 SCC 41, police investigators owe a duty of care to suspects; a duty of care exists where the suspect is a specific identifiable individual who was the primary target of the investigation, and where the conduct of the investigation was negligent.
No. The duty of care in negligent investigation can only be established by victims of crime, not by persons who were investigated.
Yes, but only where the police officer is separately convicted of an offence in connection with the investigation; mere negligence does not suffice.
expl: In Hill v Hamilton-Wentworth Regional Police Services Board, 2007 SCC 41, the Supreme Court of Canada recognized a duty of care in the tort of negligent investigation. The Court held that police officers owe a duty of care to suspects who are the primary target of a criminal investigation. Applying the Anns/Cooper test, proximity was established because the investigation was focused on a specific identifiable individual, and the police had direct control over the investigative process affecting that person. A finding of negligent investigation requires that the police conduct fell below the standard of a reasonable police officer in the same circumstances.
caseRef: Hill v Hamilton-Wentworth Regional Police Services Board, 2007 SCC 41; Cooper v Hobart, 2001 SCC 79; Proceedings Against the Crown Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. P.27
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Crown Liability | Negligent Investigation — Duty of Care to Suspect

text:
A Canadian citizen sues a foreign government in an Ontario court for unpaid wages arising from her employment as an administrative assistant at that government's trade office in Toronto. The foreign government argues it is entitled to sovereign immunity from the jurisdiction of Canadian courts and that the claim should be dismissed. The claimant argues that commercial employment activities do not attract sovereign immunity.

Under the State Immunity Act and Canadian international law, when does a foreign state retain sovereign immunity from Canadian court jurisdiction, and when does the commercial activity exception apply?
opts: Foreign states retain absolute immunity from Canadian court jurisdiction in all circumstances; no exception exists for commercial activities.

Under the State Immunity Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. S-18, foreign states are generally immune from Canadian court jurisdiction (acta jure imperii — acts of sovereignty), but the commercial activity exception removes immunity where the claim relates to commercial activities (acta jure gestionis) carried out in Canada by the foreign state.
Foreign states have no immunity from Canadian courts; all civil claims against foreign governments may be brought in Canadian courts.
Sovereign immunity applies only to heads of state personally; foreign governments themselves have never had immunity in Canadian courts.
expl: Under the State Immunity Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. S-18, s. 3, a foreign state is immune from the jurisdiction of any court in Canada. However, s. 5 provides an exception for commercial activity: a foreign state is not immune from the jurisdiction of a court where the proceedings relate to any commercial activity of the foreign state carried on in Canada. The distinction between acta jure imperii (acts of sovereignty, such as military or diplomatic acts) and acta jure gestionis (commercial activities, such as operating a trade office and employing local staff) is central. Employment of local staff in a commercial-like capacity at a trade office has been held to constitute a commercial activity, removing the immunity defence.
caseRef: State Immunity Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. S-18, ss. 3, 5
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Crown Liability | Sovereign Immunity — Acta Jure Imperii vs Acta Jure Gestionis

text:
Kevin is a Ministry of Environment field inspector. He is assigned to drive a government van from the Ministry's district office to an industrial facility 80 km away to conduct a scheduled environmental compliance inspection. Kevin departs at 9:00 a.m. and drives the Ministry van along the designated route. At approximately 9:45 a.m. — while still on the highway en route to the facility and 30 km from his destination — Kevin pulls the van into a strip mall parking lot, leaves the engine running, and spends 40 minutes conducting personal errands: returning library books, collecting a personal parcel, and stopping at a pharmacy. He then re-enters the van and resumes the route. At 11:15 a.m., still driving the government van on the return leg of the route from the completed inspection, Kevin negligently runs a red light while adjusting his personal GPS navigation device and strikes a pedestrian, causing a broken pelvis. The pedestrian sues the Crown in right of Ontario under the Proceedings Against the Crown Act, alleging vicarious liability for Kevin's negligence.

The Crown argues it is not vicariously liable for Kevin's negligence on the return journey. Which of the following scenarios would most effectively support the Crown's defence by establishing that Kevin was outside the course of employment at the time of the tort?
opts: The Crown would defeat vicarious liability by showing that the pedestrian's injuries were caused partly by the pedestrian's own inattentiveness; contributory negligence by the plaintiff is a complete defence to vicarious liability.

The Crown would defeat vicarious liability on the return journey by showing that the negligent act occurred while Kevin was adjusting a personal GPS device; using a personal device while driving a government vehicle during authorized travel hours is a deviation from the Ministry's equipment use policy, which takes the tort outside the course of employment.

The Crown would defeat vicarious liability most effectively by establishing that Kevin's 40-minute detour into the strip mall for personal errands constituted a 'frolic of his own' — a complete departure from his employment duties during which any tort would fall outside the course of employment; however, on the return leg of the authorized inspection route, Kevin had resumed his employment duties, so the earlier frolic does not affect vicarious liability for the return-leg negligence.

The Crown cannot defeat vicarious liability under the Proceedings Against the Crown Act because that statute makes the Crown liable in tort in the same manner as a person of full capacity, and this absolute liability applies regardless of whether the employee departed from authorized duties.
expl: Under the Proceedings Against the Crown Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. P.27, s. 5, the Crown in right of Ontario is subject to all liabilities in tort to which, if it were a person of full age and capacity, it would be subject, including vicarious liability for the torts of its officers and servants in the course of their employment. The key question is whether Kevin was 'in the course of employment' at the time of the tort. The frolic doctrine provides that where an employee makes a complete departure from their employment duties — going off on a 'frolic of their own' — any tort committed during that detour falls outside the course of employment, breaking the vicarious liability chain. Kevin's 40-minute excursion into the strip mall for personal errands (library, parcel, pharmacy) on the outbound journey is a strong candidate for a frolic. However, the negligent act occurred on the return journey, after the inspection was completed and while Kevin was driving the Ministry van back along the authorized route — he had resumed his employment duties. A frolic ends when the employee returns to the scope of their employment. The return-leg conduct is clearly within the course of employment; the earlier detour does not immunize the Crown from vicarious liability for the return-leg tort.
caseRef: Proceedings Against the Crown Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. P.27, s. 5; Negligence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. N.1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Crown Liability | Crown Vicarious Liability — Torts Committed by Crown Employees in the Course of Employment

text:
Amara, an Indigenous woman, applies for a senior project manager position at a large construction company. The hiring panel gives each candidate a composite score based on four criteria: technical qualifications (30%), interview performance (30%), cultural fit as assessed by panelists (20%), and seniority within the company (20%). Amara scores highest on technical qualifications and seniority (she has been with the company 11 years). She scores mid-range on interview performance. However, the panel rates her low on 'cultural fit,' noting she 'seemed uncomfortable with our team culture' and 'might have difficulty integrating with the existing group.' The position is awarded to a less-senior non-Indigenous candidate with lower technical qualifications but higher cultural fit ratings. Amara files a human rights complaint alleging race discrimination in the promotion decision. The employer argues: (i) the technical and seniority factors alone do not establish discrimination because the composite score lawfully weighs multiple criteria; (ii) cultural fit is a legitimate business consideration; and (iii) even if Amara's Indigenous identity was a subconscious factor in the cultural fit assessment, a prima facie case requires showing that race was the dominant or decisive reason for the decision, not merely one among many.

Applying the prima facie discrimination test from Moore v British Columbia (Education), which of the following best describes whether Amara has established a prima facie case?
opts: Amara has not established a prima facie case because the employer applied a composite scoring system with four criteria; where a decision-maker considers multiple factors and the protected characteristic was only one factor, the applicant must prove the protected characteristic was the dominant or decisive reason before the burden shifts.

Amara has not established a prima facie case because 'cultural fit' is a recognized legitimate business criterion; a facially neutral criterion is inherently non-discriminatory and cannot form part of a prima facie case regardless of how it is applied.

Amara has established a prima facie case. Under Moore v British Columbia (Education), 2012 SCC 61, a prima facie case of discrimination requires showing: (1) a characteristic protected by the Code; (2) an adverse impact; and (3) that the protected characteristic was a factor in the adverse treatment — not the dominant or sole factor. Where a facially neutral criterion like 'cultural fit' was assessed in a manner that may reflect racial stereotyping or group assumptions, and the complainant holds a protected characteristic, the connection between the characteristic and the adverse treatment is established sufficiently to shift the evidential burden.

Amara cannot rely on the prima facie discrimination framework at all because the Human Rights Code requires proof of intentional discrimination; where the employer did not deliberately exclude Amara because she is Indigenous, no Code violation can be established.
expl: Under Moore v British Columbia (Education), 2012 SCC 61, and the Ontario Human Rights Tribunal's prima facie discrimination framework, a prima facie case requires proof on a balance of probabilities that: (1) the complainant has a characteristic protected by the Code (Indigenous identity is a protected ground under 'race' and 'ancestry'); (2) the complainant experienced an adverse impact (denial of promotion); and (3) the protected characteristic was a factor in the adverse treatment. The protected characteristic does not need to be the dominant, decisive, or sole reason for the adverse treatment — it is sufficient that it was a contributing factor. The employer's argument that discrimination requires a dominant or decisive causal role misstates the law. Here, the low 'cultural fit' rating — assessed by language suggesting discomfort with group integration that may reflect racial stereotyping or unconscious bias — combined with Amara's objectively stronger performance on quantifiable criteria and her Indigenous identity, provides sufficient basis for a prima facie case. The burden then shifts to the employer to provide a credible, non-discriminatory explanation for the cultural fit ratings and the resulting decision.
caseRef: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 5(1); Moore v British Columbia (Education), 2012 SCC 61
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Human Rights | Prima Facie Discrimination — Onus Shift to Respondent

text:
An employee with a chronic back condition requires a height-adjustable desk and the right to take short standing breaks every 30 minutes. The employer argues that providing an adjustable desk would cost $1,200 and that allowing standing breaks would disrupt production flow. The employer refuses accommodation, stating the cost and disruption constitute undue hardship. The employee files a Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario complaint.

Under the Ontario Human Rights Code, what factors does the Tribunal consider in determining whether an employer has established undue hardship, and does the evidence described likely establish undue hardship?
opts: Undue hardship is established whenever the requested accommodation involves any additional cost to the employer; a $1,200 cost automatically constitutes undue hardship.

Under the Code and the Ontario Human Rights Commission Policy on Disability and the Duty to Accommodate, the three recognized factors for undue hardship are: (1) cost, (2) outside sources of funding, and (3) health and safety. The cost of accommodation is assessed relative to the employer's overall resources; a $1,200 cost for a large employer would very likely not constitute undue hardship. Minor production disruption does not meet the health and safety threshold for undue hardship.
An employer establishes undue hardship by showing that any accommodation would reduce productivity; production disruption is sufficient to establish undue hardship without further analysis.
Undue hardship can be established by proving the accommodation would be inconvenient or would require any modification to existing workplace practices.
expl: Under the Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 17(2), and as articulated in Meiorin (BC (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU, [1999] 3 SCR 3) and the OHRC Policy on Disability, the duty to accommodate requires employers to accommodate to the point of undue hardship. Undue hardship is assessed by reference to three defined factors: (1) cost — whether the financial cost of accommodation would fundamentally alter the nature of the enterprise; (2) outside sources of funding — whether the employer has access to external financial support; and (3) health and safety risks — whether accommodation would create unacceptable safety risks. Mere inconvenience, reduced efficiency, or minor production adjustment do not constitute undue hardship. A $1,200 expenditure, absent evidence it is disproportionate to the employer's resources, would rarely establish undue hardship.
caseRef: Human Rights Code, R.S.O. 1990, c. H.19, s. 17(2); BC (Public Service Employee Relations Commission) v BCGSEU (Meiorin), [1999] 3 SCR 3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 — Human Rights | Undue Hardship — Cost, Health, and Safety Factors
